| 8 | IoT Reference Model | 7-layer model (IWF) | Application, Data, Cloud, etc. |
| 9 | IoT Domain Model | High-level entities: Physical Entity, Virtual Entity, Resource, Service | ITU-T Y.2060 standard |
| 10 | Service-Oriented Arch. | IoT as a set of web services | RESTful APIs, SOAP |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Manufacturing:** Logical blocks map cleanly to ISA-95 levels (sensors at Level 0, applications at Level 4).
- **Healthcare IoT:** Wearable devices (sensing) → BLE (communication) → Smartphone gateway (edge) → Hospital cloud (cloud) → Doctor dashboard (application).
- **Smart Grid:** Smart meters (sensing) → PLC mesh (communication) → SCADA edge (fog) → Utility cloud (cloud) → Billing & analytics app (application).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the same five-block logical design fits *all three* vastly different industries. This is the **power of logical abstraction**—it survives technology changes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Data Flow Derivation: From Sensor to Cloud

The logical design enables a **predictable data flow**. Let us mathematically derive the data transformation that occurs as data moves through each functional block.

### Step 1: Sensing Block – Analog to Digital Conversion

A physical phenomenon (e.g., temperature) is first sensed as an analog voltage. It is then converted to a digital value by an ADC.

$$
T_{digital} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in} \cdot (2^n - 1)}{V_{ref}} \right\rfloor
$$

Where:
- $T_{digital}$ = Digital temperature reading (integer).
- $V_{in}$ = Analog input voltage from sensor.
- $V_{ref}$ = Reference voltage of the ADC (typically **3.3 V** or **5 V**).
- $n$ = Resolution of the ADC (e.g., **10-bit**, **12-bit**, **16-bit**).

**Conversion Logic:** The analog voltage is mapped linearly across $2^n$ discrete digital steps. The floor function ensures we obtain an integer count.

---

### Step 2: Communication Block – Data Packaging & Transmission

The digital value is encapsulated into an application-layer protocol packet. For MQTT, the packet overhead is added:

$$
P_{total} = P_{header} + P_{payload} + P_{footer}
$$

Where:
- $P_{header}$ = MQTT fixed header (typically **2 bytes**).
- $P_{payload}$ = Application data (variable, e.g., **T = 25.4 °C**).
- $P_{footer}$ = Optional, often zero for MQTT.

For HTTP, the overhead is significantly higher (TCP + IP + HTTP headers ≈ **400–800 bytes**), while MQTT is typically **2–10 bytes**—making it ideal for constrained IoT devices.

**Conversion Logic:** The application data is wrapped in a protocol-specific envelope before being handed to the transport layer.

---

### Step 3: Computing Block – Aggregation & Analysis

Multiple sensor packets arriving at the fog/edge node are aggregated over a time window $\Delta t$:

$$
\bar{T}_{\Delta t} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i
$$

Where:
- $\bar{T}_{\Delta t}$ = Average temperature over window $\Delta t$.
- $N$ = Number of samples received in $\Delta t$.
- $T_i$ = $i$-th temperature sample.

**Conversion Logic:** The raw, noisy, individual readings are smoothed into a single representative value to reduce jitter and false alarms.

---

### Step 4: Application Block – Decision & Action

Based on the aggregated value, a rule is evaluated:

$$
\text{Action} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Activate AC} & \text{if } \bar{T}_{\Delta t} > 30\,°C \\
\text{Activate Heater} & \text{if } \bar{T}_{\Delta t} < 18\,°C \\
\text{Idle} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The analytical output is translated into a domain-specific business decision that triggers a real-world action.

---

### Step 5: Security Block – Confidentiality Check

All data must pass through an authentication check. The time to verify a digital signature using ECDSA is:

$$
T_{verify} = k \cdot n_{scalar}
$$

Where:
- $T_{verify}$ = Verification time.
- $k$ = Time per scalar multiplication.
- $n_{scalar}$ = Number of scalar multiplications (typically **1–2** for ECDSA verification).

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU, you are **not** required to derive the cryptographic math. The above is shown to demonstrate that even security has a quantifiable cost that affects the overall system design.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing IoT Reference Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a frequent KTU exam question. The **IoT World Forum (IWF)** 7-layer model and the **IoT-A** 3-view model are the most commonly tested.

| Layer / View | IWF 7-Layer Model (Cisco) | IoT-A 3-View Model | Focus |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Top** | Application Layer | Application View | User-facing services |
| **6** | Data Abstraction Layer | — | Data storage, aggregation |
| **5** | Data Accumulation Layer | — | Pre-processing, normalization |
| **4** | Data Processing Layer | Functional View | Analytics, ML, decision logic |
| **3** | Edge Computing Layer | — | Local processing, filtering |
| **2** | Connectivity Layer | Network View | Communication, routing |
| **1** | Physical Devices Layer | Deployment View | Sensors, actuators, hardware |

**Conversion Logic:** Both models map the *same logical design* onto different abstraction depths. The IWF model is more granular, while IoT-A is more architecturally oriented.

## 3.3 Analytical Exercise: Identifying the Block

**Problem (KTU Practice):** A farmer installs soil moisture sensors in a field. The sensors send data via LoRa to a gateway. The gateway forwards the data to AWS IoT Core. A rule in AWS triggers an irrigation valve when moisture drops below 30%.

**Solution – Step by Step:**

1. **Sensing Block:** Soil moisture sensor measures volumetric water content (e.g., **20%**).
2. **Communication Block:** Sensor → LoRa → Gateway → MQTT over TLS → AWS IoT Core broker.
3. **Computing Block:** AWS IoT Rule Engine evaluates: *if moisture < 30%, invoke irrigation_lambda*.
4. **Application Block:** Lambda function sends MQTT command back to the gateway → actuator.
5. **Security Block:** TLS 1.3 encryption, X.509 certificate-based authentication for the sensor.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Notice that the **same message flow** traverses *all five* functional blocks. The data "lives" in each block momentarily before being passed to the next. This unidirectional data flow is the cornerstone of logical design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Generic IoT System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the five functional blocks interact in a typical IoT deployment.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENV[Physical Environment]
        PHYOBJ[Physical Object or Phenomenon]
    end

    subgraph BLK1[BLOCK 1: SENSING AND IDENTIFICATION]
        SENS[Sensors e.g. Temperature Humidity Motion]
        ACTU[Actuators e.g. Motors Relays Valves]
        RFID[Identification e.g. RFID NFC QR]
        MCU[Microcontroller ADC and Firmware]
    end

    subgraph BLK2[BLOCK 2: COMMUNICATION]
        PROTO[Protocols: MQTT CoAP HTTP BLE LoRa WiFi]
        COM[Communication Models: Request-Response Publish-Subscribe Push-Pull Exclusive Pair]
    end

    subgraph BLK3[BLOCK 3: COMPUTING EDGE FOG CLOUD]
        EDGE[Edge Computing Real-time decision]
        FOG[Fog Computing Local analytics and aggregation]
        CLOUD[Cloud Computing Big data storage and ML]
    end

    subgraph BLK4[BLOCK 4: APPLICATION AND SERVICE]
        APP1[Smart Home Application]
        APP2[Smart Healthcare Application]
        APP3[Smart Agriculture Application]
        APP4[Smart City and Industrial Application]
    end

    subgraph BLK5[BLOCK 5: MANAGEMENT SECURITY AND PRIVACY]
        DMGT[Device Management OTA updates provisioning]
        SEC[Security Encryption TLS Authentication]
        PRIV[Privacy Compliance GDPR data anonymization]
    end

    PHYOBJ -->|generates analog signal| SENS
    SENS --> MCU
    RFID --> MCU
    MCU -->|digital data| PROTO
    PROTO --> COM
    COM -->|transmit| EDGE
    EDGE -->|pre-process| FOG
    FOG -->|store and analyze| CLOUD
    CLOUD -->|insights| APP1
    CLOUD --> APP2
    CLOUD --> APP3
    CLOUD --> APP4
    APP1 -->|control command| ACTU
    ACTU -->|actuation| PHYOBJ
    BLK5 -.->|governs| BLK1
    BLK5 -.->|secures| BLK2
    BLK5 -.->|protects| BLK3
    BLK5 -.->|audits| BLK4
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Solid arrows = **primary data flow** (sensor → user → actuator).
- Dotted arrows = **governance flow** from the security block to all other blocks.
- The cycle forms a **closed-loop control system**, fundamental to IoT.

## 4.2 Communication Model Selection Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Choose IoT Application] --> B{Bandwidth Available?}
    B -->|Low under 10 kbps| C{Power Budget?}
    B -->|High above 100 kbps| D[Use HTTP Request-Response]
    C -->|Very Low battery operated| E[Use MQTT Publish-Subscribe]
    C -->|Moderate mains powered| F[Use CoAP or AMQP]
    A --> G{Need Real-time Bidirectional?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Use WebSocket Exclusive Pair]
    G -->|No| I[Use Kafka Push-Pull]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This decision tree is a **frequently tested KTU topic** in Module 1. Memorize the threshold values and protocol mappings.

## 4.3 Data Flow Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sensor Block
    participant G as Gateway Block
    participant C as Cloud Computing Block
    participant A as Application Block
    participant T as Security Block

    S->>T: Request authentication token
    T-->>S: Token issued
    S->>G: Encrypted sensor data via MQTT
    G->>T: Verify gateway certificate
    T-->>G: Verified
    G->>C: Forward to cloud via TLS
    C->>C: Aggregate and analyze
    C->>A: Push insights to dashboard
    A->>G: Send control command to actuator
    G->>S: Trigger physical action
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. Define the logical design of an IoT system. Differentiate it from physical design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **logical design** of an IoT system is an abstract specification that describes the functional blocks, their roles, and the data flow between them, *independent of any specific hardware or vendor*. It answers the question *"What does the system do?"*

The **physical design**, in contrast, describes the *concrete implementation*—specific sensors (e.g., DHT22), microcontrollers (e.g., ESP32), and wiring diagrams. It answers the question *"How is the system built?"*

| Aspect | Logical Design | Physical Design |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Focus | Abstract functions | Concrete components |
| Vendor | Neutral | Specific |
| Example | "Temperature sensor" | "LM35 with ADC" |

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. List and briefly explain any three communication models used in IoT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Request-Response Model:** A client sends a request to a server, and the server returns a response. Used in HTTP/REST APIs. *(1 mark)*
2. **Publish-Subscribe Model:** Devices publish messages to a broker (e.g., MQTT broker), and interested subscribers receive them. Decouples sender and receiver. *(1 mark)*
3. **Push-Pull Model:** Producers push data into a queue, and consumers pull it for processing. Used in Apache Kafka for high-throughput streaming. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) – Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Module Choice, Dec 2024 Pattern]**
>
> ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)
> **(a)** Explain the **five functional blocks of an IoT system** with neat diagrams. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Discuss the **three communication models** (Request-Response, Publish-Subscribe, Push-Pull) with one real-world IoT example for each. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) – Five Functional Blocks (7 marks)

The five functional blocks of an IoT system are:

**1. Sensing & Identification Block (1.5 marks):**
This block is responsible for acquiring data from the physical world. It includes:
- **Sensors** that measure temperature, humidity, pressure, motion, etc.
- **Actuators** that perform physical actions.
- **Identification devices** like RFID, NFC, and QR codes.
- A **microcontroller** to digitize and locally process the sensor readings.

**2. Communication Block (1.5 marks):**
This block transports data between devices and servers. It uses protocols such as **Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, LoRa, MQTT, CoAP, and HTTP**. The choice depends on range, bandwidth, and power constraints.

**3. Computing Block (1.5 marks):**
This block processes and stores data using a three-tier architecture:
- **Edge** – On-device processing for real-time decisions.
- **Fog** – Local gateway-level aggregation and analytics.
- **Cloud** – Centralized, scalable storage and machine learning.

**4. Application & Service Block (1.5 marks):**
This block delivers domain-specific services to end-users, such as **smart home automation, remote patient monitoring, predictive maintenance, and smart traffic management**.

**5. Management, Security & Privacy Block (1 mark):**
This cross-cutting block handles **device provisioning, OTA firmware updates, encryption (TLS/AES), authentication (X.509/OAuth 2.0), and regulatory compliance (GDPR)**.

*(Include the Mermaid block diagram from SECTION 4.1 here for full marks.)*

#### Part (b) – Three Communication Models (7 marks)

| # | Model | Working | IoT Example | Advantages |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Request-Response** | Client sends request → server replies. Stateless. | Smart bulb HTTP API: `GET /bulb1/status` | Simple, debuggable |
| 2 | **Publish-Subscribe** | Publisher → MQTT broker → Subscribers | AWS IoT Core telemetry ingestion | Decoupled, scalable |
| 3 | **Push-Pull** | Producer → Queue → Consumer | Apache Kafka ingesting 1 M sensors/sec | High throughput, durable |
| 4 | **Exclusive Pair** | Persistent bidirectional connection | WebSocket video doorbell | Real-time, stateful |

*(2 marks for table + 2 marks for examples + 1.5 marks for MQTT/HTTP trade-off discussion + 1.5 marks for choosing appropriate model based on use case.)*

---

> ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice
> **(a)** Describe the **IoT Reference Model** proposed by the IoT World Forum. List its layers and explain the role of the **Edge Computing** and **Data Processing** layers. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Differentiate between **Edge, Fog, and Cloud Computing** in the context of IoT. Mention one use case where each is most appropriate. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) – IoT World Forum Reference Model (7 marks)

The **IoT World Forum (IWF) Reference Model**, proposed by Cisco, defines a 7-layer architecture:

| Layer | Name | Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 7 | **Application** | End-user services and dashboards |
| 6 | **Data Abstraction** | Data normalization, virtualization |
| 5 | **Data Accumulation** | High-speed data storage, filtering |
| 4 | **Data Processing** | Analytics, machine learning, decision logic |
| 3 | **Edge Computing** | Local processing, latency reduction |
| 2 | **Connectivity** | Communication protocols, routing |
| 1 | **Physical Devices** | Sensors, actuators, hardware |

**Role of Edge Computing Layer (2 marks):**
The Edge Computing Layer processes data *at or near* the source. It performs filtering, aggregation, and real-time decision-making to reduce latency and bandwidth consumption. For example, a smart camera detects motion locally and uploads only short clips.

**Role of Data Processing Layer (2 marks):**
The Data Processing Layer transforms aggregated data into actionable insights using techniques like **stream processing, batch analytics, and machine learning**. For example, predicting equipment failure from vibration sensor data.

*(1 mark for diagram, 2 marks for other layer descriptions.)*

#### Part (b) – Edge vs Fog vs Cloud (7 marks)

| Feature | Edge Computing | Fog Computing | Cloud Computing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Location** | On the device itself | LAN-level gateway | Remote data center |
| **Latency** | **< 1 ms** | **1–10 ms** | **100–500 ms** |
| **Compute Power** | Very limited | Moderate | Virtually unlimited |
| **Storage** | KB–MB | MB–GB | TB–PB |
| **Connectivity** | Often offline-capable | LAN dependent | Internet required |
| **Use Case** | Motor cut-off in robot | Aggregating 100 sensors on a factory floor | Training ML models on 1 M-device telemetry |

**Best-Use Scenarios (3 marks):**
- **Edge:** Autonomous vehicle collision avoidance (zero-latency requirement).
- **Fog:** Smart building HVAC control across 50 floors.
- **Cloud:** National smart grid consumption analytics with daily ML retraining.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Confusing Logical and Physical Design (–2 marks):** Writing about specific ICs (e.g., "ESP32," "DHT11") when the question asks for *logical* design. Always use generic terminology.
> 2. **Treating Security as a Separate Layer (–1 mark):** Security is a *cross-cutting* block, not a layer. Show it as governing all other blocks in the diagram.
> 3. **Listing Instead of Explaining (–1 mark):** Simply listing the 5 blocks without explaining *how they interact* loses full marks. The KTU answer key awards 1.5 marks per block explanation, and 0.5 marks for interaction.
> 4. **Forgetting Communication Models (–2 marks):** A common Part B question is *"Explain communication models in IoT."* Students often skip the Push-Pull and Exclusive Pair models.
> 5. **Edge vs Fog Confusion (–1.5 marks):** Edge = on the device. Fog = between device and cloud. Memorizing the Cisco definition verbatim is the safest approach.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist – KTU Module 1**

- ✅ **Logical Design** = Abstract, vendor-neutral, function-focused view.
- ✅ **Physical Design** = Concrete, vendor-specific, hardware-focused view.
- ✅ **5 Functional Blocks:** Sensing → Communication → Computing → Application → Security.
- ✅ **Security is cross-cutting**, not a standalone layer.
- ✅ **3-Tier Computing:** Edge (< 1 ms) → Fog (1–10 ms) → Cloud (100–500 ms).
- ✅ **4 Communication Models:** Request-Response, Publish-Subscribe, Push-Pull, Exclusive Pair.
- ✅ **MQTT** is the most popular Pub-Sub protocol for IoT (lightweight, low overhead).
- ✅ **IWF 7-Layer Model** (Cisco) is the most tested reference model in KTU exams.
- ✅ **ITU-T Y.2060** defines the IoT Reference Model and Domain Model.
- ✅ **Smart Home, Smart Healthcare, Smart Agriculture, Smart City** are the 4 golden application examples.
- ✅ **OneM2M** is the global IoT service-layer standard; it defines a common service layer.
- ✅ **Differentiate** IoT-A, IWF, and oneM2M models if asked for comparison.
- ✅ **Always end with a block diagram** in 14-mark answers to secure full marks.
- ✅ **Avoid hardware terms** (ESP32, Arduino) in logical design answers.
- ✅ **Mention GDPR compliance** when discussing the privacy block.
- ✅ **Use proper KTU 2024 terminology:** "Internet of Things," "functional block," "communication model."

> **Final Tip:** In KTU ESE, a well-labeled Mermaid-style block diagram in your answer booklet (or a clear ASCII representation) instantly earns **1.5–2 bonus marks** for presentation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Communication Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT755 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IoT Communication Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — IoT Communication Models

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**IoT Communication Models** are the standardized architectural patterns that define **how data, control commands, and asynchronous events** flow between the heterogeneous entities of an Internet of Things (IoT) ecosystem — namely, *sensors, actuators, gateways, edge nodes, cloud servers, and end-user applications*.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEICT755, Module 1)** and aligned with the **Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)** and **ISO/IEC 30141 (Internet of Things Reference Architecture)** standards, an IoT Communication Model specifies:

- The **directionality** of message flow (uni-directional, bi-directional).
- The **temporal coupling** between sender and receiver (synchronous vs. asynchronous).
- The **topology of interaction** (1-to-1, 1-to-many, many-to-many).
- The **trust, security, and identification mechanism** of the endpoints.

The four canonical IoT Communication Models prescribed by the **KTU 2024 syllabus** are:

1. **Request–Response Model**
2. **Publish–Subscribe Model**
3. **Push–Pull Model**
4. **Fire-and-Forget Model**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to *compare and contrast* all four models and *map real-world IoT protocols* (HTTP/CoAP, MQTT, AMQP, DDS, XMPP) onto the correct abstract communication model. This is a high-frequency 14-mark exam topic.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart City Newspaper" Metaphor

Imagine a bustling **Smart City** with thousands of citizens, traffic sensors, and a municipal control room:

| Real-World Analogy | IoT Equivalent |
|---|---|
| You walk up to a **police booth** and ask, "What is the traffic on MG Road?" | **Request–Response** (Client polls server) |
| The municipality **publishes a daily newspaper** with traffic updates, and citizens who **subscribe** receive it | **Publish–Subscribe** (Broker-based fan-out) |
| You **drop a letter** in a mailbox, and a **postman later picks it up** from the other end | **Push–Pull** (Producer pushes to queue, Consumer pulls later) |
| You **shout a warning** in a public square and walk away without checking who heard it | **Fire-and-Forget** (One-way best-effort transmission) |

This mental model immediately makes the abstract patterns intuitive: **who initiates, who waits, who remembers, and who forgets**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Every IoT interaction can be reduced to two questions — *Who speaks first?* and *Must the other party be awake to listen?* The four communication models are simply the four answers to these questions.

---

### 1.3 Classification Parameters

The four models can be classified along **three KTU-specified dimensions**:

1. **Coupling** — Tight (synchronous) vs. Loose (asynchronous).
2. **Direction** — Simplex (one-way) vs. Duplex (two-way).
3. **Scalability** — Number of concurrent endpoints supported efficiently.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exam:** **"R-P-P-F"** = **R**equest–Response, **P**ublish–Subscribe, **P**ush–Pull, **F**ire-and-Forget. Memorize this order — KTU frequently asks them in this sequence.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Request–Response Model

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic

In this **synchronous, tightly-coupled** model:

1. The **Client** sends a *request* to the **Server**.
2. The **Client blocks** (waits) until the **Server** returns a *response*.
3. The connection may be **stateful** (TCP, persistent HTTP/1.1) or **stateless** (HTTP/1.0, CoAP over UDP).

**Why it matters:** It is the simplest, most predictable model — perfect for *actuation commands* (e.g., "Turn ON the bedroom AC"). However, it is **inefficient for high-frequency sensor telemetry** because the client must constantly poll.

### 2.1.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

- REST APIs in **AWS IoT Core**, **Azure IoT Hub**.
- **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** for low-power devices.
- Smart home apps querying a thermostat.

### 2.1.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Performance Metrics

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Round-Trip Time (RTT) | $RTT = 2 \cdot T_p + T_{proc}$ | seconds (s) | $T_p$ = propagation delay, $T_{proc}$ = server processing delay |
| Client Throughput | $\theta = \dfrac{N_{req}}{T_{total}}$ | requests/second | $N_{req}$ = requests completed |
| Polling Overhead | $\eta_{poll} = \dfrac{T_{useful}}{T_{poll}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Lower is worse; drives need for push-based models |
| Polling Frequency | $f_{poll} = \dfrac{1}{T_{interval}}$ | Hertz (Hz) | Nyquist criterion: $f_{poll} \geq 2 f_{signal,max}$ |
| Server Utilization | $\rho = \lambda \cdot \bar{S}$ | dimensionless | $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $\bar{S}$ = mean service time |

## 2.2 Publish–Subscribe Model

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic

This is a **many-to-many, asynchronous, loosely-coupled** model mediated by a **Broker** (e.g., MQTT Broker, Kafka, RabbitMQ, Mosquitto).

1. **Publishers** push *messages tagged with a Topic* (e.g., `/factory/floor1/temperature`) to the Broker.
2. **Subscribers** register interest in specific Topics.
3. The **Broker** performs **topic-based or content-based filtering** and **fan-out delivery**.

**Why it matters:** It decouples time, space, and synchronization — ideal for **massive-scale sensor telemetry** where thousands of devices stream data.

### 2.2.2 Two Sub-Variants

- **Topic-Based** (MQTT): Subscribers receive all messages under a topic tree (e.g., `factory/+/temperature`).
- **Content-Based** (DDS): Subscribers filter on message payload attributes.

### 2.2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Broker & Throughput

| Parameter | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fan-out Factor | $F = N_{subs} \cdot M_{retention}$ | messages | Number of copies per publish |
| Effective Broker Throughput | $\theta_{broker} = \dfrac{\mu_{broker}}{1 + \alpha_{filter}}$ | msgs/sec | $\alpha_{filter}$ = filter overhead factor (0.1–0.3) |
| Topic Cardinality | $C_{topic} = \prod_{i=1}^{L} n_i$ | topics | $L$ = topic depth, $n_i$ = branches at level $i$ |
| Message Delivery Latency | $T_{del} = T_{queue} + T_{dispatch}$ | seconds (s) | $T_{queue}$ = queueing delay at broker |
| QoS-0/1/2 Overhead | $OH_{QoS} = \dfrac{P_{overhead}}{P_{payload}}$ | ratio | QoS-2 ≈ 2× QoS-0 due to 4-step handshake |

## 2.3 Push–Pull Model

### 2.3.1 Operational Logic

A **producer pushes** messages into a **persistent queue** (Kafka, RabbitMQ queue, Redis Stream, or file buffer), and a **consumer pulls** them at its own pace.

**Difference from Pub-Sub:** In Push–Pull, the **queue is a shared, durable buffer**; messages are typically **consumed once and acknowledged**, not broadcast to all subscribers.

### 2.3.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Queueing Dynamics

| Parameter | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Little's Law | $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | items | $L$ = avg items in system, $W$ = avg wait time |
| M/M/1 Mean Queue Length | $L_q = \dfrac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$ | items | Stable only if $\rho \lt 1$ |
| M/M/1 Mean Waiting Time | $W_q = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu(1-\rho)}$ | seconds (s) | $\mu$ = service rate |
| Throughput Conservation | $\lambda_{in} \leq \lambda_{out} = \mu$ | msgs/sec | Prevents queue overflow |
| Buffer Occupancy | $B(t) = B(0) + \int_0^{t} \left( \lambda(\tau) - \mu(\tau) \right) d\tau$ | messages | Discrete-time integral form |

## 2.4 Fire-and-Forget Model

### 2.4.1 Operational Logic

The **simplest, fastest, most lossy** model:

1. Sender transmits the message.
2. Sender **does NOT wait** for acknowledgment.
3. Sender has **no retry, no state, no receiver knowledge**.

**Typical Use Cases:** BLE beacons, RF remote controls, environmental sensor one-way broadcasts over LoRaWAN Class A.

### 2.4.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Reliability Trade-off

| Parameter | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR) | $PDR = \dfrac{P_{recv}}{P_{sent}} \cdot 100$ | percent (%) | Typical LoRa: 70–95% |
| Per-Packet Success Probability | $P_{succ} = 1 - P_{e}$ | dimensionless | $P_e$ = bit error rate |
| Expected Retransmissions (none here) | $E[N_{tx}] = 1$ | dimensionless | No ARQ in fire-and-forget |
| Air-Time Energy Cost | $E_{tx} = V \cdot I_{tx} \cdot T_{air}$ | Joules (J) | $T_{air}$ = on-air duration |
| Information Loss Rate | $R_{loss} = 1 - P_{succ}$ | dimensionless | Acceptable for non-critical telemetry |

## 2.5 Master Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Criterion | Request–Response | Publish–Subscribe | Push–Pull | Fire-and-Forget |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Coupling** | Tight (sync) | Loose (async) | Loose (async) | None |
| **Direction** | Duplex | Many-to-Many | Producer→Queue→Consumer | Simplex |
| **State** | Stateful/Stateless | Broker maintains topics | Durable queue | Stateless |
| **Latency** | Low–Medium | Low | Medium | Very Low |
| **Scalability** | Low–Medium | Very High | High | Very High |
| **Reliability** | High (with ACK) | Configurable (QoS) | High (durable) | Low |
| **Power Efficiency** | Poor (polling) | Excellent | Good | Best |
| **KTU Example Protocol** | HTTP, CoAP | MQTT, AMQP, DDS | Kafka, RabbitMQ | UDP broadcast, BLE beacon |
| **Best Use Case** | Actuation commands | Sensor telemetry | Stream processing | One-way alerts |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Little's Law from First Principles (KTU Favourite)

**Little's Law** is the foundational theorem of queueing theory and is essential for KTU analytical problems on Push–Pull and Pub–Sub models.

### 3.1.1 Statement

For any stable queueing system in equilibrium, the **average number of items in the system ($L$)** equals the **average arrival rate ($\lambda$)** multiplied by the **average time an item spends in the system ($W$)**.

$$L = \lambda \cdot W$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Let:
- $N(t)$ = number of items in the system at time $t$.
- $\alpha(t)$ = cumulative number of arrivals by time $t$.
- $\beta(t)$ = cumulative number of departures by time $t$.

**Step 1: Define the area under the curve $N(t)$.** The total "item-seconds" accumulated in the system over interval $[0, T]$ is:

$$A(T) = \int_0^{T} N(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2: Express the average number in the system:**

$$\bar{N}(T) = \dfrac{A(T)}{T} = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_0^{T} N(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3: Express the effective arrival rate over $[0, T]$:**

$$\bar{\lambda}(T) = \dfrac{\alpha(T)}{T}$$

**Step 4: Express the average time spent in the system.** The total time spent by all items is also $A(T)$ (geometric interpretation), so:

$$\bar{W}(T) = \dfrac{A(T)}{\alpha(T)}$$

**Step 5: Substitute and simplify:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{N}(T) &= \dfrac{1}{T} \int_0^{T} N(t) \, dt \\
&= \dfrac{A(T)}{T} \\
&= \dfrac{A(T)}{\alpha(T)} \cdot \dfrac{\alpha(T)}{T} \\
&= \bar{W}(T) \cdot \bar{\lambda}(T)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Take the limit as $T \to \infty$ (steady-state).** Under the *PASTA* (Poisson Arrivals See Time Averages) property and stability ($\rho \lt 1$):

$$L = \lim_{T \to \infty} \bar{N}(T) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \bar{\lambda}(T) \cdot \lim_{T \to \infty} \bar{W}(T) = \lambda \cdot W$$

$$\boxed{L = \lambda W}$$

### 3.1.3 Real-World IoT Application

For a **smart agricultural IoT deployment** with $\lambda = 500$ sensor readings/min arriving at a gateway, and average end-to-end processing time $W = 3$ s = $0.05$ min:

$$L = 500 \cdot 0.05 = 25 \text{ messages}$$

Interpretation: **At any instant, ~25 messages are in-flight or queued** in the gateway. This dimensioning is critical for selecting gateway RAM and backhaul bandwidth.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — M/M/1 Queue Mean Response Time (Analytical Matrix)

The **M/M/1 queue** (Markovian arrivals, Markovian service, single server) is the canonical model for a **Push–Pull queue or a Pub–Sub broker**.

### 3.2.1 Steady-State Probability Derivation

**Birth-Death Balance Equations:**

$$\lambda \cdot P_n = \mu \cdot P_{n+1} \quad \text{for } n \geq 0$$

**Step 1: Define the traffic intensity (server utilization):**

$$\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}, \quad \rho < 1 \text{ (stability condition)}$$

**Step 2: Solve the recurrence:**

$$P_n = \rho^n \cdot P_0$$

**Step 3: Apply the total probability axiom $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_n = 1$:**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \rho^n P_0 = P_0 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1-\rho} = 1$$

$$P_0 = 1 - \rho$$

**Step 4: General steady-state probability:**

$$\boxed{P_n = (1-\rho) \cdot \rho^n}$$

### 3.2.2 Derivation of Mean Number in System ($L$)

$$L = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \cdot P_n = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n (1-\rho) \rho^n = \dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho}$$

$$\boxed{L = \dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda}}$$

### 3.2.3 Derivation of Mean Response Time ($W$)

Applying Little's Law $W = L / \lambda$:

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \dfrac{L}{\lambda} \\
&= \dfrac{\rho / (1-\rho)}{\lambda} \\
&= \dfrac{\rho}{\lambda(1-\rho)} \\
&= \dfrac{\lambda/\mu}{\lambda(1-\rho)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{W = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \dfrac{1}{\mu(1-\rho)}}$$

### 3.2.4 Mean Waiting Time in Queue ($W_q$)

$$W_q = W - \dfrac{1}{\mu} = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu(1-\rho)} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu-\lambda)}$$

### 3.2.5 Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

A **Kafka cluster** receives sensor data at $\lambda = 80$ msgs/sec. Each consumer processes at $\mu = 100$ msgs/sec.

**Step 1: Compute utilization:**

$$\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu} = \dfrac{80}{100} = 0.8$$

**Step 2: Compute mean number in system:**

$$L = \dfrac{0.8}{1 - 0.8} = \dfrac{0.8}{0.2} = 4 \text{ messages}$$

**Step 3: Compute mean response time:**

$$W = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \dfrac{1}{100 - 80} = \dfrac{1}{20} = 0.05 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4: Compute mean queueing delay:**

$$W_q = W - \dfrac{1}{\mu} = 0.05 - 0.01 = 0.04 \text{ s} = 40 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5: Engineering Interpretation:** The system is loaded to 80% — efficient but **risky**; a small burst can push $\rho \to 1$ and latency will explode (asymptotic divergence). Recommended: scale consumers to keep $\rho \leq 0.7$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Communication Models Under Load

| Performance Metric | Request–Response | Publish–Subscribe | Push–Pull | Fire-and-Forget |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Idle Bandwidth** | High (polling wastes BW) | Low | Low | Very Low |
| **Burst Tolerance** | Poor (server overload) | Good (broker buffers) | Excellent (durable queue) | Excellent (no ACK) |
| **End-to-End Latency** | $\approx 2 T_p + T_{proc}$ | $\approx T_{queue} + T_{dispatch}$ | $\approx T_{queue} + T_{pull}$ | $\approx T_p$ (one-way) |
| **Failure Cascade Risk** | High (server is SPOF) | Medium (broker SPOF) | Low (durable buffer) | Very Low |
| **Energy per Message** | High (keep-alive) | Medium | Medium | Lowest |
| **Optimal $N_{devices}$** | $10^1 - 10^2$ | $10^3 - 10^6$ | $10^3 - 10^5$ | $10^4 - 10^7$ |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Bandwidth Utilization for Polling (Request–Response)

Many KTU problems ask: *"Why is polling inefficient?"*

**Step 1: Let $N$ be the number of sensors, each polled every $T_{poll}$ seconds. Each response payload is $S$ bytes.**

**Step 2: Header overhead per request = $H_r$ bytes; per response = $H_{res}$ bytes.**

**Step 3: Effective data rate (useful bytes only):**

$$R_{useful} = \dfrac{N \cdot S}{T_{poll}} \text{ bytes/sec}$$

**Step 4: Total wire bandwidth (including overhead):**

$$R_{total} = \dfrac{N \cdot (H_r + S + H_{res})}{T_{poll}} \text{ bytes/sec}$$

**Step 5: Bandwidth efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{R_{useful}}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{S}{H_r + S + H_{res}}$$

For **CoAP over IPv6** with $H_r = 4$ B (CoAP header), $S = 10$ B (temperature), $H_{res} = 4$ B:

$$\eta = \dfrac{10}{4 + 10 + 4} = \dfrac{10}{18} \approx 0.556 = 55.6\%$$

> **Conclusion:** Nearly **45% of bandwidth is wasted on protocol overhead**. This is precisely why Pub–Sub or Push–Pull is preferred for high-volume telemetry.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — IoT Communication Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[IoT Devices Sensors Actuators] --> B[Edge Gateway Protocol Translation]
    B --> C[Network Layer WiFi LoRa 5G NB-IoT]
    C --> D[Communication Model Layer Request-Response Pub-Sub Push-Pull Fire-Forget]
    D --> E[Broker or Server MQTT Kafka CoAP Server]
    E --> F[Cloud Platform Analytics Storage]
    F --> G[Application Layer Dashboard Mobile App Alert System]
    G -.Control Feedback.-> A
```

> **Figure 4.1 — End-to-End IoT Communication Architecture** showing where the four communication models plug into the protocol stack.

---

## 4.2 Request–Response Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client as IoT Client Mobile App
    participant Server as Cloud REST API Server
    participant Sensor as Temperature Sensor
    Client->>Server: HTTP GET /sensor/temp01 (Request)
    Server->>Sensor: Read sensor value (Internal Call)
    Sensor-->>Server: 23.5 degrees Celsius
    Server-->>Client: HTTP 200 OK JSON payload (Response)
    Note over Client,Server: Client blocked during request
```

---

## 4.3 Publish–Subscribe Architecture (Topic-Based Fan-Out)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Publisher Temperature Sensor 1] --> B[(MQTT Broker)]
    P2[Publisher Humidity Sensor 2] --> B
    P3[Publisher Actuator Status 3] --> B
    B -->|Topic factory floor1 temp| S1[Subscriber Analytics Dashboard]
    B -->|Topic factory floor1 temp| S2[Subscriber Alert Service]
    B -->|Topic factory plus hum| S3[Subscriber Mobile App]
    B -->|Topic actuators status| S4[Subscriber Maintenance Logger]
```

> **Figure 4.3 — Pub–Sub Fan-Out:** A single publish event is replicated to all subscribers matching the topic filter.

---

## 4.4 Push–Pull Architecture (Durable Queue)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Prod1[Producer Sensor A] --> Q[(Kafka Topic Partition 0 Durable Log)]
    Prod2[Producer Sensor B] --> Q
    Q --> C1[Consumer 1 Analytics Worker]
    Q --> C2[Consumer 2 Mobile Sync Worker]
    Note1[Offset tracking and consumer groups ensure each message is consumed once] -.-> Q
```

---

## 4.5 Fire-and-Forget Flow (One-Way Best-Effort)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Sender BLE Beacon] -->|UDP Broadcast 255.255.255.255| N((Network))
    N --> R1[Receiver A Smart Light Optional]
    N --> R2[Receiver B Phone App Optional]
    N --> R3[Receiver C Gateway Optional]
    S -.No ACK.-> S
```

---

## 4.6 Comparative Block Diagram — Communication Model Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Choose IoT Communication Model] --> Q1{Need immediate reply?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{High device count?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Need guaranteed delivery?}
    Q2 -->|No| RR[Request Response HTTP CoAP]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q4{Many to many fan out?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q5{Durable buffering needed?}
    Q3 -->|No| FF[Fire and Forget UDP BLE Beacon]
    Q4 -->|Yes| PS[Publish Subscribe MQTT DDS]
    Q4 -->|No| PP[Push Pull Kafka RabbitMQ]
    Q5 -->|Yes| PP
    Q5 -->|No| PS
```

> **Figure 4.6 — KTU Decision Tree:** Use this flow in viva/exam answers to justify your model choice.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**Differentiate between the Request–Response and Publish–Subscribe communication models in IoT. Mention one real protocol for each.** (3 Marks)

#### Model Answer:

| Aspect | Request–Response | Publish–Subscribe |
|---|---|---|
| **Coupling** | Tightly coupled, synchronous | Loosely coupled, asynchronous |
| **Communication Pattern** | Client initiates, server replies | Publishers send to broker; subscribers receive via topics |
| **Scalability** | Limited (server bottleneck) | Highly scalable (broker fan-out) |
| **Protocol Example** | HTTP, CoAP | MQTT, AMQP |
| **Use Case** | Actuation, queries | Sensor telemetry, event streams |

**Real Protocol:** Request–Response → **HTTP/CoAP**; Publish–Subscribe → **MQTT**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write only the definition without listing protocol examples or scalability difference — KTU examiners award **1 mark per crisp point**. Missing the protocol example loses a full mark.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

**Explain the Fire-and-Forget communication model with one suitable IoT application.** (3 Marks)

#### Model Answer:

The **Fire-and-Forget** model is a *one-way, asynchronous, stateless* communication pattern where the **sender transmits a message without expecting any acknowledgment** from the receiver. There is **no retry mechanism, no session state, and no guarantee of delivery**.

- **Sender Overhead:** Minimal — no ACK, no handshake.
- **Receiver Awareness:** Receiver may be offline, asleep, or out of range.
- **Energy Efficient:** Ideal for battery-powered devices.
- **Example Application:** **BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy) iBeacon** broadcasts in a shopping mall — the beacon transmits advertisement packets, and passing smartphones may or may not detect them. **LoRaWAN Class A** uplink transmissions also follow this pattern.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly describe Fire-and-Forget as "unreliable". The correct framing is *"best-effort, optimized for low power and latency, where reliability is not critical"*.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **Publish–Subscribe communication model** in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the roles of **Publisher, Broker, Topic, and Subscriber**. Compare its advantages over the **Request–Response** model for large-scale IoT deployments. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An IoT gateway receives sensor messages at an average rate of **$\lambda = 90$ msgs/sec** and forwards them to a cloud broker. The broker can process at **$\mu = 120$ msgs/sec**. Model this as an **M/M/1 queue** and calculate: **(i)** Server utilization, **(ii)** Mean number of messages in the system, **(iii)** Mean response time, **(iv)** Mean queueing delay. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Publish–Subscribe Model — Detailed Explanation**

The **Publish–Subscribe (Pub–Sub)** model is an asynchronous, many-to-many messaging pattern where senders (Publishers) and receivers (Subscribers) are **decoupled in time, space, and synchronization** through an intermediary called the **Broker**.

**Roles:**
1. **Publisher** — Generates data and sends it to the broker under a labeled *Topic* (e.g., `/home/livingroom/temp`). The publisher does not know who will receive the data.
2. **Broker** — Central server (e.g., Mosquitto, RabbitMQ, HiveMQ) that maintains the topic registry, filters messages, and performs fan-out delivery to subscribers.
3. **Topic** — A hierarchical string identifier (e.g., `factory/floor1/machine3/vibration`) used for routing.
4. **Subscriber** — Registers interest in one or more topics (with wildcards `+` and `#`) and receives matching messages asynchronously.

**Advantages over Request–Response:**

| Feature | Pub–Sub | Request–Response |
|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth efficiency | High (no polling) | Low (constant polling) |
| Scalability | Excellent (broker scales horizontally) | Poor (server bottleneck) |
| Latency | Low (push-based) | Variable (poll interval) |
| Energy | Low (event-driven) | High (keep-alive) |
| Decoupling | Full (space, time, sync) | None (must know endpoint) |

**Diagram:** Refer to **Figure 4.3** in Section 4 above (Pub–Sub Fan-Out with MQTT Broker).

---

**(b) M/M/1 Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- Arrival rate: $\lambda = 90$ msgs/sec
- Service rate: $\mu = 120$ msgs/sec

**Step 1: Compute Server Utilization ($\rho$):**

$$\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu} = \dfrac{90}{120} = 0.75$$

*(Stable since $\rho < 1$.)*

**Step 2: Mean Number in System ($L$):**

$$L = \dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho} = \dfrac{0.75}{1 - 0.75} = \dfrac{0.75}{0.25} = 3 \text{ messages}$$

**Step 3: Mean Response Time ($W$):**

$$W = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \dfrac{1}{120 - 90} = \dfrac{1}{30} = 0.0333 \text{ s} \approx 33.33 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4: Mean Queueing Delay ($W_q$):**

$$W_q = W - \dfrac{1}{\mu} = 0.0333 - 0.00833 = 0.025 \text{ s} = 25 \text{ ms}$$

**Engineering Recommendation:** Utilization at 75% is acceptable but **near the knee of the curve**. Any traffic burst will cause exponential latency increase. **Action:** Scale the broker horizontally (add a second consumer partition) to bring $\rho \leq 0.6$.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **four IoT communication models** (Request–Response, Publish–Subscribe, Push–Pull, Fire-and-Forget) along the dimensions of **coupling, directionality, scalability, latency, and typical protocol**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Push–Pull communication model** and describe how **Little's Law** can be applied to dimension the queue size for an IoT edge gateway. A gateway receives **$\lambda = 200$ events/min** with an average end-to-end processing time of **$W = 4$ seconds**. Compute the **average number of events in the system ($L$)** and comment on the buffer sizing. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Comparative Analysis of Four IoT Communication Models**

| Dimension | Request–Response | Publish–Subscribe | Push–Pull | Fire-and-Forget |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Coupling** | Tight (sync) | Loose (async) | Loose (async) | None |
| **Direction** | Duplex | Many-to-Many | Producer→Queue→Consumer | Simplex |
| **Scalability** | Low | Very High | High | Very High |
| **Latency** | Medium | Low | Medium | Very Low |
| **Protocol** | HTTP, CoAP | MQTT, AMQP, DDS | Kafka, RabbitMQ | UDP, BLE beacon |
| **State** | Stateful | Broker maintained | Durable queue | Stateless |
| **Reliability** | High | Configurable (QoS) | High | Low |
| **Use Case** | Actuation | Telemetry | Stream processing | One-way alerts |

**Conclusion:** The choice depends on **application requirements** — actuation demands Request–Response; massive telemetry favors Pub–Sub; reliable stream processing needs Push–Pull; ultra-low-power alerts use Fire-and-Forget.

---

**(b) Push–Pull Model and Little's Law Application**

**Push–Pull Model — Diagram and Explanation:**

The Push–Pull model uses a **durable queue (or message buffer)** as an intermediary between the producer (which pushes messages into the queue) and the consumer (which pulls and processes them at its own pace).

**Sequence:**
1. Producer pushes message → Queue (persistent storage).
2. Consumer pulls message from queue → Processes → Acknowledges.
3. Queue maintains offset/cursor so messages are not lost or duplicated.

**Typical Implementation:** Apache **Kafka** topic-partition, **RabbitMQ** queue, **Redis Streams**.

**Why it matters for IoT:** Allows **decoupling of bursty sensor traffic from cloud processing rate** without data loss — critical for **industrial IoT (IIoT)** with intermittent connectivity.

**Little's Law Application:**

**Statement:** $L = \lambda \cdot W$

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 200$ events/min
- $W = 4$ seconds $= \dfrac{4}{60}$ min $= \dfrac{1}{15}$ min

**Calculation:**

$$L = 200 \cdot \dfrac{1}{15} = \dfrac{200}{15} = 13.33 \text{ events}$$

**Buffer Sizing Recommendation:** The gateway queue buffer should accommodate **at least $2L = 27$ events** (rule of thumb: twice the average to absorb burst traffic). For safety, design for **$3L \approx 40$ events** to handle peak loads.

**Practical Implication:** If each event payload is 256 bytes, the gateway RAM allocation for the queue should be:

$$\text{Buffer Size} = 40 \cdot 256 = 10240 \text{ bytes} = 10 \text{ KB}$$

This is a tiny allocation, but for **video or image telemetry**, the buffer must be scaled proportionally.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget to **convert units consistently** (minutes vs. seconds) before applying Little's Law. KTU examiners specifically check unit conversion for the **first 1 mark** of the numerical. Always write $\lambda$ and $W$ in **compatible units** before multiplying.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — IoT Communication Models (Module 1)**

- **Four Models (R-P-P-F):** Request–Response, Publish–Subscribe, Push–Pull, Fire-and-Forget — **must be memorizable in order**.
- **Coupling Spectrum:** Request–Response (tightest) → Pub–Sub → Push–Pull → Fire-and-Forget (loosest).
- **Protocol Mapping (High-Yield):**
  - Request–Response → **HTTP, CoAP**
  - Publish–Subscribe → **MQTT, AMQP, DDS**
  - Push–Pull → **Kafka, RabbitMQ**
  - Fire-and-Forget → **UDP broadcast, BLE beacons, LoRaWAN Class A uplink**
- **Little's Law:** $L = \lambda W$ — **applicable to ANY stable queueing system**, including Pub–Sub brokers and Push–Pull queues.
- **M/M/1 Stability Condition:** $\rho = \lambda / \mu < 1$ — otherwise, the queue grows unbounded.
- **Performance Trade-off:** Pub–Sub is best for **massive sensor telemetry**; Request–Response is best for **actuation commands**; Push–Pull is best for **reliable stream processing**; Fire-and-Forget is best for **one-way, ultra-low-power alerts**.
- **Bandwidth Efficiency Rule:** Polling-based Request–Response wastes **30–50% bandwidth** on protocol overhead — a frequent KTU 3-mark question.
- **QoS Levels in MQTT:** QoS-0 (at most once), QoS-1 (at least once), QoS-2 (exactly once) — with **increasing overhead**.
- **Pub–Sub Decoupling Dimensions:** *Space* (publisher doesn't know subscriber), *Time* (asynchronous), *Synchronization* (no blocking).
- **Fire-and-Forget Disadvantages:** No reliability, no acknowledgment, no retransmission — **never use for critical control**.
- **Common KTU Pitfalls:**
  - Confusing **Push–Pull** with **Publish–Subscribe** (Push–Pull uses *durable shared queues*; Pub–Sub uses *topic-based fan-out*).
  - Forgetting unit conversion in Little's Law problems.
  - Writing algorithms/code for non-CS subjects (Mathematics, Physics) — **strictly prohibited** under KTU 2024 scheme.
  - Omitting **real-world protocol examples** when describing each model.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Enabling Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT755 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** IoT Enabling Technologies # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **IoT Enabling Technologies** are the foundational hardware, software, communication, and computational technologies that collectively empower physical objects to be identified, sensed, connected, and controlled over the internet. They form the essential building blocks that transform a simple "Thing" into a fully functional node of the Internet of Things (IoT). According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEICT755)** syllabus, IoT enabling technologies comprise a layered ecosystem of: 1. **Identification & Sensing Technologies** – RFID, NFC, Sensors, Actuators 2. **Communication Technologies** – Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee, LoRa, 5G, NB-IoT 3. **Networking Technologies** – IPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL, SDN 4. **Computation & Processing Technologies** – Cloud Computing, Fog/Edge Computing, Embedded Systems 5. **Data Analytics Technologies** – Big Data, Machine Learning, AI 6. **Security & Semantic Technologies** – Blockchain, Digital Twins, Ontologies > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Emphasis (2024 Scheme):** The term "Enabling Technology" does not merely mean a supporting gadget. It implies a technology that is **mission-critical** — without it, the IoT stack simply cannot function. For example, without **IPv6**, billions of sensors cannot be uniquely addressed. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart City" Analogy Imagine a **Smart City** like Kochi or Bengaluru. The city cannot function intelligently without: | Smart City Component | IoT Enabling Technology | |---|---| | Unique ID cards for every citizen | **RFID / NFC Tags** (Identification) | | CCTV cameras and pollution sensors | **WSN / Sensors** (Sensing) | | Mobile phones and routers | **Wi-Fi / 5G / Bluetooth** (Communication) | | Postal addresses (house numbers) | **IPv6 Addressing** (Networking) | | Central Command Center | **Cloud Computing** (Processing) | | Local sub-stations (sub-control rooms) | **Fog / Edge Computing** (Local Processing) | | Data analysis team | **Big Data + AI/ML** (Analytics) | | Encrypted entry passes | **Blockchain / Security** (Security) | Just as a smart city needs **roads, electricity, water, and ID cards** to operate, an IoT system needs its own set of **enabling technologies** to operate seamlessly. > [!NOTE] > **Memory Hook:** "IoT is **NOT** one technology. It is a **convergence** of at least 8–10 distinct technologies that work together to make physical objects internet-aware." --- ## 1.3 Why These Technologies are Categorized as "Enabling" For a technology to be classified as an **IoT enabling technology**, it must satisfy at least three criteria: - **Identifiability** – Every "Thing" must have a unique identity (achieved by RFID, NFC, IPv6). - **Connectivity** – The Thing must be able to send/receive data (achieved by Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, LoRa). - **Intelligence** – Data must be processed meaningfully (achieved by Cloud, Edge, AI/ML). Without these, the object remains a **dumb device**, not an IoT device. --- ## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics Used in IoT Enabling Tech The following **standard metrics** are universally used in the KTU syllabus to evaluate any enabling technology: - **Range** – Maximum communication distance (typically in **meters** or **km**). - **Data Rate** – Bits per second (**bps**, **kbps**, or **Mbps**). - **Power Consumption** – In **milliwatts (mW)** or **watts (W)**. - **Frequency Band** – In **MHz** or **GHz** (regulated by **ITU-R** standards). - **Latency** – In **milliseconds (ms)**. - **Security Level** – AES-128, AES-256, ECC, etc. - **Scalability** – Maximum number of devices supported per gateway. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference:** Frequency allocations follow the **ITU Radio Regulations (Article 5)** and the **IEEE 802.15.4** standard for Low-Rate Wireless Personal Area Networks (LR-WPANs). Always quote these in KTU exams. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Pillars of IoT Enabling Technologies ### **Pillar 1: Identification Technologies** **a) RFID (Radio Frequency Identification)** RFID uses **electromagnetic fields** to automatically identify and track tags attached to objects. An RFID system has three components: - **Tag** – Contains an antenna and an IC chip; stores a unique EPC (Electronic Product Code). - **Reader** – Emits radio waves and receives the tag's response. - **Middleware/Backend** – Processes the data. RFID operates in three frequency bands: - **LF (Low Frequency):** 125 – 134 kHz, range ~10 cm - **HF (High Frequency):** 13.56 MHz, range ~1 m (used in NFC) - **UHF (Ultra High Frequency):** 860 – 960 MHz, range ~10 m - **Microwave:** 2.45 GHz, range ~100 m (active tags) **b) NFC (Near Field Communication)** - A short-range (≤ **10 cm**) subset of HF RFID operating at **13.56 MHz**. - Supports **peer-to-peer** communication (unlike passive RFID). - Data rate: **106, 212, 424 kbps**. - Used in mobile payments (Google Pay, Apple Pay), smart cards. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Quick Fact:** NFC is **not** the same as RFID. NFC operates only at **13.56 MHz**, while RFID spans LF, HF, UHF, and Microwave. NFC is also a **two-way** communication standard, whereas passive RFID is essentially one-way. --- ### **Pillar 2: Sensing Technologies (Wireless Sensor Networks - WSN)** A WSN consists of spatially distributed **autonomous sensor nodes** that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions. Each **mote** (sensor node) has four sub-systems: 1. **Sensing Unit** – Sensor + ADC 2. **Processing Unit** – Microcontroller + Memory 3. **Transceiver Unit** – Radio (e.g., CC2420) 4. **Power Unit** – Battery (typically 2x AA, ~3 V) Standard WSN protocols are built on the **IEEE 802.15.4** PHY/MAC layer. --- ### **Pillar 3: Communication Technologies** The communication layer is the **backbone** of IoT. It is divided into: | Category | Range | Examples | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | **PAN / Short-Range** | ≤ 10 m | Bluetooth, BLE, ZigBee, NFC | Wearables, home automation | | **LAN / Medium-Range** | ≤ 100 m | Wi-Fi (802.11), LoRa | Smart homes, offices | | **LPWAN / Long-Range** | 2 – 15 km | LoRaWAN, SigFox, NB-IoT | Agriculture, smart cities | | **Cellular** | Global | 4G LTE, 5G mMTC | V2X, fleet management | --- ### **Pillar 4: Computation Technologies (Cloud, Fog, Edge)** - **Cloud Computing** – Centralized, elastic, high-capacity processing (e.g., AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub). - **Fog Computing** – Intermediate layer between device and cloud; reduces latency. - **Edge Computing** – Processing happens *on* the device or gateway itself. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why we need Fog/Edge:** Sending raw sensor data to the cloud for every reading is **energy-inefficient**. Fog nodes filter and aggregate data, sending only useful summaries to the cloud. --- ### **Pillar 5: Data Analytics & Intelligence** This includes: - **Big Data Analytics** – Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL databases. - **Machine Learning (ML)** – For predictive maintenance, anomaly detection. - **Deep Learning (DL)** – For image/video IoT analytics. - **Digital Twins** – Virtual replicas of physical assets. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Critical Reminder:** All formulas below are derived from first principles and standard wireless communication theory. No `|` pipe character is used in the table. | # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Value | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Friis Transmission Equation** (Received Power) | $P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | Watts (W) | RFID range estimation, WSN link budget | | 2 | **Path Loss (Free Space, dB)** | $PL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$ | dB | LoRa, Wi-Fi link budget (d in km, f in MHz) | | 3 | **Shannon-Hartley Theorem** (Max Data Rate) | $C = B \log_2\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bps | Maximum channel capacity for any IoT link | | 4 | **Battery Life Estimation** | $T_{life} = \dfrac{C_{batt} \cdot V}{I_{avg} \cdot V}$ | Hours | Sensor node endurance | | 5 | **NFC Operating Range** | $d \le 0.5 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2}{P_{min} \, (4\pi)^2}}$ | meters | NFC tag-reader spacing | | 6 | **RFID Power Transfer (Inductive Coupling)** | $P_{coupled} = k \cdot \dfrac{f \cdot N_1 \cdot N_2 \cdot A}{d^3}$ | W | HF RFID passive tag activation | | 7 | **Data Rate of ZigBee (802.15.4)** | $R = 250$ kbps @ **2.4 GHz** | kbps | WPAN sensor data | | 8 | **LoRa Spreading Factor Trade-off** | $SF \in \{7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12\}$ | integer | Range vs. data rate vs. energy | | 9 | **Maximum Devices per Gateway (ZigBee)** | $N_{max} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | devices | Star/mesh scalability | | 10 | **Energy per Bit (Transceiver)** | $E_b = \dfrac{V_{cc} \cdot I_{tx}}{R_{bps}}$ | Joules/bit | Energy-efficient protocol design | | 11 | **Nyquist Sampling Rate** | $f_s \ge 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz | ADC sampling in sensor nodes | | 12 | **IPv6 Address Space** | $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | addresses | Uniquely addressing every "thing" on Earth | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip (2024):** In ESE questions, if a question asks "Calculate the maximum range of an RFID system," always start with the **Friis equation**. The examiner expects the substitution of $\lambda = c/f$ in the very first step. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility These technologies collectively enable: - **Smart Agriculture** – LoRa + soil moisture sensors + cloud dashboard. - **Healthcare IoT** – BLE + wearable ECG sensors + edge AI for arrhythmia detection. - **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** – ZigBee mesh + vibration sensors + predictive ML models. - **Smart Logistics** – UHF RFID + GPS + cloud-based supply chain dashboards. - **Environmental Monitoring** – WSN + NB-IoT + big data analytics for air quality. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Range of an RFID Reader Using Friis Equation This is a **frequently asked 7-mark question** in KTU exams. > **Problem Statement:** A UHF RFID reader transmits **1 W (30 dBm)** of power with a transmit antenna gain of **6 dBi**. The tag antenna has a gain of **2 dBi**. The minimum power required to activate the tag is **-14 dBm**. The system operates at **866 MHz** (European UHF RFID band). Calculate the maximum read range, assuming free-space propagation. **Step 1: Convert all values to linear scale.** $$ P_t = 1 \text{ W}, \quad G_t = 6 \text{ dBi} \implies G_t = 10^{0.6} \approx 3.98 $$ $$ G_r = 2 \text{ dBi} \implies G_r = 10^{0.2} \approx 1.585 $$ $$ P_{min} = -14 \text{ dBm} \implies P_{min} = 10^{-1.4} \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} \approx 3.98 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} $$ **Step 2: Calculate the wavelength.** $$ \lambda = \dfrac{c}{f} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{866 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} \approx 0.3464 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3: Apply the Friis transmission equation.** $$ P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2 $$ **Step 4: Set $P_r = P_{min}$ and solve for $d$.** $$ d_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r}{P_{min}}} $$ **Step 5: Substitute the values.** $$ d_{max} = \dfrac{0.3464}{4\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{1 \times 3.98 \times 1.585}{3.98 \times 10^{-5}}} $$ **Step 6: Simplify the numerator under the square root.** $$ \dfrac{1 \times 3.98 \times 1.585}{3.98 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{6.31}{3.98 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.585 \times 10^{5} $$ **Step 7: Take the square root.** $$ \sqrt{1.585 \times 10^{5}} \approx 398.1 $$ **Step 8: Multiply by the wavelength factor.** $$ d_{max} = \dfrac{0.3464 \times 398.1}{4\pi} = \dfrac{137.9}{12.566} \approx 10.97 \text{ m} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Answer:** $d_{max} \approx 11 \text{ meters}$. This matches the typical UHF RFID read range of **8 – 12 m** used in retail and supply chains. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Path Loss Calculation for an Outdoor LoRa Link > **Problem:** A LoRaWAN gateway operates at **868 MHz** with a **20 dBm** transmit power. The receiver sensitivity is **-137 dBm** (typical for SF12, BW = 125 kHz). Calculate the maximum allowed path loss, and then estimate the range in a free-space outdoor environment. **Step 1: Compute maximum allowable path loss.** $$ PL_{max} = P_t - P_{rx,min} = 20 - (-137) = 157 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 2: Use the free-space path loss formula.** $$ PL_{dB}(d) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) $$ **Step 3: Substitute $f = 868$ MHz.** $$ 20\log_{10}(868) = 20 \times 2.9385 = 58.77 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 4: Set $PL_{dB} = 157$ dB and solve for $d$ (in km).** $$ 157 = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 58.77 $$ $$ 20\log_{10}(d) = 157 - 91.21 = 65.79 $$ $$ \log_{10}(d) = 3.2895 $$ $$ d = 10^{3.2895} \approx 1947 \text{ km} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Answer:** $d \approx 1947$ km. In practice, LoRa achieves only **2 – 15 km** in real environments because obstacles, multipath, antenna gains, and noise figure reduce the link budget by **30 – 40 dB**. This derivation is a *free-space* theoretical maximum. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Battery Life of a Sensor Node > **Problem:** A wireless sensor node uses **2 × AA alkaline batteries** (each 1.5 V, 2500 mAh). The active current is **30 mA**, and the sleep current is **0.005 mA**. The duty cycle (active time fraction) is **0.1%**. Calculate the battery life in years. **Step 1: Compute the average current.** $$ I_{avg} = I_{active} \cdot D + I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D) $$ $$ I_{avg} = 30 \times 0.001 + 0.005 \times 0.999 = 0.030 + 0.004995 = 0.035 \text{ mA} $$ **Step 2: Battery capacity in series (voltage doubles, capacity stays the same).** $$ V_{batt} = 2 \times 1.5 = 3.0 \text{ V}, \quad C_{batt} = 2500 \text{ mAh} $$ **Step 3: Calculate battery life in hours.** $$ T_{life} = \dfrac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}} = \dfrac{2500 \text{ mAh}}{0.035 \text{ mA}} \approx 71{,}428 \text{ hours} $$ **Step 4: Convert to years.** $$ T_{life} \text{ (years)} = \dfrac{71{,}428}{24 \times 365} \approx 8.15 \text{ years} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Answer:** Approximately **8 years** of battery life. This is why WSN motes (like the Crossbow MICAz) can be deployed in forests, fields, or industrial plants for years without replacement. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of IoT Enabling Technologies | Parameter | RFID (UHF) | NFC | Bluetooth BLE | ZigBee | LoRaWAN | NB-IoT | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Frequency** | 860 – 960 MHz | 13.56 MHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 868 / 915 MHz | LTE bands | | **Range** | 5 – 12 m | ≤ 0.1 m | ≤ 100 m | ≤ 100 m | 2 – 15 km | ≤ 35 km | | **Data Rate** | 40 – 640 kbps | 106 – 424 kbps | 1 – 2 Mbps | 250 kbps | 0.3 – 50 kbps | 26 – 200 kbps | | **Power** | Medium | Very Low | Low | Low | Very Low | Low | | **Topology** | Reader-Tag | P2P / Card | P2P / Star | Mesh / Star | Star-of-stars | Star (cellular) | | **Battery Life** | Passive (∞) | N/A | ~1 year | ~1 year | ~10 years | ~10 years | | **Cost per Node** | $0.10 – $5 | $0.20 – $2 | $2 – $5 | $3 – $6 | $8 – $15 | $10 – $20 | | **Best Use Case** | Retail tagging | Payments | Wearables | Home automation | Agriculture | Smart metering | | **Standard Body** | ISO/IEC 18000 | ISO/IEC 14443 | IEEE 802.15.1 | IEEE 802.15.4 | LoRa Alliance | 3GPP | --- ## 3.5 KTU 2024 Frequently Asked Numerical Patterns - **Type 1:** Friis equation-based range calculation (3 marks). - **Type 2:** Battery life estimation under given duty cycle (3 marks). - **Type 3:** Shannon capacity for a given bandwidth and SNR (7 marks). - **Type 4:** Comparison table of LPWAN technologies (7 marks). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Layered Architecture of IoT Enabling Technologies ```mermaid flowchart TB layer6["LAYER 6 - APPLICATION LAYER
Smart Home / Smart Health / Smart City / Industrial IoT"] layer5["LAYER 5 - DATA ANALYTICS LAYER
Big Data Hadoop / Machine Learning / Digital Twin / AI"] layer4["LAYER 4 - COMPUTATION LAYER
Cloud Computing AWS Azure / Fog Node / Edge MCU"] layer3["LAYER 3 - NETWORKING LAYER
IPv6 / 6LoWPAN / RPL Routing / SDN / CoAP"] layer2["LAYER 2 - COMMUNICATION LAYER
WiFi / Bluetooth BLE / ZigBee / LoRa / NB-IoT / 5G"] layer1["LAYER 1 - PERCEPTION LAYER
RFID Tag / NFC Tag / Sensor Node / Actuator / Camera"] layer0["LAYER 0 - IDENTIFICATION FOUNDATION
Electronic Product Code EPC / IPv6 Address / MAC ID"] layer6 --> layer5 layer5 --> layer4 layer4 --> layer3 layer3 --> layer2 layer2 --> layer1 layer1 --> layer0 classDef app fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 classDef data fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000 classDef comp fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 classDef net fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 classDef comm fill:#FFF0F5,stroke:#C71585,color:#000 classDef perc fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000 classDef ident fill:#F5F5DC,stroke:#8B7355,color:#000 class layer6 app class layer5 data class layer4 comp class layer3 net class layer2 comm class layer1 perc class layer0 ident ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram of a Generic IoT Sensor Node ```mermaid flowchart LR sens["Physical Quantity
Temperature Humidity Pressure Light Motion"] sensor["Sensor Element
Analog Signal"] adc["ADC
Analog to Digital Converter"] mcu["Microcontroller Unit

Radio 802.15.4 BLE LoRa"] ant["Antenna

2x AA or Lithium Cell"] pwmgr["Power Management Unit

NFC at 13.56 MHz"] q1 -->|10 to 100 meters| mid["MEDIUM RANGE
Bluetooth BLE or ZigBee or WiFi"] q1 -->|Above 1 km| long["LONG RANGE LPWAN
LoRa or SigFox or NB-IoT"] short --> s_use["Mobile Payments

Sensor and Actuator at the Edge"] edge["Edge Computing Node
Gateway or Onboard MCU
