> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the **exam-ready formula table** for all mathematical calculations in WSNs.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **First-Order Radio Model (Energy per bit)** | $E_{tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^2$ | $E_{elec}$: Electronics energy, $\epsilon_{amp}$: Amplifier energy, $k$: bits, $d$: distance | Joules (J) |
| 2 | **Receive Energy** | $E_{rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$ | Energy to receive $k$ bits | J |
| 3 | **Network Lifetime** | $T_{life} = \frac{E_{initial}}{E_{per\_round}}$ | Ratio of initial energy to per-round energy consumption | Rounds / Seconds |
| 4 | **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$: km, $f$: MHz | dB |
| 5 | **Throughput** | $\eta = \frac{P_{succ}}{T_{slot}}$ | Successful packets per time unit | packets/s |
| 6 | **Optimal Number of Clusters (LEACH)** | $k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{M}{d_{toBS}^2}$ | $N$: nodes, $M$: side of field, $d_{toBS}$: avg distance to BS | Dimensionless |
| 7 | **Energy saved via Aggregation** | $E_{saved} = (n-1) \cdot E_{tx}$ | $n$: cluster size | J |

> **Note on the Radio Model:**
> The **first-order radio model** by *Heinzelman et al.* is the **most frequently tested** energy formula in KTU numerical problems. In the free-space model ($d^2$ power loss), it is used for short distances; the multi-path fading model ($d^4$ power loss) is used for longer distances.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of WSNs

- **Precision Agriculture** — Soil moisture, crop health, irrigation control.
- **Environmental Monitoring** — Forest fire detection, volcano monitoring, flood alerts.
- **Healthcare** — Patient vital sign monitoring (wearable WSN).
- **Smart Cities** — Parking, traffic, pollution, structural health monitoring of bridges.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — Machine condition monitoring in factories.
- **Military** — Battlefield surveillance, intruder detection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Consumption in the First-Order Radio Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **high-priority derivation** for KTU 2024 numerical/analytical questions.

### Problem Setup
We want to transmit a $k$-bit message over a distance $d$ using the first-order radio energy model. We need to derive the **total transmission energy** and the **crossover distance** between free-space and multi-path regimes.

### Step 1 — Transmitter Energy Decomposition
The transmitter consumes energy in two distinct processes:
- Running the **radio electronics** (encoding, modulation, filtering): $E_{elec} \cdot k$
- Powering the **transmit amplifier** to project the signal over distance: $\epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$

$$
E_{tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}
$$

Here, $n$ is the **path-loss exponent** ($n = 2$ for free-space, $n = 4$ for multi-path).

### Step 2 — Receiver Energy
The receiver only spends energy on **electronics** (no amplifier needed):

$$
E_{rx}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

### Step 3 — Splitting into Two Regimes
**Free-space regime** (short range, $d \le d_0$):

$$
E_{tx,fs}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}
$$

**Multi-path regime** (long range, $d > d_0$):

$$
E_{tx,mp}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d^{4}
$$

### Step 4 — Deriving the Crossover Distance
At the crossover point $d_0$, both equations yield the same transmission energy. Equating them:

$$
E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d_0^{2} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{mp} \cdot k \cdot d_0^{4}
$$

The $E_{elec} \cdot k$ terms cancel from both sides. Dividing by $k$:

$$
\epsilon_{fs} \cdot d_0^{2} = \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d_0^{4}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
d_0^{2} = \frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,d_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}}\,}
$$

This **crossover distance** is the threshold at which a node should switch from free-space to multi-path amplification — a critical parameter in routing protocol design (e.g., LEACH).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Multi-Hop vs. Direct Transmission — Energy Trade-Off

### Problem Setup
A node wants to send a $k$-bit packet to a sink located at distance $D$. Should it transmit **directly** (1 hop) or use **multi-hop** through $n$ equal intermediate hops?

### Step 1 — Direct Transmission Energy (Free-Space)
$$
E_{direct} = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot D^{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Multi-Hop Transmission Energy
Each hop is of distance $d = D/n$, and we need $n$ transmits and $(n-1)$ receives (excluding the final sink reception in this analysis):

$$
E_{multi} = n \cdot (E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}) + (n-1) \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $d = D/n$
$$
E_{multi} = n \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + n \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot \left(\frac{D}{n}\right)^{2} + (n-1) \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

Expanding:

$$
E_{multi} = n \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + \frac{\epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot D^{2}}{n} + (n-1) \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

### Step 4 — Comparing the Two Strategies
We want to find when $E_{multi} < E_{direct}$:

$$
n \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + \frac{\epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot D^{2}}{n} + (n-1) \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k < E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot D^{2}
$$

### Step 5 — Simplified Comparison (ignoring $E_{elec}$ electronics for clarity)
For large $D$, the dominant term is the amplifier energy. The condition reduces to:

$$
\frac{\epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot D^{2}}{n} < \epsilon_{fs} \cdot k \cdot D^{2}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{n} < 1
$$

Since $n \ge 2$ for multi-hop, this is **always true** — meaning **multi-hop is always more energy-efficient** than direct transmission in the free-space model. This is the **fundamental justification** for multi-hop routing in WSNs.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Question:** Consider a WSN where a node needs to transmit $k = 4000$ bits to a sink $D = 100$ m away. Use the free-space model with $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit and $\epsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m². Compare direct transmission with a 4-hop multi-hop scheme.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Direct transmission

$$
E_{direct} = (50 \times 10^{-9})(4000) + (10 \times 10^{-12})(4000)(100)^2
$$

$$
E_{direct} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + 4 \times 10^{-4} = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.6 \text{ mJ}
$$

**Step 2:** Multi-hop with $n = 4$ hops, each of $d = 25$ m

$$
E_{multi} = 4 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9})(4000) + 4 \cdot (10 \times 10^{-12})(4000)(25)^2 + 3 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9})(4000)
$$

$$
E_{multi} = 8 \times 10^{-4} + 1 \times 10^{-4} + 6 \times 10^{-4} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} = 1.5 \text{ mJ}
$$

**Result:** In this case (small $E_{elec}$ relative to amplifier energy), direct transmission is more efficient. This highlights that the optimal hop count depends on the **ratio of $E_{elec}$ to $\epsilon_{fs}$**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: WSN vs. Traditional Ad-Hoc Networks

| Parameter | Traditional Ad-Hoc Network | Wireless Sensor Network |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of nodes** | Tens to hundreds | Hundreds to thousands |
| **Node identity** | Individual addressable (IP) | Often addressable by attribute/location |
| **Data flow** | Peer-to-peer | Many-to-one (sensor → sink) |
| **Energy** | Rechargeable / mains | Limited battery (non-rechargeable) |
| **Mobility** | High (laptops, phones) | Mostly static (or low mobility) |
| **Traffic pattern** | Bursty, asymmetric | Continuous, periodic, query-driven |
| **Computational power** | High (multi-core CPUs) | Low (8/16-bit microcontrollers) |
| **Primary design goal** | QoS, throughput | **Energy efficiency, network lifetime** |
| **Failure tolerance** | Tolerable | Must be **highly fault-tolerant** |
| **Scale of deployment** | Sparse, planned | Dense, often random (e.g., dropped from a plane) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Sensor Node

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most commonly drawn diagram** in KTU WSN questions. You are expected to reproduce it from memory in a 7-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Environment] --> B[Sensing Unit]
    B --> C[ADC Analog to Digital]
    C --> D[Processing Unit Microcontroller]
    D --> E[Memory RAM Flash]
    D --> F[Communication Unit Radio Transceiver]
    D --> G[Location Finding System GPS Optional]
    F --> H[Wireless Channel]
    H --> I[Base Station Sink]
    I --> J[Internet or User Network]
    K[Power Unit Battery] -.-> B
    K -.-> C
    K -.-> D
    K -.-> E
    K -.-> F
    K -.-> G
```

**Explanation of Flow:**
- The **sensing unit** captures physical phenomena (heat, light, motion).
- The **ADC** converts analog signals to digital values.
- The **microcontroller** processes and locally decides whether to forward data.
- The **radio transceiver** transmits packets over the wireless channel.
- The **power unit** (dotted lines) supplies energy to *all* other units.
- The **location unit** (optional) provides geographical coordinates.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hierarchical Clustered WSN

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram illustrates the **hierarchical (clustered) architecture** with Cluster Heads — a high-yield KTU topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClusterA [Cluster 1]
        N1[Node N1] --> CH1[Cluster Head CH1]
        N2[Node N2] --> CH1
        N3[Node N3] --> CH1
    end
    subgraph ClusterB [Cluster 2]
        N4[Node N4] --> CH2[Cluster Head CH2]
        N5[Node N5] --> CH2
        N6[Node N6] --> CH2
    end
    subgraph ClusterC [Cluster 3]
        N7[Node N7] --> CH3[Cluster Head CH3]
        N8[Node N8] --> CH3
        N9[Node N9] --> CH3
    end
    CH1 -->|Aggregated Data| BS[Base Station Sink]
    CH2 -->|Aggregated Data| BS
    CH3 -->|Aggregated Data| BS
    BS --> GW[Gateway]
    GW --> NET[Internet]
    NET --> USER[End User / Cloud]
```

**Functional Explanation:**
- Each **Cluster Head (CH)** aggregates data from its members, performs **data fusion**, and forwards only one summary packet to the **Base Station**.
- This drastically reduces redundant transmissions, saving up to **~70% energy** in dense deployments.
- The **Gateway** acts as a protocol translator between the WSN domain and the IP world (Internet).

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: WSN Protocol Stack (KTU-Favorite)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stack [WSN Protocol Stack]
        AL[Application Layer]
        TL[Transport Layer]
        NL[Network Layer]
        DL[Data Link Layer MAC]
        PL[Physical Layer]
        AL --> TL --> NL --> DL --> PL
    end
    subgraph Planes [Management Planes]
        PM[Power Management Plane]
        MM[Mobility Management Plane]
        TM[Task Management Plane]
    end
    PM -.-> AL
    PM -.-> TL
    PM -.-> NL
    PM -.-> DL
    PM -.-> PL
    MM -.-> NL
    TM -.-> AL
```

**Explanation of Management Planes:**
- **Power Management Plane:** Puts idle nodes to sleep, wakes them on schedule.
- **Mobility Management Plane:** Tracks which nodes have moved (in mobile WSNs).
- **Task Management Plane:** Balances sensing tasks across the network to avoid overburdening any one node.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Energy-Efficient Data Flow in Multi-Hop WSN

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Source Node S] -->|hop 1 d1| R1[Relay R1]
    R1 -->|hop 2 d2| R2[Relay R2]
    R2 -->|hop 3 d3| BS[Base Station Sink]
    BS -.->|Aggregated Result| U[End User]
    S1 -.- B1[Small Energy Reserve]
    R1 -.- B2[Small Energy Reserve]
    R2 -.- B3[Small Energy Reserve]
```

**Key Insight:** Instead of one long-range transmission draining the source's battery, energy is **distributed** across multiple nodes, each making a short-range transmission. Total amplifier energy scales as $\sum d_i^{2}$ instead of $D^{4}$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Wireless Sensor Network. List any four applications of WSN.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of spatially distributed autonomous sensor nodes that cooperatively sense, compute, and communicate data over wireless radio links to a central sink.

**Four Applications:**
1. **Environmental monitoring** — Forest fire detection, flood warning.
2. **Precision agriculture** — Soil moisture and crop health monitoring.
3. **Healthcare** — Wearable patient monitoring systems.
4. **Industrial automation** — Machine condition and predictive maintenance.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between a sensor node and a base station in a WSN.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Sensor Node | Base Station (Sink) |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Limited battery | Mains / rechargeable |
| Computation | Low (microcontroller) | High (PC, server, edge gateway) |
| Role | Sense and forward | Aggregate, store, and act as gateway |
| Mobility | Mostly static | Mostly static |
| Number in network | Hundreds–thousands | One or a few |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**A) (a)** Draw and explain the architecture of a sensor node with a neat block diagram. List the four main subsystems. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 above)

**Four Main Subsystems:**

1. **Sensing Subsystem**
   - Contains physical **sensors** (temperature, pressure, light, etc.) and an **ADC**.
   - Converts environmental analog signals into digital form.
   - Energy-efficient sensors like MEMS are typically used.

2. **Processing Subsystem**
   - Microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, MSP430) with limited **RAM/Flash memory**.
   - Hosts the WSN operating system (TinyOS, Contiki).
   - Performs local computations, scheduling, and routing decisions.

3. **Communication Subsystem**
   - A short-range **radio transceiver** (e.g., CC2420, nRF24L01).
   - Implements the **Physical** and **MAC** layers.
   - **Most power-hungry** component in the node.

4. **Power Subsystem**
   - A **battery** (primary or rechargeable) sometimes coupled with an **energy harvester** (solar, vibration).
   - Power management is critical; nodes use **sleep/wake cycling**.

**Auxiliary Subsystems:** Locator (GPS), Power Generator (harvester), Mobilizer.

---

**(b) Describe the WSN protocol stack in detail. Explain the role of the three management planes. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**WSN Protocol Stack Layers:**

1. **Application Layer**
   - Hosts user applications: query processing, data aggregation, location tracking.
   - Examples: Sensor Management Protocol (SMP), Task Assignment and Data Advertisement Protocol (TADAP).

2. **Transport Layer**
   - Provides reliable end-to-end data delivery.
   - Uses protocols like **PSFQ** (Pump-Slowly Fetch-Quickly) and **RMST** (Reliable Multi-Segment Transport).
   - Often omitted in WSNs due to energy constraints.

3. **Network Layer**
   - Handles **routing** of packets from sensor nodes to the sink.
   - Protocols: **LEACH**, **PEGASIS**, **Directed Diffusion**, **SPIN**.
   - Uses multi-hop communication.

4. **Data Link Layer (MAC)**
   - Manages **channel access** (CSMA/CA, TDMA, S-MAC) and **error control**.
   - Critical for energy efficiency via **duty cycling**.

5. **Physical Layer**
   - Radio frequency transmission, modulation, encoding.
   - Standard: **IEEE 802.15.4** (2.4 GHz, 250 kbps).

**Three Management Planes:**

- **Power Management Plane:** Puts redundant/idle nodes into sleep mode to save energy. Works across all layers.
- **Mobility Management Plane:** Detects and registers node movement, ensuring the route remains valid. Critical for mobile WSNs.
- **Task Management Plane:** Distributes sensing and processing tasks across the network to balance the load and prevent any single node from exhausting its battery.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 2]`

**B) (a)** Explain the three main WSN topologies (Star, Mesh, Hierarchical) with diagrams. State their advantages and disadvantages. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Star Topology**
- All sensor nodes communicate **directly** with a central sink.
- The sink acts as a **coordinator**; nodes cannot communicate with each other.
- **Advantages:** Simple, low latency, easy to implement.
- **Disadvantages:** Sink is a single point of failure; distant nodes drain batteries quickly.

**2. Mesh (Peer-to-Peer) Topology**
- Nodes form an **ad-hoc multi-hop mesh**; data can take any path.
- Self-healing and fault-tolerant.
- **Advantages:** Highly robust, energy-efficient, scalable.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher routing overhead, more complex protocols, increased latency.

**3. Hierarchical (Clustered) Topology**
- Nodes are grouped into **clusters**, each with a **Cluster Head (CH)**.
- CHs aggregate, compress, and forward data to the sink.
- **Advantages:** Scalable, energy-efficient via data aggregation, easy to manage.
- **Disadvantages:** Cluster Head failure disrupts the cluster; CH selection is challenging.

**Diagram Reference:** (Refer to Section 4.2 — Clustered WSN diagram)

---

**(b) Discuss the major challenges in designing Wireless Sensor Networks. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Challenge | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Limited Energy** | Battery is non-rechargeable in most cases; protocols must be energy-aware. |
| 2 | **Limited Hardware** | Small size, low-cost nodes with constrained memory and processing power. |
| 3 | **Scalability** | Protocols must work efficiently for thousands of nodes. |
| 4 | **Fault Tolerance** | Network must keep functioning even when individual nodes fail. |
| 5 | **Unreliable Wireless Channel** | Prone to interference, fading, asymmetric links, high packet loss. |
| 6 | **Security** | Susceptible to eavesdropping, DoS, Sybil, wormhole attacks. |
| 7 | **Data Aggregation** | Need to avoid redundant transmissions to save bandwidth and energy. |
| 8 | **Harsh Deployment Environments** | Nodes may be exposed to heat, dust, water, or physical damage. |
| 9 | **Topology Maintenance** | Routing tables must be updated as nodes die or move. |
| 10 | **Real-Time Constraints** | Some applications (e.g., fire detection) demand ultra-low latency. |

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU students make in WSN questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Power Unit in the node diagram** — Examiners deduct 1-2 marks if the dotted power lines are missing.
> 2. **Confusing "Sink" with "Gateway"** — The Sink is *inside* the WSN; the Gateway is the bridge to the *outside* network.
> 3. **Writing the radio model formula incorrectly** — Forgetting the $E_{elec} \cdot k$ term on the transmitter side is a frequent mark-losing mistake.
> 4. **Conflating WSN with MANET** — Always emphasize that WSNs are *energy-constrained* and *data-centric*, unlike traditional ad-hoc networks.
> 5. **Not stating units** in numerical answers — Always write "Joules" or "J" alongside energy values.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2: WSNs**
>
> ✅ WSN = self-organizing, energy-constrained network of sensor motes + sink.
>
> ✅ Sensor node has 4 blocks: **Sensing, Processing, Communication, Power** (+ optional Locator/Mobilizer).
>
> ✅ Energy budget: $E_{comm} \gg E_{sense} \gg E_{compute}$.
>
> ✅ Multi-hop is more energy-efficient than direct transmission in long-range WSNs.
>
> ✅ Three topologies: **Star, Mesh, Hierarchical (Clustered)**.
>
> ✅ Protocol stack has 5 layers + 3 management planes (Power, Mobility, Task).
>
> ✅ Key routing protocols: **LEACH, PEGASIS, Directed Diffusion, SPIN**.
>
> ✅ Most common MAC protocols: **S-MAC, B-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4**.
>
> ✅ Standard operating system: **TinyOS, Contiki OS**.
>
> ✅ Most common PHY/MAC standard: **IEEE 802.15.4** (2.4 GHz, 250 kbps).
>
> ✅ First-order radio model formula (KTU favorite): $E_{tx}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$
>
> ✅ Crossover distance: $d_0 = \sqrt{\epsilon_{fs} / \epsilon_{mp}}$.
>
> ✅ Major WSN challenges: energy, scalability, fault-tolerance, security, real-time delivery.
>
> ✅ Key applications: agriculture, healthcare, environment, smart cities, military, IIoT.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication Protocols for Wireless Sensor Networks - Connecting Smart Objects - Communication Criteria
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT755 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Communication Protocols for Wireless Sensor Networks - Connecting Smart Objects - Communication Criteria

# 📡 Communication Protocols for WSN — Connecting Smart Objects

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 2):** This note directly addresses the unit segment covering **"Communication Protocols for Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)"** and **"Connecting Smart Objects — Communication Criteria."** It is mapped to **CO2** of the PEICT755 course outcome descriptor (Apply IoT communication technologies to design networked embedded systems).

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)?

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a distributed, self-organizing network of spatially distributed autonomous sensors (smart objects) that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions such as temperature, sound, vibration, pressure, motion, or pollutants, and transmit the collected data through wireless communication links to a central sink or gateway.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of a WSN (Mandatory KTU Definition):**
> 1. **Sensor Nodes (Smart Objects)** — The sensing, processing, and transmitting units.
> 2. **Wireless Communication Protocols** — The shared language governing data exchange.
> 3. **Base Station / Sink / Gateway** — The aggregator that bridges the WSN to the wider Internet (IoT).

---

### 1.2 What is a "Smart Object"?

**Formal Definition:**
A **Smart Object** (also termed a *Smart Thing* or *Smart Transducer*) is a physical or virtual entity that possesses **(a)** a unique identifier, **(b)** embedded sensing/actuation capability, **(c)** local data processing power, and **(d)** a communication interface to participate in an IoT/WSN ecosystem.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Village Postman"

Imagine a small Kerala coastal village where every house has a **village postman** (smart object). Each postman:
- **Knows his house's ID** (unique identifier)
- **Can measure temperature, water level, or wind speed** (sensing)
- **Can decide if the reading is unusual** (local processing)
- **Can call or signal another postman up the chain** (communication protocol)

When postmen coordinate through **agreed-upon rules** (e.g., "talk only one at a time on the radio channel"), the village functions as a **WSN**. The post office in town is the **sink/gateway**, and the rules they follow are the **communication protocol**.

This is *exactly* how a WSN operates: many tiny smart objects with strict energy, range, and bandwidth constraints collaborating via lightweight protocols.

---

### 1.3 Communication Criteria — The Six Axes of Evaluation

When connecting smart objects, an IoT engineer must judge a communication technology against **six fundamental criteria** (often the foundation of KTU 14-mark and 3-mark questions):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Communication Criteria (Master List — KTU Board Favourite):**
>
> | # | Criterion | Engineering Meaning |
> |---|-----------|--------------------|
> | 1 | **Range** | Maximum distance over which reliable communication is possible |
> | 2 | **Bandwidth / Data Rate** | Bits transmitted per second (bps, kbps, Mbps) |
> | 3 | **Power Consumption** | Energy (mW, mJ, mAh) required to transmit/receive |
> | 4 | **Security** | Encryption, authentication, and integrity guarantees |
> | 5 | **Topology Support** | Star, mesh, tree, peer-to-peer capability |
> | 6 | **Scalability & Cost** | Ability to add nodes cheaply without redesign |

These criteria **directly drive protocol selection** for any IoT deployment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Memorise (Board Favourite):**
> - Speed of Light in vacuum: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - Speed of Light in fiber: ≈ **2 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - Free-space path loss reference frequency for IoT: typically **2.4 GHz** ISM band

---

### 1.4 Connecting Smart Objects — The Three-Layer Perspective

Connecting a smart object to the network involves three coordinated layers:

1. **Perception Layer** — The smart object physically senses the world (sensors + micro-controller + radio).
2. **Network Layer** — The communication protocol transports data (e.g., IEEE 802.15.4, ZigBee, BLE).
3. **Application Layer** — The data is interpreted and used (e.g., MQTT, CoAP).

A smart object can connect **directly** (single-hop) or **indirectly** (multi-hop through other smart objects) to reach the sink.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Stack of a WSN Communication Protocol

A WSN communication protocol is best understood as a **layered stack**. KTU expects students to know each layer's role:

- **Physical Layer (PHY):** Modulation, radio frequency, channel encoding. Standard: **IEEE 802.15.4** operates in the **2.4 GHz** ISM band globally (also 868 MHz in EU, 915 MHz in US).
- **MAC Layer (Medium Access Control):** Controls *who* speaks *when* on the shared channel. CSMA/CA, TDMA, or hybrid.
- **Network Layer:** Routing of packets from source node to sink (AODV, RPL, DSR).
- **Transport Layer:** Reliability, congestion control (e.g., TCP-like reliability over UDP for WSN).
- **Application Layer:** Application semantics (sensor data formats, time sync, localization).

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — "The Why & How"

**Why a dedicated PHY?**
> Sensor nodes are energy-constrained (often coin-cell powered). The radio is the largest consumer. PHY design minimizes bit energy **Eb** (energy per bit) and optimizes spectral efficiency $\eta = R_b / B$ where $R_b$ is bit rate and $B$ is bandwidth.

**Why a dedicated MAC?**
> Hundreds of nodes share one wireless medium. A naïve "everyone transmits anytime" causes **collisions**, wasting energy on retransmission. A WSN MAC must balance **collision avoidance** with **energy efficiency** (the dominant MAC goal, unlike Wi-Fi where throughput dominates).

**Why multi-hop routing?**
> Long-range direct transmission from a sensor to a distant sink consumes **exponentially more energy** than multi-hop short hops. This follows the energy consumption model where $E_{TX} \propto d^n$ with $n$ being the path loss exponent (typically 2 for free space, 3–4 for cluttered environments).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.3 — High-Yield Formulas (Save This for the Exam)**

| # | Formula / Law | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Typical Value | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shannon Capacity: $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | $C$ = channel capacity, $B$ = bandwidth, $\text{SNR}$ = signal-to-noise ratio | bps, Hz, dimensionless | Computing maximum data rate of a wireless link |
| 2 | Friis Free-Space Path Loss: $\text{FSPL} = \left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | $d$ = distance, $\lambda$ = wavelength | dimensionless (linear), or dB | Range estimation in open-air WSN |
| 3 | FSPL in dB: $\text{FSPL}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (d, km; f, MHz) | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz | dB | Quick link-budget calculation |
| 4 | Path Loss with exponent: $L_p = L_0 + 10n \log_{10}(d/d_0)$ | $L_0$ = reference loss, $n$ = path loss exponent | dB | Indoor / outdoor WSN range analysis |
| 5 | Energy Model: $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec}\cdot k + E_{amp}\cdot k \cdot d^n$ | $k$ = bits, $E_{elec}$ = electronics energy | Joules (J) | Lifetime estimation of a battery-powered sensor |
| 6 | Receive Energy: $E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec}\cdot k$ | — | Joules | Symmetric link energy |
| 7 | Bit Energy: $E_b = P_{TX} / R_b$ | $P_{TX}$ = transmit power, $R_b$ = bit rate | Joules/bit | Energy efficiency comparison |
| 8 | Spectral Efficiency: $\eta = R_b / B$ | $R_b$ bit rate, $B$ bandwidth | bps/Hz | Comparing modulation schemes |
| 9 | Link Budget: $P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_T + G_R - \text{FSPL} - L_{misc}$ | All gains/losses in dB | dBm | Maximum range computation |
| 10 | Noise Floor: $N = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B)$ | $B$ in Hz | dBm | Minimum detectable signal calculation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Path Loss Exponents (Board Exam Favourite):**
> - $n = 2$ → Free space (open outdoor)
> - $n = 3$ → Urban / indoor with walls
> - $n = 4$ → Dense multipath, heavy clutter

---

### 2.4 Mapping Communication Criteria to Real Protocols

| Protocol | Range | Data Rate | Power | Topology | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IEEE 802.15.4 (PHY/MAC)** | 10–100 m | 250 kbps | Very Low | Star, Mesh | Basis of ZigBee, 6LoWPAN |
| **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4 based)** | 10–100 m | 250 kbps | Very Low | Mesh | Home automation, WSN |
| **Bluetooth LE (BLE 5.0)** | 10–200 m | 2 Mbps | Low | Star, Mesh | Wearables, beacons |
| **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)** | 50–100 m | 54–600+ Mbps | High | Star | Video, high data IoT |
| **LoRaWAN** | 2–15 km | 0.3–50 kbps | Very Low | Star-of-stars | Long-range agriculture |
| **NB-IoT (LTE Cat-NB1)** | >10 km | 26 kbps | Low | Star | Smart metering |
| **Z-Wave** | 30–100 m | 100 kbps | Low | Mesh | Home automation |
| **RFID (Passive)** | <10 m | Few kbps | Zero (harvested) | Point-to-point | Inventory, ID tags |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agriculture:** LoRaWAN-based soil moisture sensors deployed across a cardamom estate in Idukki use the long-range criterion (>2 km).
- **Healthcare:** Bluetooth LE pacemakers prioritize the *low-power* and *security* criteria.
- **Smart Cities:** ZigBee street-light mesh networks emphasize *scalability* and *mesh topology support*.
- **Industrial IoT:** WirelessHART (a WSN protocol) demands *deterministic, secure, time-synchronized* communication, ideal for chemical plants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shannon Channel Capacity (Theoretical Maximum Data Rate)

The Shannon-Hartley theorem sets the **absolute upper bound** on the data rate of a noisy wireless channel. KTU examiners love setting problems where students compute $C$ and then compare it with a real protocol's advertised bit rate.

**Statement:** For a continuous-time channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz perturbed by Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN), the maximum reliable data rate (capacity) is:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \text{SNR}\right) \quad \text{[bits per second, bps]}
$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Starting from the definition of mutual information for an AWGN channel, the maximum mutual information per second is achieved by a Gaussian-distributed input. The mutual information expression is:

$$
I(X;Y) = H(Y) - H(Y \vert X)
$$

Since the noise is AWGN with variance $N = N_0 \cdot B$, the conditional entropy $H(Y \vert X)$ is fixed at:

$$
H(Y \vert X) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(2\pi e N)
$$

The differential entropy of the received signal $Y$ (with signal power $S$ added to noise) is maximized as:

$$
H(Y) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(2\pi e (S + N)\right)
$$

Subtracting the conditional entropy yields the mutual information per sample:

$$
I = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(\frac{S + N}{N}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(1 + \text{SNR})
$$

Since $B$ independent samples can be transmitted per second (Nyquist), the per-second capacity becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 2B \cdot \frac{1}{2}\log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \\[4pt]
  &= B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic (for solving KTU numerical problems):**
- If SNR is given in dB, first convert: $\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{(\text{SNR}_{dB}/10)}$
- If bandwidth $B$ is in kHz or MHz, convert to Hz before applying the formula.
- The final answer $C$ is in **bps**; convert to **kbps or Mbps** as required.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** No practical modulation scheme (BPSK, QPSK, OFDM, etc.) can ever exceed this capacity. ZigBee at 250 kbps in 2 MHz channel with SNR ≈ 20 dB has a Shannon limit of $C = 2 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(101) \approx 13.4$ Mbps — showing ZigBee operates far below the capacity ceiling, trading speed for power efficiency.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Friis Free-Space Path Loss and Range

**Statement:** In free space, the received power $P_R$ relates to transmitted power $P_T$ by:

$$
P_R = P_T \cdot G_T \cdot G_R \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2
$$

**Derivation Steps:**

Consider an isotropic radiator (ideal antenna radiating equally in all directions) transmitting power $P_T$. At distance $d$, the power is distributed uniformly over the surface of a sphere of area $4\pi d^2$. Therefore, the **power flux density** (W/m²) at distance $d$ is:

$$
S_d = \frac{P_T}{4\pi d^2}
$$

An ideal receiving antenna of aperture $A_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{4\pi}$ captures the power:

$$
P_R = S_d \cdot A_{\text{eff}} = \frac{P_T}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{\lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

Re-arranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_R &= P_T \cdot \frac{\lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2} \\[4pt]
\frac{P_R}{P_T} &= \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{c}{4\pi f d}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

In decibel form (dB), using $\lambda = c/f$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL}_{dB} &= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_T}{P_R}\right) \\[4pt]
                &= 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For $d$ in **kilometres** and $f$ in **MHz**, the constant $20\log_{10}(4\pi/c)$ evaluates numerically to approximately **32.44** dB (with $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s). Hence the **practitioner's formula**:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{FSPL}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44\;}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style):**

> A smart-object sensor transmits at $f = 2.4$ GHz with $P_{TX} = 0$ dBm. The receiver sensitivity is $-92$ dBm. Antenna gains are $G_T = G_R = 2$ dBi. Miscellaneous losses $L_{\text{misc}} = 4$ dB. Find the maximum free-space range.

**Solution:**

Using the link budget equation:
$$
P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_T + G_R - \text{FSPL} - L_{\text{misc}}
$$

For the *just-receivable* link, $P_{RX} = -92$ dBm:
$$
-92 = 0 + 2 + 2 - \text{FSPL} - 4
$$

Solving:
$$
\begin{aligned}
-92 &= 0 - \text{FSPL} \\[4pt]
\text{FSPL} &= 92 \;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the FSPL formula with $f = 2400$ MHz:
$$
\begin{aligned}
92 &= 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(2400) + 32.44 \\[4pt]
92 &= 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 67.6 + 32.44 \\[4pt]
20\log_{10}(d_{km}) &= 92 - 100.04 = -8.04 \\[4pt]
\log_{10}(d_{km}) &= -0.402 \\[4pt]
d_{km} &= 10^{-0.402} \approx 0.396
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting: $d \approx \mathbf{396 \;m}$ of free-space range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Note:** Indoors with walls, $n \approx 3$, so the actual range drops to roughly $0.396^{(2/3)} \approx 0.54$ of the free-space value, giving **~210 m indoor range** — consistent with typical ZigBee marketing claims of "100 m outdoor, 30 m indoor."

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sensor Node Energy Consumption Model

A widely used first-order energy model (Heinzelman et al.) for a WSN node transmitting a $k$-bit packet over distance $d$:

$$
E_{TX}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + E_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}
$$

The receiver consumes:
$$
E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

**Parameter Values (Typical):**
- $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit (transmit electronics)
- $E_{amp} = 100$ pJ/bit/m² (free-space, $n = 2$)
- $E_{amp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴ (multipath, $n = 4$)

**Total Energy for Multi-Hop vs Direct (Derivation of Optimal Hop Count):**

If a node transmits a $k$-bit message over total distance $D$ via $h$ equal hops of $D/h$ each:

$$
E_{\text{total}}(h) = h \cdot \left[E_{elec}\cdot k + E_{amp}\cdot k \cdot \left(\frac{D}{h}\right)^{n}\right] + (h-1) \cdot E_{elec}\cdot k
$$

Ignoring the $(h-1)$ receive overhead (for large $h$), differentiating w.r.t. $h$ and setting to zero:
$$
\frac{d E_{\text{total}}}{dh} = E_{elec}\cdot k + E_{amp}\cdot k \cdot D^{n} \cdot \frac{d}{dh}\!\left(\frac{1}{h^{n-1}}\right) = 0
$$

Solving yields the optimal number of hops. For $n = 2$, this simply reduces to direct transmission being competitive, but for $n = 4$ (multipath indoor), multi-hop is **dramatically** more efficient.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Exam Cram)

| Criterion | Best-in-Class Protocol | Worst-in-Class for WSN | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Long Range | LoRaWAN (>10 km) | Bluetooth (~10 m) | Range vs Data Rate |
| Low Power | ZigBee / BLE | Wi-Fi | Power vs Throughput |
| High Data Rate | Wi-Fi (600+ Mbps) | LoRaWAN (50 kbps) | Throughput vs Range |
| Strong Security | NB-IoT (LTE-grade) | Passive RFID | Security vs Cost |
| Mesh Support | ZigBee, Thread | Bluetooth (only 5.0+) | Topology vs Simplicity |
| Lowest Cost | RFID tags | NB-IoT modules | Cost vs Capability |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram — Layered WSN Protocol Stack

```mermaid
graph TD
    APP["Application Layer - Sensor Data, Time Sync, Localization"]
    TRAN["Transport Layer - Reliability and Congestion Control"]
    NET["Network Layer - RPL AODV DSR Routing to Sink"]
    MAC["MAC Layer - CSMA-CA TDMA Duty Cycling"]
    PHY["Physical Layer - IEEE 802.15.4 2.4 GHz O-QPSK Modulation"]
    RADIO["Radio Hardware - Antenna and Transceiver"]

    APP --> TRAN
    TRAN --> NET
    NET --> MAC
    MAC --> PHY
    PHY --> RADIO

    N1["Cross-layer optimization: Energy aware MAC"]
    MAC -.-> N1
    N1 -.-> NET
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each block represents a layer of the communication stack. The dotted line labelled "Cross-layer optimization" reflects a key WSN innovation — traditional layered isolation is broken to allow MAC and Network layers to share energy information, extending battery life.

---

### 4.2 Diagram — Smart Object Connection Topologies

```mermaid
graph TD
    SINK["Sink or Gateway Node"]
    STAR1["Sensor Node 1"]
    STAR2["Sensor Node 2"]
    STAR3["Sensor Node 3"]
    STAR4["Sensor Node 4"]
    MESH1["Mesh Node A"]
    MESH2["Mesh Node B"]
    MESH3["Mesh Node C"]
    MESH4["Mesh Node D"]
    MESH5["Mesh Node E"]
    CLOUD["Internet Cloud IoT Platform"]

    STAR1 --> SINK
    STAR2 --> SINK
    STAR3 --> SINK
    STAR4 --> SINK

    MESH1 <--> MESH2
    MESH2 <--> MESH3
    MESH3 <--> MESH4
    MESH4 <--> MESH5
    MESH5 <--> MESH1
    MESH1 --> SINK
    MESH3 --> SINK

    SINK --> CLOUD
```

**Two Topologies Shown:**
- **Star Topology** (top half): Each sensor talks directly to the sink — simple but range-limited.
- **Mesh Topology** (bottom half): Sensors relay through each other — robust, self-healing, supports multi-hop communication.

---

### 4.3 Diagram — Communication Criteria Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{"What is the deployment distance?"}
    Q2{"What is the data per sample?"}
    Q3{"What is the battery availability?"}
    Q4{"Is mesh required?"}
    R1["Use LoRaWAN or NB-IoT"]
    R2["Use ZigBee or BLE or Wi-Fi"]
    R3["Use RFID or NFC"]
    R4["Use BLE or ZigBee"]
    R5["Use Wi-Fi or NB-IoT"]
    R6["Use ZigBee Mesh or Thread"]

    Q1 -->|More than 1 km| R1
    Q1 -->|10 to 100 m| Q2
    Q1 -->|Less than 1 m| R3
    Q2 -->|Small below 100 bytes| Q3
    Q2 -->|Large video audio| R5
    Q3 -->|Coin cell months| R4
    Q3 -->|Mains powered| R5
    Q4 -->|Yes| R6
    Q4 -->|No| R2
```

---

### 4.4 Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Smart Object

```mermaid
graph LR
    SENS["Sensors Temperature Humidity Motion"]
    ADC["ADC and Signal Conditioning"]
    MCU["Microcontroller Local Processing"]
    MEM["Memory Buffer"]
    RADIO["Radio Transceiver IEEE 802.15.4"]
    ANT["Antenna 2.4 GHz"]
    BAT["Battery or Energy Harvester"]
    PMU["Power Management Unit"]

    SENS --> ADC
    ADC --> MCU
    MCU --> MEM
    MCU --> RADIO
    RADIO --> ANT
    BAT --> PMU
    PMU --> MCU
    PMU --> RADIO
    PMU --> SENS
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Architecture:** This block diagram illustrates the *functional anatomy* of a smart object sensor node. Each block has a defined role: **Sensors** collect analog phenomena, **ADC** digitizes them, **MCU** runs embedded logic (e.g., threshold checks), **Memory** buffers packets, **Radio + Antenna** transmit wirelessly, and the **Power Management Unit (PMU)** orchestrates energy distribution from the battery or harvester. This is a KTU-favourite structural diagram for full-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Rules (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks
> - **Part B:** Answer 1 out of 2 questions × 14 marks = 14 marks
> - **Total Module Weightage:** 20 marks per ESE module question

---

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any THREE communication criteria used for selecting an IoT/Wireless protocol, with one example protocol for each criterion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Three communication criteria for IoT/Wireless protocol selection are:
> 1. **Range** — Maximum distance for reliable communication. Example: **LoRaWAN** offers 2–15 km, ideal for long-range agriculture.
> 2. **Power Consumption** — Energy required by the smart object. Example: **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** consumes only ~15 mW average, suitable for wearables.
> 3. **Security** — Encryption and authentication strength. Example: **NB-IoT** uses LTE-grade 128-bit encryption for smart metering.
>
> *(Award 1 mark per correctly stated criterion + matching example. Total: 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define a Smart Object. Mention the FOUR mandatory capabilities it must possess to be considered "smart."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Smart Object** is a physical or virtual entity embedded with sensing, processing, and communication capabilities, allowing it to interact with the environment and other entities in an IoT ecosystem.
>
> Four mandatory capabilities are:
> 1. **Unique Identifier** — An addressable identity (e.g., IPv6 address, UUID, EPC).
> 2. **Sensing / Actuation** — Ability to measure physical phenomena or perform an action.
> 3. **Local Data Processing** — Embedded microcontroller to compute, filter, or aggregate data.
> 4. **Communication Interface** — Wireless or wired transceiver to exchange data with peers.
>
> *(Award 1 mark for definition + 0.5 per capability. Total: 3 marks.)*

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — WSN Protocol Deep Dive**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **IEEE 802.15.4** standard in detail. Discuss its physical layer specifications, MAC layer features, and explain why it forms the foundation for many WSN protocols such as ZigBee and 6LoWPAN.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the **Star and Mesh topologies** used in Wireless Sensor Networks. Compare them on the basis of range, energy efficiency, fault tolerance, and scalability.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) IEEE 802.15.4 — Detailed Explanation (7 marks):**

> IEEE 802.15.4 is the cornerstone standard for **Low-Rate Wireless Personal Area Networks (LR-WPAN)**, designed specifically for low-power, low-data-rate smart-object communication.
>
> **Physical Layer Specifications:**
> - Operates in three ISM bands: **868 MHz (Europe)**, **915 MHz (USA)**, and **2.4 GHz (global)**.
> - In the **2.4 GHz** band, it uses **O-QPSK modulation** with a chip rate of 2 Mchips/s, achieving a raw data rate of **250 kbps** across 16 channels.
> - Maximum transmit power typically **0 dBm (1 mW)**.
>
> **MAC Layer Features:**
> - Employs **CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)** for channel access.
> - Supports **beacon-enabled** mode (slotted CSMA) and **non-beacon** mode (unslotted).
> - Provides **acknowledged** and **unacknowledged** frame delivery.
> - Uses 16-bit short addresses and 64-bit extended addresses.
>
> **Foundation for ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, Thread, WirelessHART:**
> IEEE 802.15.4 only defines PHY and MAC. Higher-layer protocols such as ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, Thread, and WirelessHART add networking, security, and application semantics on top of this foundation. This separation allows IoT designers to mix-and-match protocols while keeping the radio hardware identical.
>
> *(Allocation: 2 marks PHY + 2 marks MAC + 3 marks foundation explanation.)*

**(b) Star vs Mesh Topology (7 marks):**

> **Star Topology:** Every sensor node communicates **directly** with a central coordinator (sink). Simpler routing, lower latency, but limited range (each node must reach the sink) and single-point-of-failure at the coordinator.
>
> **Mesh Topology:** Each node can communicate with multiple neighbours and act as a relay. Multi-hop routing extends range dramatically; the network is self-healing because packets can reroute around failed nodes.
>
> **Comparison Table:**
>
> | Parameter | Star | Mesh |
> |---|---|---|
> | Range | Short, single-hop | Extended, multi-hop |
> | Energy Efficiency | High for close nodes | Better for distant nodes |
> | Fault Tolerance | Low (single sink) | High (self-healing) |
> | Scalability | Limited by hub capacity | Excellent |
> | Latency | Low | Higher (multi-hop) |
>
> *(Allocation: 2 marks diagram + 1 mark each for the 4 criteria = 7 marks.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Communication Criteria & Quantitative Analysis**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **six communication criteria** (range, bandwidth, power, security, topology, scalability) used to evaluate IoT/WSN protocols. Provide one example protocol that excels in each criterion.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] A WSN node transmits at $P_{TX} = 10$ dBm at a frequency of 2.4 GHz. The receiver sensitivity is $-85$ dBm. Antenna gains are $G_T = G_R = 1.5$ dBi and miscellaneous losses total 3 dB. Calculate the **maximum free-space communication range** using the FSPL formula.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Six Communication Criteria (7 marks):**

> 1. **Range** — Maximum distance for reliable communication. *Best Example:* **LoRaWAN** offers 2–15 km in rural deployments.
> 2. **Bandwidth / Data Rate** — Bits per second the link supports. *Best Example:* **Wi-Fi (802.11ac)** offers up to 1.3 Gbps.
> 3. **Power Consumption** — Energy use of the radio. *Best Example:* **Bluetooth Low Energy** consumes ~1 µA in sleep mode.
> 4. **Security** — Encryption, authentication, integrity. *Best Example:* **NB-IoT** uses 128-bit LTE encryption.
> 5. **Topology Support** — Network architecture capability. *Best Example:* **ZigBee** natively supports mesh.
> 6. **Scalability & Cost** — Cheap addition of many nodes. *Best Example:* **Passive RFID** tags cost <₹10 each, billions deployed.
>
> *(Award 1 mark each for criterion + example + 1 mark for introduction/conclusion = 7 marks.)*

**(b) Range Calculation (7 marks):**

> Given:
> - $P_{TX} = 10$ dBm
> - Receiver sensitivity = $-85$ dBm (i.e., minimum required $P_{RX}$)
> - $G_T = G_R = 1.5$ dBi
> - $L_{\text{misc}} = 3$ dB
> - $f = 2400$ MHz
>
> **Step 1 — Link Budget Equation:**
> $$
> P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_T + G_R - \text{FSPL} - L_{\text{misc}}
> $$
>
> **Step 2 — Substitute known values:**
> $$
> -85 = 10 + 1.5 + 1.5 - \text{FSPL} - 3
> $$
>
> **Step 3 — Solve for FSPL:**
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> -85 &= 10 - \text{FSPL} \\
> \text{FSPL} &= 95 \;\text{dB}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 4 — Apply FSPL formula:**
> $$
> \text{FSPL}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44
> $$
> $$
> 95 = 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(2400) + 32.44
> $$
> $$
> 95 = 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 67.6 + 32.44
> $$
> $$
> 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) = 95 - 100.04 = -5.04
> $$
> $$
> d_{km} = 10^{-5.04/20} = 10^{-0.252} \approx 0.559
> $$
>
> **Step 5 — Final Answer:**
> $$
> \boxed{d \approx 0.559 \;\text{km} \approx 559 \;\text{metres}}
> $$
>
> *(Allocation: 1 mark link budget setup + 1 mark FSPL value + 2 marks substitution + 1 mark algebra + 1 mark final answer + 1 mark unit conversion = 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert dBm values:** Many students write $P_{TX} = 10$ dBm directly in mW without remembering that dBm is already logarithmic. The dB framework is *already* applied in the link budget equation — do **not** convert dBm back to linear.
> 2. **Using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\log_2$ in Shannon's formula:** Shannon's formula uses $\log_2$ for capacity in **bits**. If you accidentally use $\log_{10}$, your answer will be off by ~3.32× — and you will lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up "range" with "coverage":** Range is the maximum distance from a node to the sink; coverage is the *area* (proportional to $d^2$) — examiners penalize confusion.
> 4. **Omitting units in the FSPL formula:** The constant 32.44 is valid **only** when $d$ is in km and $f$ is in MHz. Wrong units = wrong answer = **zero marks** for that step.
> 5. **Defining "Smart Object" without all four capabilities:** KTU strict markers award 1 mark only for a complete definition with all four traits (ID, sensing, processing, communication).
> 6. **Drawing topologies without labels:** A mesh diagram without explicit "Node" labels loses 1 mark.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **WSN** = distributed autonomous sensor nodes + wireless protocol + sink/gateway.
- **Smart Object** must have **unique ID + sensing/actuation + local processing + communication interface**.
- The **six communication criteria** are **range, bandwidth, power, security, topology, scalability/cost**.
- **IEEE 802.15.4** is the PHY/MAC foundation of most WSN protocols (ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, Thread).
- **Shannon Capacity** $C = B\log_2(1+\text{SNR})$ sets the theoretical maximum rate; no real protocol exceeds it.
- **FSPL formula** (dB): $\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44$.
- **Path loss exponent** $n = 2$ (free space), $n = 3$ (urban), $n = 4$ (dense multipath indoor).
- **Energy model:** $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec}\cdot k + E_{amp}\cdot k \cdot d^{n}$.
- **Multi-hop is more energy-efficient** for $n = 4$ (typical indoor WSN).
- **Star topology** = simple, short range; **Mesh topology** = robust, long range, self-healing.
- **Best protocol per criterion:** LoRaWAN (range), Wi-Fi (bandwidth), BLE (power), NB-IoT (security), ZigBee (mesh), RFID (cost/scalability).
- Always label units: **dBm** for power, **dBi** for antenna gain, **dB** for path loss, **Hz/bps** for frequency/rate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unified Data Standards - Protocols - IEEE 802.15.4 - The Physical Layer
The Media-Access Control Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT755 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Media-Access Control Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **IoT and Applications**, the **Media Access Control (MAC) Layer** is the lower sub-layer of the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the OSI / TCP-IP reference model. It is responsible for **framing, addressing, channel access control, and reliable delivery of data over a shared physical medium**. In IoT systems, the MAC layer governs *how thousands of energy-constrained, low-power devices* (sensors, actuators, RFID tags) arbitrate their access to the wireless or wired channel *without destructively colliding*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The MAC sub-layer protocols of the **IEEE 802.15.4** standard, **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** MAC protocols (S-MAC, B-MAC, X-MAC, ContikiMAC), and **IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi MAC** form the high-yield core. Board questions are frequently asked from the **CSMA/CA mechanism, frame structure, duty cycling, and energy-efficiency trade-offs**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Polite Round-Table Conversation"

Imagine **50 friends sitting around a single dinner table** trying to speak at the same time. If everyone talks simultaneously, no message reaches anyone (collision → garbled noise). A **MAC protocol** is like the *unwritten rule of turn-taking*:

- **FDMA** → Each person gets a *different color of glass* and can only speak when holding it (different frequency).
- **TDMA** → A strict *time-slot rota* is maintained: Person 1 speaks 8:00-8:01, Person 2 at 8:01-8:02...
- **CSMA/CA** → Before speaking, each person *listens* to see if the table is silent. If silent, they speak; otherwise, they wait a random time and try again.

This rule-set is exactly what the MAC layer encodes in hardware/firmware on every IoT mote.

### 1.3 Position of MAC Layer in the IoT Protocol Stack

The MAC layer sits between the **Physical Layer (PHY)** below it and the **Logical Link Control (LLC)** above it. For IoT, the relevant mapping is:

$$
\text{Application Layer (CoAP / MQTT)} \rightarrow \text{Network (6LoWPAN / RPL)} \rightarrow \text{LLC} \rightarrow \boxed{\textbf{MAC Layer}} \rightarrow \text{PHY (Radio)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric to Memorise:** The **IEEE 802.15.4 MAC** operates in the **2.4 GHz** industrial-scientific-medical (ISM) band, supporting a raw over-the-air data rate of **250 kbps** with **16 channels** (channels 11–26, each 2 MHz wide).

### 1.4 Primary Responsibilities of the MAC Layer

1. **Channel Access Control** — Deciding *when* a node may transmit.
2. **Addressing** — 64-bit IEEE EUI-64 or 16-bit short MAC address.
3. **Frame Construction / Deconstruction** — Building MAC Protocol Data Units (MPDUs).
4. **Collision Avoidance** — Through CSMA/CA backoff algorithms.
5. **Acknowledgement & Retransmission** — ARQ (Automatic Repeat reQuest).
6. **Power Management** — Beacon-enabled superframe, duty cycling.
7. **Network Synchronization** — Beacons for time-sync.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Multiple Access Techniques — The Heart of MAC

When a single shared medium (the radio channel) must serve many nodes, multiple-access schemes are required. These are classified as **Fixed Assignment**, **Random Access**, and **Demand Assignment**.

#### A. Fixed Assignment (Collision-Free, Deterministic)

| Technique | Full Form | Operating Principle | IoT Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FDMA** | Frequency Division Multiple Access | Each node gets a unique frequency band | Satellite IoT (SigFox) |
| **TDMA** | Time Division Multiple Access | Each node gets a unique time slot | 4G LTE-M, NB-IoT |
| **CDMA** | Code Division Multiple Access | Each node uses a unique orthogonal code | 3G cellular IoT |
| **OFDMA** | Orthogonal FDMA | Sub-carrier allocation in OFDM grid | 5G mMTC, Wi-Fi 6 |

#### B. Random Access (Contention-Based, Probabilistic)

- **ALOHA** — Transmit whenever ready; vulnerable up to ~18 % efficiency.
- **Slotted ALOHA** — Synchronized slots; double the efficiency to ~36 %.
- **CSMA** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access) — Listen before transmit.
- **CSMA/CD** — Collision *Detection* (wired Ethernet).
- **CSMA/CA** — Collision *Avoidance* (wireless — the IoT workhorse).

#### C. Hybrid Schemes (KTU Favourite)

- **Hybrid TDMA/CSMA** — Combine deterministic slots with contention slots.
- **CSMA/CA + Slotted Aloha** — Used in IEEE 802.15.4 beacon-enabled mode.

### 2.2 MAC Layer Metrics & Formulas (KTU Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Following Formula Table Is Examination-Critical. Memorise the Boundary Conditions.**

| # | Metric / Parameter | Formula | Definition of Symbols | Typical Value (IoT) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Throughput (S)** of Pure ALOHA | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | $G$ = offered load (packets/slot) | Max $S = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$ at $G=0.5$ |
| 2 | **Throughput (S)** of Slotted ALOHA | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ | $G$ = offered load | Max $S = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ at $G=1$ |
| 3 | **CSMA/CA Backoff Counter** | $T_{\text{backoff}} = \text{rand} \cdot a_{\text{unit}}$ | $a_{\text{unit}} = 320 \mu s$ | 0 to $2^{BE}-1$ slots |
| 4 | **Duty Cycle (DC)** | $DC = \frac{T_{\text{active}}}{T_{\text{active}} + T_{\text{sleep}}}$ | $T_{\text{active}}$ = radio ON time | $1\%$ for WSN |
| 5 | **Average Energy per Bit** | $E_b = \frac{P_{\text{tx}} \cdot t_{\text{tx}} + P_{\text{rx}} \cdot t_{\text{rx}} + P_{\text{sleep}} \cdot t_{\text{sleep}}}{N_b}$ | $N_b$ = bits transmitted | $\approx 50 \mu J$ / bit |
| 6 | **Maximum Theoretical Capacity (Shannon)** | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$ | $B$ = bandwidth (Hz) | Bounded upper limit |
| 7 | **Energy per Useful Bit** | $E_u = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{L(1-P_e)}$ | $P_e$ = packet error rate | Used in lifetime models |
| 8 | **Network Lifetime** | $L_{\text{net}} = \frac{E_{\text{initial}}}{E_{\text{per-round}}}$ | Energy budget / per-round drain | 1–5 years (WSN) |
| 9 | **Collision Probability (CSMA)** | $P_{\text{coll}} = 1 - (1-p)^{n-1}$ | $n$ = nodes, $p$ = transmit prob | High for dense IoT |
| 10 | **Frame Transmission Time** | $t_{\text{frame}} = \frac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = payload bits, $R$ = data rate | IEEE 802.15.4 superframe bounded |

> **Pitfall Note for Students:** Do not confuse $S$ (throughput) with $G$ (offered load). Throughput is the *successful* delivery; offered load includes both successful and collided packets.

### 2.3 Why MAC Matters for IoT — The Engineering Reality

IoT devices are typically:
- **Battery-powered** (often coin-cell, e.g., CR2032 = 220 mAh / 3 V).
- **Sleepy** — They spend 99 % of their time in sleep mode.
- **Dense** — A smart factory may deploy **10 000+ nodes** in a few hundred m².
- **Lossy** — Wireless channels in 2.4 GHz suffer from multi-path fading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Therefore, the **single most important design goal of an IoT MAC protocol is ENERGY EFFICIENCY**, not raw throughput. This is why protocols like **B-MAC, X-MAC, and ContikiMAC** were designed with aggressive duty-cycling as the primary optimization target.

### 2.4 Classification of IoT MAC Protocols (Hierarchical Logic)

**Step 1 — Schedule-based (Synchronous)**
- TDMA-like
- Nodes share a common clock
- Examples: **S-MAC, T-MAC, DSMAC**
- Energy efficient; sync overhead

**Step 2 — Contention-based (Asynchronous)**
- CSMA-based, no global sync
- Examples: **B-MAC, X-MAC, ContikiMAC**
- Robust, scalable, lower latency

**Step 3 — Hybrid**
- Mix of both; example: **Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4 beacon-enabled mode)**

**Step 4 — Cross-layer / Receiver-initiated**
- Examples: **RI-MAC, PW-MAC**

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IEEE 802.15.4 MAC** → Foundation of **Zigbee, Thread, 6LoWPAN, WirelessHART, ISA100.11a** — used in smart meters, industrial automation.
- **IEEE 802.11 MAC (DCF / PCF)** → Wi-Fi for smart-home gateways.
- **LoRaWAN MAC** → Uses pure **ALOHA** with duty-cycle regulation (EU 868 MHz: 1 % duty cycle).
- **NB-IoT MAC** → Scheduled access on LTE, optimised for ultra-low data rate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Pure ALOHA Throughput (Full Algebraic Working)

**Step 1 — Define the offered load.**

Let $G$ be the average number of transmission attempts (success + collisions) per packet time $T$. The probability that exactly $k$ packets are generated in time $T$ follows a Poisson distribution:

$$
P(k) = \frac{G^k e^{-G}}{k!}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the vulnerability window.**

In Pure ALOHA, a packet transmitted at time $t_0$ will collide with **any other packet that starts in the interval $(t_0 - T, t_0 + T)$**. This is the vulnerable interval of length $2T$.

**Step 3 — Apply Poisson statistics over the vulnerable window.**

For no collision, the number of other packets generated in $2T$ must be zero. Let the offered load over $2T$ be $2G$. Then:

$$
P(\text{no collision}) = \frac{(2G)^0 e^{-2G}}{0!} = e^{-2G}
$$

**Step 4 — Relate throughput to successful transmissions.**

Throughput $S$ is the rate of successful packets per packet time. Since the average rate of total attempts is $G$ and the probability of success per attempt is $e^{-2G}$:

$$
\boxed{S = G \cdot e^{-2G}}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximise the throughput.**

Differentiate $S$ with respect to $G$ and set to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dS}{dG} &= e^{-2G} + G \cdot (-2)e^{-2G} = 0 \\
e^{-2G} (1 - 2G) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $e^{-2G} \neq 0$, the optimum is $1 - 2G = 0 \Rightarrow G = 0.5$.

**Step 6 — Substitute back to obtain the maximum throughput.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= 0.5 \cdot e^{-2 \cdot 0.5} \\
&= 0.5 \cdot e^{-1} \\
&= \frac{0.5}{e} \\
&= \frac{1}{2e} \\
&\approx 0.1839
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Pure ALOHA can deliver at most **~18.4 %** channel utilisation. This is the reason pure ALOHA is **almost never used in modern IoT** but is **conceptually fundamental**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Slotted ALOHA Throughput

In slotted ALOHA, transmission may only begin at the start of a discrete time slot of duration $T$. The vulnerable window shrinks from $2T$ to exactly $T$ (only one other transmission in the same slot causes collision).

$$
\boxed{S = G \cdot e^{-G}}
$$

Maximising:

$$
\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G}(1 - G) = 0 \Rightarrow G = 1
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{S_{\max} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU-Boards Favourite)

| Property | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA | CSMA | CSMA/CA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Synchronization | None | Required | None | Optional |
| Max Throughput | $\frac{1}{2e} \approx 18.4\%$ | $\frac{1}{e} \approx 36.8\%$ | Up to $\sim 80\%$ | Up to $\sim 90\%$ |
| Collision Handling | No detection | No detection | Detection (wired) | Avoidance (wireless) |
| Vulnerable Window | $2T$ | $T$ | $\approx$ Propagation Delay | RTS/CTS handshaking |
| IoT Suitability | LoRaWAN (license-free) | Poor | Not for WSN | **IEEE 802.15.4** |
| Energy Cost | Low (no sync) | Low | Medium | Higher (CCA + ACKs) |

### 3.4 Analytical Derivation — CSMA/CA Backoff in IEEE 802.15.4

The IEEE 802.15.4 MAC performs **Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance (CSMA/CA)** with binary exponential backoff.

**Step 1 — Initialisation.**

$$
NB = 0, \quad CW = 2, \quad BE = \text{macMinBE} = 3
$$

Where:
- $NB$ = Number of Backoffs (retries)
- $CW$ = Contention Window length (backoff periods)
- $BE$ = Backoff Exponent

**Step 2 — Random backoff delay.**

$$
\text{Backoff} = \text{rand}(0, 2^{BE} - 1) \text{ unit backoff periods}
$$

where 1 unit backoff period $= 20$ symbols $= 320 \mu s$.

**Step 3 — Clear Channel Assessment (CCA).**

After the backoff, the node samples the channel. If channel is **IDLE for CW consecutive times**, the packet is transmitted. Otherwise, it backs off again with:

$$
NB \leftarrow NB + 1, \quad BE \leftarrow \min(BE + 1, \text{macMaxBE} = 5)
$$

**Step 4 — Failure threshold.**

If $NB > \text{macMaxCSMABackoffs} = 4$, the packet is dropped and an error reported to the upper layer.

This analysis is the **most-marks-yielding derivation** in KTU board examinations on this topic.

### 3.5 Derivation of Energy Efficiency of Duty-Cycled MAC

Let the radio draw currents $I_{tx}$, $I_{rx}$, $I_{sleep}$ for time fractions $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$ (where $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$). The average current is:

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = \alpha I_{tx} + \beta I_{rx} + \gamma I_{\text{sleep}}
$$

Typical CC2420 values (TelosB mote): $I_{tx} = 17.4$ mA, $I_{rx} = 19.7$ mA, $I_{sleep} = 1 \mu A$.

For a $1\%$ duty cycle ($\alpha + \beta = 0.01$, $\gamma = 0.99$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{avg}} &\approx 0.005 \cdot 17.4 + 0.005 \cdot 19.7 + 0.99 \cdot 0.001 \\
&\approx 0.087 + 0.0985 + 0.00099 \\
&\approx 0.186 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **quantitative justification** for the B-MAC / ContikiMAC design philosophy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the MAC Layer in IoT

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP["Application Layer CoAP / MQTT"] --> NET["Network Layer 6LoWPAN RPL"]
    NET --> LLC["Logical Link Control LLC Sublayer"]
    LLC --> MAC["MAC Sublayer 802.15.4 CSMA CA"]
    MAC --> PHY["Physical Layer 2.4 GHz Radio"]

    subgraph MACDETAIL["MAC Sublayer Internal Blocks"]
        direction TB
        FRM["Frame Construction MPDU"] --> ADR["Addressing EUI 64 16 bit"]
        ADR --> ACC["Channel Access CSMA CA Backoff"]
        ACC --> ACK["ACK and Retransmit ARQ"]
        ACK --> PWR["Power Management Beacon Duty Cycle"]
        PWR --> SYN["Beacon Synchronization"]
    end

    MAC -. governs .-> MACDETAIL
    PHY -. 250 kbps .-> PHYOUT["Air Interface 16 Channels"]

    style MACDETAIL fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#1f77b4,stroke-width:2px
    style MAC fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d4a017,stroke-width:3px
    style PHYOUT fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow of CSMA/CA Algorithm in IEEE 802.15.4

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start New Packet at MAC Layer"] --> B["Init NB 0, CW 2, BE 3"]
    B --> C["Compute Random Backoff rand 0 2 to BE minus 1"]
    C --> D["Wait Random Backoff Periods"]
    D --> E{"Perform CCA Channel Idle?"}
    E -- Yes --> F{"CCA Idle Count Equals CW?"}
    E -- No --> G["Increment NB and BE"]
    G --> H{"NB greater than macMaxCSMABackoffs 4?"}
    H -- Yes --> Z["Drop Packet Report Error"]
    H -- No --> C
    F -- Yes --> Y["Transmit Packet and Wait for ACK"]
    F -- No --> E
    Y --> K{"ACK Received?"}
    K -- Yes --> END["Success"]
    K -- No --> G

    style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style Z fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style END fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style Y fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
```

### 4.3 Taxonomy of MAC Protocols Used in IoT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ROOT["IoT MAC Protocols"]

    ROOT --> SCHED["Schedule Based Synchronous"]
    ROOT --> CONT["Contention Based Asynchronous"]
    ROOT --> HYB["Hybrid"]
    ROOT --> RXI["Receiver Initiated"]

    SCHED --> SMAC["S MAC"]
    SCHED --> TMAC["T MAC"]
    SCHED --> DSMAC["DS MAC"]
    SCHED --> TSCH["IEEE 802.15.4e TSCH"]

    CONT --> BMAC["B MAC"]
    CONT --> XMAC["X MAC"]
    CONT --> CKMAC["ContikiMAC"]
    CONT --> ALOHA["Pure and Slotted ALOHA"]

    HYB --> ZB["Zigbee Beacon Mode"]
    HYB --> LORA["LoRaWAN"]

    RXI --> RIMAC["RI MAC"]
    RXI --> PWMAC["PW MAC"]

    style ROOT fill:#0d6efd,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style SCHED fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style CONT fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style HYB fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style RXI fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
```

### 4.4 IEEE 802.15.4 MAC Superframe Structure (Beacon-Enabled Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SF["Beacon Interval BI = 15.36 ms to 251.6 s"] --> CAP["Contention Access Period CAP"]
    SF --> CFP["Contention Free Period CFP"]
    SF --> INACT["Inactive Period Sleep Mode"]

    CAP --> CSMA["Slotted CSMA CA"]
    CFP --> GTS1["GTS Slot 1"]
    CFP --> GTS2["GTS Slot 2"]
    CFP --> GTS3["GTS Slot n"]

    style SF fill:#0d6efd,color:#fff
    style CAP fill:#fff3cd
    style CFP fill:#d1e7dd
    style INACT fill:#e2e3e5
    style CSMA fill:#ffe69c
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** In beacon-enabled mode, the PAN coordinator broadcasts a **beacon** at the start of each **Beacon Interval (BI)**. The **Contention Access Period (CAP)** uses slotted CSMA/CA. The **Contention Free Period (CFP)** contains up to **7 Guaranteed Time Slots (GTS)** reserved for time-critical nodes. The **Inactive Period** is where all non-GTS nodes enter deep sleep — the principal source of energy savings.

### 4.5 IEEE 802.15.4 MAC Frame (MPDU) Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FR["MAC Frame MPDU"]

    FR --> FCTRL["Frame Control 2 bytes"]
    FR --> FSEQ["Sequence Number 1 byte"]
    FR --> ADDR["Addressing Fields 4 to 20 bytes"]
    FR --> AUX["Aux Security Header 0 to 14 bytes"]
    FR --> PAY["Payload 0 to 101 bytes"]
    FR --> FCS["Frame Check Sequence FCS 2 bytes CRC"]

    FCTRL --> FTYPE["Frame Type Beacon Data ACK Command"]
    FCTRL --> SEC["Security Enabled Flag"]
    FCTRL --> ACKREQ["ACK Request Flag"]
    FCTRL --> PANCOMP["PAN ID Compression"]
    FCTRL --> DAMODE["Dest Addressing Mode"]
    FCTRL --> SAMODE["Source Addressing Mode"]

    style FR fill:#0d6efd,color:#fff
    style FCTRL fill:#fff3cd
    style PAY fill:#d1e7dd
    style FCS fill:#f8d7da
```

### 4.6 Energy vs Throughput Trade-off in MAC Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TR["MAC Protocol Design Objective"] --> E1["Maximize Energy Efficiency"]
    TR --> E2["Maximize Throughput"]
    TR --> E3["Minimize Latency"]
    TR --> E4["Maximize Fairness"]

    E1 --> ECHOICE{"Radio ON Time"}
    ECHOICE -- High --> LOWPWR["Low duty cycle MAC B MAC ContikiMAC"]
    ECHOICE -- Low --> HIGHPWR["Always on MAC IEEE 802.11 DCF"]

    E2 --> LOWLAT["Low MAC overhead Contention based"]
    E3 --> REAL["Real time GTS in 802.15.4"]
    E4 --> TDMA2["TDMA scheduling"]

    style TR fill:#0d6efd,color:#fff
    style LOWPWR fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:3px
    style HIGHPWR fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Define the MAC sublayer. List any two responsibilities of the MAC layer in an IoT protocol stack.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Media Access Control (MAC) sublayer** is the lower sublayer of the **Data Link Layer** of the OSI model, responsible for controlling how multiple nodes share a common transmission medium.

**Two responsibilities (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Channel access control** — arbitrates who transmits when using protocols like CSMA/CA or TDMA.
2. **Frame construction and addressing** — builds MAC Protocol Data Units (MPDUs) with 64-bit EUI-64 or 16-bit short addresses.
3. **Collision avoidance / detection** — manages backoff and retransmission for reliability.
4. **Power management** — coordinates beacon-enabled duty cycling for energy savings.

*(Mark allocation: 1 mark definition + 1.5 marks for two responsibilities = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA in terms of vulnerable window and maximum throughput.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA |
|---|---|---|
| Time axis | Continuous | Discrete slots |
| Synchronization | Not required | Mandatory |
| Vulnerable window | $2T$ | $T$ |
| Throughput formula | $S = G e^{-2G}$ | $S = G e^{-G}$ |
| Maximum throughput | $\frac{1}{2e} \approx 18.4\%$ | $\frac{1}{e} \approx 36.8\%$ |
| Optimal offered load $G$ | 0.5 | 1.0 |

*(Mark allocation: 1 mark vulnerability window + 1 mark formula + 1 mark numerical max throughput = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulation**, the candidate answers **ONE full question of 14 marks** per module. The model questions below provide two alternatives; examiners choose one.

---

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]

**(a)** Explain the architecture and frame format of the **IEEE 802.15.4 MAC sublayer** in detail. Discuss the **CSMA/CA algorithm** with binary exponential backoff. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **superframe structure** of IEEE 802.15.4 in beacon-enabled mode. Discuss the role of GTS in supporting time-critical IoT traffic. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Q(a) (7 marks)

**Architecture (3 marks):** The IEEE 802.15.4 MAC layer provides channel access via **CSMA/CA** in the 2.4 GHz ISM band. It supports both **beacon-enabled** (slotted) and **non-beacon** (unslotted) modes. It serves devices in two roles: **Full Function Device (FFD)** and **Reduced Function Device (RFD)**. A PAN coordinator manages the network.

**Frame Format (2 marks):** The MAC Protocol Data Unit (MPDU) contains the **Frame Control (2 B)**, **Sequence Number (1 B)**, **Addressing Fields (4–20 B)**, **Aux Security Header (0–14 B)**, **Payload (0–101 B)**, and **Frame Check Sequence / CRC (2 B)**. Frame Control fields include Frame Type (Beacon/Data/ACK/Command), Security, ACK Request, PAN ID compression, and Addressing modes.

**CSMA/CA with Binary Exponential Backoff (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.4:

- Initialise $NB = 0$, $CW = 2$, $BE = 3$.
- Random backoff in $[0, 2^{BE}-1]$ slots.
- Two CCAs; if both idle, transmit.
- If busy, increment $NB$, increase $BE \leq 5$, retry.
- After 4 failures, drop the packet.

#### Model Answer — Q(b) (7 marks)

**Superframe Structure (4 marks):** The superframe is bounded by **beacons** sent by the PAN coordinator. It comprises:

- **Active Period**: divided into **CAP** (16 slots min, slotted CSMA/CA) and **CFP** (up to 7 GTS slots).
- **Inactive Period**: nodes enter sleep.

**Beacon Interval** $BI = aBaseSuperframeDuration \cdot 2^{BO}$, where $BO$ = Beacon Order (0–14).
**Superframe Duration** $SD = aBaseSuperframeDuration \cdot 2^{SO}$, where $SO$ = Superframe Order (0–14).

**GTS Mechanism (3 marks):** A **Guaranteed Time Slot** is reserved via GTS request command from a device to the PAN coordinator. Up to 7 GTS slots can be allocated (one must remain for CAP). They occupy contiguous time slots at the end of the superframe, providing **bounded latency** for real-time control loops in industrial IoT.

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]

**(a)** Compare and contrast **S-MAC, B-MAC, and ContikiMAC** protocols. Explain the design rationale for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the maximum throughput of **Slotted ALOHA** and explain why CSMA/CA outperforms both ALOHA variants in dense IoT deployments. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Q(a) (7 marks)

**S-MAC (Sensor-MAC) — 2.5 marks:**
- **Synchronous, schedule-based**, uses a **virtual cluster** concept.
- Nodes exchange SYNC packets to align sleep/wake schedules.
- Fixed duty cycle (e.g., 10 %).
- Pros: low energy, predictable. Cons: clock drift, sync overhead, scalability issues.

**B-MAC (Berkeley-MAC) — 2 marks:**
- **Asynchronous, contention-based**, uses **Clear Channel Assessment (CCA)** and **Long Preambles**.
- Sender transmits a preamble longer than the receiver's sleep interval; receiver wakes, detects preamble, stays awake for the data packet.
- Pros: no sync, robust to drift. Cons: long preamble wastes energy at sender and overhears.

**ContikiMAC — 2.5 marks:**
- Successor of B-MAC in **ContikiOS**.
- Uses **phase-lock** optimisation; receiver wakes twice briefly per cycle to detect transmissions.
- Sender transmits fast bursts only at expected wake times.
- Achieves **< 1 % duty cycle**, the benchmark for IoT.
- Used commercially in **Zolertia, OpenMote** motes.

#### Model Answer — Q(b) (7 marks)

**Derivation of Slotted ALOHA (4 marks):** As derived in Section 3.2 — vulnerable window = $T$, $S = G e^{-G}$, $S_{\max} = 1/e \approx 36.8\%$ at $G = 1$.

**Why CSMA/CA outperforms ALOHA (3 marks):**
1. **Carrier sensing** prevents most collisions before they happen; ALOHA does not sense.
2. **Collision avoidance** via RTS/CTS handshake resolves the hidden-terminal problem.
3. **Binary exponential backoff** spreads retransmissions, reducing collision probability in dense deployments.
4. Throughput scales to **>80 %** under moderate load, vs. ALOHA's hard **~37 %** ceiling.

In dense IoT (1000+ nodes), CSMA/CA dominates. Pure ALOHA survives only in **low-density, low-duty applications** such as **LoRaWAN** due to regulatory duty-cycle constraints.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the Factor of 2 in ALOHA**: Pure ALOHA has vulnerable window $2T$, Slotted has $T$. Students often write the same formula for both — **lose 2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing CSMA/CD with CSMA/CA**: CD is for **wired Ethernet** (collision detection); CA is for **wireless** (collision avoidance). Mixing these up is a common, marks-losing error.
> 3. **Forgetting the units of the backoff period**: IEEE 802.15.4 unit backoff = **20 symbols = 320 µs**, not 1 ms.
> 4. **Skipping the frame structure diagram**: Always draw the MPDU layout — 2 marks reserved for diagram.
> 5. **Naming wrong duty cycle percentage**: B-MAC / ContikiMAC operate at **<1 %** duty cycle, not 10 %. S-MAC operates at ~10 %.
> 6. **Confusing EUI-64 with IPv6 address**: EUI-64 is a **MAC-layer hardware address**, not the same as IPv6 IID, although the IPv6 IID is often derived from EUI-64.
> 7. **Forgetting the role of GTS in superframe**: A minimum of **1 slot must remain in CAP** — do not allocate all 7 GTS slots.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember — KTU Module 3: The MAC Layer**

- [x] The **MAC sublayer** is sublayer-2a of the **Data Link Layer** in the OSI model.
- [x] IoT MAC design is dominated by the **energy-efficiency** requirement, not raw throughput.
- [x] **Pure ALOHA**: $S = G e^{-2G}$, max = $1/(2e) \approx 18.4\%$.
- [x] **Slotted ALOHA**: $S = G e^{-G}$, max = $1/e \approx 36.8\%$.
- [x] **CSMA/CA** is the workhorse of wireless IoT (IEEE 802.15.4, Wi-Fi).
- [x] **CSMA/CD** is **wired-only** (Ethernet); CSMA/CA is **wireless**.
- [x] **IEEE 802.15.4**: 2.4 GHz, 16 channels, 250 kbps, O-QPSK modulation.
- [x] **Unit backoff period** = 20 symbols = **320 µs**.
- [x] **macMinBE = 3**, **macMaxBE = 5**, **macMaxCSMABackoffs = 4**.
- [x] **MAC frame (MPDU)** has max 127 bytes; payload max 101 bytes.
- [x] **Superframe** contains **CAP (CSMA/CA)** + **CFP (up to 7 GTS)** + **Inactive Period**.
- [x] **Beacon Interval** = $aBaseSuperframeDuration \cdot 2^{BO}$.
- [x] **S-MAC**: synchronous, virtual clusters, ~10 % duty cycle.
- [x] **B-MAC**: asynchronous, long-preamble listening, low overhead.
- [x] **ContikiMAC**: phase-lock wake-up, <1 % duty cycle, production-grade.
- [x] **LoRaWAN** uses **pure ALOHA** with **1 % duty-cycle** regulatory cap in EU 868 MHz.
- [x] **NB-IoT and LTE-M** use scheduled (TDMA-like) access under LTE.
- [x] **Hidden-terminal problem** is solved by **RTS/CTS** in 802.11.
- [x] **Energy per bit** in WSN: aim for **< 100 µJ/bit** for multi-year lifetime.
- [x] **PAN Coordinator** in IEEE 802.15.4 owns beacon generation and GTS allocation.
- [x] **EUI-64** = 64-bit extended unique identifier, IEEE-defined hardware address.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uses of 802.15.4
topics.content:
