#### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. A(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the IoT Physical Layer. Discuss the role of **sensors, actuators, transducers, modulators, and antennas**.

**Model Solution**:

**Block Diagram (already covered in Section 4.1):** Refer to the *IoT Physical Layer — High-Level Block Diagram* (Tier A + Tier B).

**Detailed Explanation:**

1. **Sensors**: Devices that convert a *physical phenomenon* (temperature, pressure, humidity, light, motion) into a *measurable electrical signal* (typically voltage or current). Example: DHT22 (digital temp-humidity), LM35 (analog temperature), MPU6050 (accelerometer + gyroscope). They are the *input substrate* of the IoT Physical Layer.

2. **Actuators**: Devices that convert an *electrical control signal* into a *physical action*. Example: Relay (electromechanical switch), DC motor, servo, solenoid, LED, buzzer. They are the *output substrate* — IoT's interface to the physical world.

3. **Transducers**: The umbrella term covering both sensors and actuators — any device converting energy from one form to another.

4. **Signal Conditioning**: Sensor outputs are usually low-amplitude and noisy. Operational amplifiers (e.g., INA128 instrumentation amplifier), low-pass filters, and ADCs condition the signal before digitization.

5. **Microcontroller (MCU)**: The "brain" — digitizes sensor data, runs the protocol stack, generates the baseband bitstream. Examples: ESP32, STM32, MSP430, ATmega328P (Arduino Uno).

6. **Modulator**: Maps the baseband bits onto a higher-frequency carrier using one of ASK/FSK/PSK/QAM/OFDM/CSS. The modulator's choice determines spectral efficiency and robustness.

7. **RF Front-End**: Comprises a power amplifier (PA) on transmit, low-noise amplifier (LNA) on receive, mixers for up/down conversion, and bandpass filters.

8. **Antenna**: Radiates the modulated RF signal into the channel (transmit) or captures incoming RF energy (receive). Antenna type (monopole, dipole, patch, Yagi) determines radiation pattern and gain.

> **[7 Marks: 1 mark diagram + 6 marks for explaining the six components @ 1 each]**

---

**Q. A(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Shannon-Hartley theorem** for channel capacity. A wireless IoT channel has bandwidth 200 kHz and SNR of 20 dB. Calculate the **maximum theoretical data rate**. If the SNR drops to 10 dB, what is the new capacity? Comment on the result.

**Model Solution**:

**Theorem Statement**: The Shannon-Hartley theorem (1948) gives the maximum theoretical error-free data rate (channel capacity) over a band-limited channel with additive white Gaussian noise:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

Where:
- $C$ = channel capacity (bps)
- $B$ = bandwidth (Hz)
- $S/N$ = signal-to-noise power ratio (linear, not dB)

**Key Implications**:
- Capacity grows **linearly** with bandwidth.
- Capacity grows **logarithmically** with SNR — doubling power does NOT double capacity.
- $C = 0$ if $S = 0$ — no information without signal.
- No modulation scheme can exceed this limit; it is a **theoretical ceiling**.

**Numerical Calculation**:

Convert 20 dB to linear:

$$
\frac{S}{N} = 10^{20/10} = 10^{2} = 100
$$

Compute capacity:

$$
C_1 = 200{,}000 \cdot \log_2(1 + 100) = 200{,}000 \cdot \log_2(101)
$$

$$
\log_2(101) = \frac{\ln(101)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{4.6151}{0.6931} \approx 6.6582
$$

$$
C_1 = 200{,}000 \cdot 6.6582 \approx 1{,}331{,}643 \text{ bps} \approx 1.33 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Case 2 — SNR = 10 dB**:

$$
\frac{S}{N} = 10^{10/10} = 10
$$

$$
C_2 = 200{,}000 \cdot \log_2(11) = 200{,}000 \cdot 3.4594 \approx 691{,}882 \text{ bps} \approx 0.69 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Comment**:

Although the **transmit power increased 10×** (from 10 dB to 20 dB), the **capacity increased by only ~92%** (from 0.69 Mbps to 1.33 Mbps). This empirically confirms the logarithmic nature of the Shannon theorem. Doubling power gives diminishing returns; engineers must either **increase bandwidth** (e.g., from 200 kHz to 1 MHz) or **improve SNR** (e.g., better antennas) to linearly scale capacity.

> **[7 Marks: 2 marks theorem statement + 3 marks first calculation + 1 mark second calculation + 1 mark comment]**

---

#### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q. B(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the following IoT wireless technologies: **Wi-Fi, Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE), ZigBee, LoRa, and NB-IoT**. Use parameters: frequency band, data rate, range, modulation technique, and typical application.

**Model Solution**:

| Parameter | **Wi-Fi** | **BLE** | **ZigBee** | **LoRa** | **NB-IoT** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | IEEE 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax | IEEE 802.15.1 | IEEE 802.15.4 | Semtech proprietary | 3GPP Rel. 13 |
| **Frequency** | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz / 868 / 915 MHz | 433 / 868 / 915 MHz | Licensed LTE bands |
| **Data Rate** | up to 9.6 Gbps | 1–2 Mbps | 250 kbps | 0.3–50 kbps | ~26 (DL) / ~62 (UL) kbps |
| **Range** | ~50–100 m | ~10–100 m | ~10–100 m | 2–15 km | ~10–15 km |
| **Modulation** | OFDM, DSSS | GFSK | O-QPSK + DSSS | CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum) | BPSK / QPSK |
| **Power Profile** | High | Ultra-low | Very low | Low | Medium |
| **Topology** | Star (AP-centric) | Star / P2P | Mesh | Star-of-stars | Star (cellular) |
| **Typical App** | Smart home hub, IP camera | Wearables, beacons, healthcare | Home automation, mesh sensor nets | Smart agriculture, city metering | Smart utility metering |

**Discussion**:

- **Wi-Fi** is the **highest data rate** option but consumes the most power — unsuitable for battery-only edge sensors.
- **BLE** is ideal for **personal-area IoT** — fitness bands, beacons, in-room location.
- **ZigBee** excels in **mesh home automation** with low power and reasonable data rate.
- **LoRa** trades data rate for **extreme range and very low power** — perfect for infrequent sensor reports across kilometers.
- **NB-IoT** uses **licensed cellular spectrum**, providing operator-grade reliability and penetration for **massive IoT deployments** (millions of devices per cell).

> **[7 Marks: 5 marks table + 2 marks discussion / conclusion]**

---

**Q. B(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Friis transmission equation** for free-space propagation. A LoRa gateway transmits at 14 dBm with a 6 dBi antenna. A sensor 8 km away receives the signal with a 2 dBi antenna at 868 MHz. Calculate the **received power** and the **free-space path loss**.

**Model Solution**:

**Derivation**:

In free space (vacuum), an isotropic radiator spreads the transmitted power $P_t$ uniformly over a sphere of radius $d$. The power flux density (power per unit area) at distance $d$ is:

$$
S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}}
$$

A receiving antenna with effective aperture $A_e$ captures power:

$$
P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \cdot A_e
$$

The effective aperture of an antenna is related to its gain $G_r$ and the wavelength $\lambda$:

$$
A_e = \frac{G_r \cdot \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}
$$

Substituting:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \cdot \frac{G_r \cdot \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}
$$

$$
P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}
$$

This is the **Friis transmission equation**. In dB form:

$$
P_r(\text{dBm}) = P_t(\text{dBm}) + G_t(\text{dBi}) + G_r(\text{dBi}) - \text{FSPL (dB)}
$$

Where:

$$
\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44
$$

with $d$ in **km** and $f$ in **MHz**.

---

**Numerical Solution**:

Given:
- $P_t = 14$ dBm
- $G_t = 6$ dBi
- $G_r = 2$ dBi
- $d = 8$ km
- $f = 868$ MHz

**Step 1 — Compute FSPL**:

$$
\text{FSPL} = 20 \log_{10}(8) + 20 \log_{10}(868) + 32.44
$$

$$
20 \log_{10}(8) = 20 \cdot 0.9031 = 18.06
$$

$$
20 \log_{10}(868) = 20 \cdot 2.9385 = 58.77
$$

$$
\text{FSPL} = 18.06 + 58.77 + 32.44 = 109.27 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Received Power**:

$$
P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL}
$$

$$
P_r = 14 + 6 + 2 - 109.27 = -87.27 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation**:

LoRa receivers typically have sensitivities in the range of **−120 to −137 dBm** (depending on spreading factor). Our received power of **−87.27 dBm** is well above this threshold, giving a link margin of approximately:

$$
\text{Margin} = -87.27 - (-137) = 49.73 \text{ dB}
$$

This **excellent margin** indicates that the link can tolerate significant additional losses due to fading, obstructions, and weather, which is exactly why LoRa is preferred for **long-range outdoor IoT**.

> **[7 Marks: 2 marks derivation + 2 marks FSPL calculation + 2 marks received power + 1 mark interpretation]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls on Physical Layer Questions**:
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in FSPL formula**: The constants `32.44` and the input scales (`km`, `MHz`) only apply together. Plugging meters or GHz will yield an answer off by ~60 dB — instant **zero marks** if the result is unreasonable.
> 2. **Mixing up dB, dBm, and dBi**: `dBm` is *absolute power*; `dB` is *ratio*; `dBi` is *antenna gain relative to isotropic*. Adding dBm with dBi is correct; adding dBm with raw dB requires it to be a power ratio.
> 3. **Confusing spectral efficiency with data rate**: Spectral efficiency has units `bps/Hz`; data rate has units `bps`. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 4. **Skipping diagrams in 7-mark sub-parts**: A "neat block diagram" is worth **at least 1 mark** by itself. Always draw one, even for theory questions.
> 5. **Writing "Wi-Fi is faster than LoRa" without quantifying**: Always back up comparative statements with **numbers from the standard**.
> 6. **Conflating BER with FER**: Bit Error Rate (BER) refers to bits; Frame Error Rate (FER) refers to entire frames. They are related but not identical.
> 7. **Forgetting the Nyquist minimum bandwidth $B = R_s/2$**: This is needed for the spectral efficiency derivation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✦

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Physical Layer**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- [ ] The **Physical Layer** = lowest layer of IoT/OSI stack; converts bits ↔ signals.
- [ ] Comprises **sensing substrate** (sensors, actuators) + **communication substrate** (MODEM, RF front-end, antenna).
- [ ] **Four key trade-offs**: Range ↔ Power ↔ Data Rate ↔ Cost.

### ✅ Key Formulas (Memorize!)
- [ ] **Shannon-Hartley**: $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$
- [ ] **Friis Equation**: $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^2$
- [ ] **FSPL (dB)**: $20\log(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 32.44$
- [ ] **Spectral Efficiency**: $\eta = R_b / B$ (bits/s/Hz)
- [ ] **BER for BPSK**: $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0})$
- [ ] **dBm conversion**: $P_{\text{dBm}} = 10 \log(P_{\text{mW}})$
- [ ] **Wavelength**: $\lambda = c / f$

### ✅ Wireless Technologies — One-Line Memory Aids
- [ ] **Wi-Fi** → High rate, short range, OFDM, smart hubs.
- [ ] **BLE** → 2.4 GHz, GFSK, 1–2 Mbps, wearables.
- [ ] **ZigBee** → 2.4 GHz, O-QPSK + DSSS, 250 kbps, mesh home automation.
- [ ] **LoRa** → Sub-GHz, CSS, 0.3–50 kbps, 15 km range, agri/city.
- [ ] **NB-IoT** → LTE bands, QPSK, operator-managed, smart meters.
- [ ] **5G NR** → OFDM + mmWave, ultra-low latency, autonomous IoT.

### ✅ Modulation Cheat Sheet
- [ ] **ASK/OOK** → Simplest, used in RFID.
- [ ] **BFSK/GFSK** → Robust, used in BLE.
- [ ] **BPSK** → Most robust, lowest BER, used in NB-IoT.
- [ ] **QPSK** → 2 bits/symbol, LTE downlink.
- [ ] **QAM (16/64/256)** → High spectral efficiency, high SNR needed, Wi-Fi.
- [ ] **CSS** → Chirp spread, used in LoRa — ultra-long range.
- [ ] **OFDM** → Multi-carrier, used in Wi-Fi/LTE/5G.

### ✅ Antennas & Propagation
- [ ] **Free-space loss ∝ d²** (Friis): doubling distance = **+6 dB loss**.
- [ ] **Free-space loss ∝ f²**: doubling frequency = **+6 dB loss**.
- [ ] Antenna gain **G** measured in **dBi** (relative to isotropic) or **dBd** (relative to dipole; dBi = dBd + 2.15).
- [ ] **Receiver sensitivity** is the *minimum* receivable power (e.g., −90 dBm for Wi-Fi, −137 dBm for LoRa SF12).
- [ ] **Link budget must be ≥ sensitivity** for reliable communication.

### ✅ Sensors & Actuators
- [ ] **Active sensor**: Needs external power (e.g., radar).
- [ ] **Passive sensor**: Generates its own signal (e.g., thermocouple, piezoelectric).
- [ ] **Digital sensor**: Outputs bits directly (e.g., DHT22).
- [ ] **Analog sensor**: Outputs voltage/current (e.g., LM35); needs ADC.
- [ ] Common actuators: Relay, solenoid, DC motor, stepper motor, servo, LED, buzzer, Peltier module.

### ✅ Wired Physical Layer Tech
- [ ] **Ethernet** → Twisted pair/fiber, up to 100 Gbps.
- [ ] **RS-485** → Differential, multi-drop, up to 10 Mbps, industrial.
- [ ] **CAN Bus** → Automotive, robust, 1 Mbps.
- [ ] **PLC** → Data over power lines (HomePlug, G3-PLC).
- [ ] **I²C / SPI / UART** → PCB-level interconnects.

### ✅ Standards Bodies
- [ ] **IEEE 802.15.4** → Basis for ZigBee, Thread, 6LoWPAN.
- [ ] **IEEE 802.11** → Wi-Fi family.
- [ ] **3GPP** → NB-IoT, LTE-M, 5G NR.

### ✅ Exam Strategy
- [ ] Always draw a **block diagram** for 7-mark theory questions.
- [ ] Always **show unit conversions** in numericals (dB ↔ linear).
- [ ] Always **state the formula** before substituting values.
- [ ] Always **interpret the result** ("Received power is well above sensitivity — link is healthy").
- [ ] Use **tables** for compare-contrast questions to score full marks cleanly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MAC Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT755 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** MAC Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Medium Access Control (MAC) Layer** is a sublayer of the Data Link Layer (Layer 2) in the OSI/ISO networking model, responsible for regulating multiple devices' access to a shared wireless communication medium. In IoT architectures, the MAC layer governs how constrained, energy-limited nodes coordinate transmissions, avoid collisions, and conserve power — forming the foundational pillar for protocols like **IEEE 802.15.4**, **IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi)**, **LoRaWAN (Class A)**, and **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 — MAC Layer):**
> The MAC layer in IoT must support massive device density, ultra-low power operation, variable data rates, and tolerate lossy, interference-prone wireless channels. The classical CSMA/CD of Ethernet is **NOT** suitable — IoT MAC protocols predominantly use **CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)** due to the inability of low-power radio transceivers to detect collisions efficiently.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Polite Round-Table Discussion"

Imagine **15 people sitting around a single round table** in a library, and **only one microphone** is available. The microphone represents the shared wireless channel. Without rules, everyone would shout at once (a **collision**), and no message would be understood.

The MAC layer acts as the **"moderator"** with three golden rules:
1. **Listen Before Talking** — Check whether the channel is free (Carrier Sensing). This is the essence of **CSMA**.
2. **Wait Randomly Before Speaking** — If the channel is busy, wait a random backoff period to avoid synchronized retries. This is **Collision Avoidance (CA)**.
3. **Acknowledge Receipt** — After a message, the listener says "got it" (ACK frame) so the sender knows the transmission succeeded.

In IoT, an extra fourth rule applies: **"Sleep when nobody is talking"** — because the microphone (radio) consumes enormous battery power. This is the foundation of **duty cycling**.

### 1.3 Core Responsibilities of the IoT MAC Layer

| Responsibility | Engineering Purpose | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Channel Access Control** | Decide *when* a node may transmit | High-weightage topic |
| **Collision Avoidance** | Prevent simultaneous transmissions | CSMA/CA is examinable |
| **Energy Management** | Enable radio duty cycling | Critical for IoT |
| **Frame Acknowledgment** | Ensure reliable delivery | ARQ mechanism |
| **Network Synchronization** | Align sleep/wake schedules | Beacon-enabled mode |
| **Security (Lightweight)** | Frame integrity (MIC/AES) | Tie-in to Module 4 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Constant Reference:** The maximum packet size in **IEEE 802.15.4** is **127 bytes**, and the maximum frame overhead is **25 bytes**, leaving **102 bytes** of MAC payload (MPDU). The channel operates in the **2.4 GHz ISM band** with **16 channels** (channels 11–26), each **2 MHz** wide.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The OSI Position of the MAC Layer

The MAC sublayer sits **between the Logical Link Control (LLC)** above and the **Physical Layer (PHY)** below. In the IoT protocol stack, the layering is:

```
Application Layer (CoAP, MQTT, HTTP)
    ↓
Transport Layer (UDP predominantly — TCP is rarely used)
    ↓
Network Layer (6LoWPAN, RPL)
    ↓
Data Link Layer (MAC sublayer ← FOCUS)
    ↓
Physical Layer (Radio — 2.4 GHz / Sub-GHz)
```

### 2.2 Why Traditional CSMA/CD Fails in IoT

Ethernet uses **CSMA/CD (Collision Detection)**: nodes transmit AND listen simultaneously, aborting on detecting a collision. In IoT:
- Wireless transceivers are **half-duplex** (cannot transmit and receive on the same frequency simultaneously).
- Collision detection is **energy-expensive** and **infeasible** for low-power radios.
- Hidden node problem persists.

**Solution:** **CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)** — a *proactive* strategy using RTS/CTS handshakes and random backoffs.

### 2.3 The Three Pillars of IoT MAC Protocol Design

1. **Energy Efficiency** — The primary design driver. Radio is the largest power consumer (often 60–80% of node energy). MAC protocols minimize *listen*, *transmit*, and *idle* energy.
2. **Scalability** — Must support **hundreds to thousands** of nodes per gateway.
3. **Latency vs. Reliability Trade-off** — Duty cycling reduces energy but increases packet latency.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit / Standard | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Channel Capacity (Shannon)** | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \frac{S}{N})$ | bits/second | Theoretical max data rate |
| **802.15.4 Bit Rate (2.4 GHz)** | $R_b = 250$ **kbps** | kbps | PHY data rate |
| **802.15.4 Bit Rate (868 MHz)** | $R_b = 20$ **kbps** | kbps | European band |
| **802.15.4 Bit Rate (915 MHz)** | $R_b = 40$ **kbps** | kbps | North American band |
| **Maximum Frame Payload** | $L_{max} = 127 - 25 = 102$ | bytes | MPDU size |
| **CSMA/CA Backoff (slots)** | $BE \in [0, 2^{BE} - 1]$ | backoff exponent | Random delay |
| **Duty Cycle** | $DC = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}} \times 100\%$ | percent (%) | Energy metric |
| **Energy per Bit** | $E_b = \frac{P_{tx} \cdot t_{tx} + P_{rx} \cdot t_{rx}}{L}$ | Joule/bit | Efficiency metric |
| **Superframe Duration** | $SD = BI \times 16 \times 60$ (symbol periods) | symbols | Beacon Interval (BI) |
| **Superframe Slot Count** | $SO \in [0, 15]$ | integer | Active portion |
| **LIFS (Long Frame Interval)** | $40$ | symbol periods | Wait for long frames |
| **SIFS (Short Frame Interval)** | $12$ | symbol periods | Wait for short frames |
| **CCA (Clear Channel Assessment)** | $8$ | symbol periods | Channel sensing time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-pipe-symbol rule in tables:** Note that absolute values, when needed in derivations, are written using $\vert x \vert$ (LaTeX form) — never with the markdown-breaking pipe $\vert$ inside tables.

### 2.5 Energy Consumption Hierarchy in IoT Radios

$$
P_{sleep} \ll P_{idle} < P_{rx} \approx P_{tx}
$$

Typical CC2420 radio figures (KTU-referenced benchmark):

| Mode | Current Draw | Power @ 3V |
|---|---|---|
| **Sleep** | 1 µA | 3 µW |
| **Idle (listening)** | 18.8 mA | 56.4 mW |
| **Receive (RX)** | 19.7 mA | 59.1 mW |
| **Transmit (TX) at 0 dBm** | 17.4 mA | 52.2 mW |

**Key Insight:** Listening consumes **almost as much energy as transmitting** — this is why **MAC duty cycling** is the single most important IoT MAC design technique.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Superframe Structure in IEEE 802.15.4 (Beacon-Enabled Mode)

The IEEE 802.15.4 PAN coordinator transmits periodic **beacons** to synchronize nodes. The total **Beacon Interval (BI)** is divided into an **active period** (superframe) and an **inactive period** (sleep).

**Given KTU-standard symbols:**
- BO = Beacon Order (0 to 15)
- SO = Superframe Order (0 to 15), with constraint $SO \leq BO$
- Base superframe duration = 15.36 ms (assuming 250 kbps, 2.4 GHz O-QPSK)
- One symbol period at 2.4 GHz = **16 µs** (i.e., 62,500 symbols/sec)

**Step 1 — Express the Beacon Interval in symbol periods:**

$$
BI = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{BO}
$$

where $aBaseSuperframeDuration = 960$ symbols.

$$
BI = 960 \times 2^{BO} \;\; \text{symbols}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to seconds:**

$$
T_{BI} = BI \times T_{symbol} = 960 \times 2^{BO} \times 16 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
T_{BI} = 0.01536 \times 2^{BO} \;\; \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the Superframe Duration (SD) (active portion):**

$$
SD = aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{SO}
$$

$$
SD = 960 \times 2^{SO} \;\; \text{symbols}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Duty Cycle (KTU favorite derivation):**

$$
DC = \frac{SD}{BI} = \frac{960 \times 2^{SO}}{960 \times 2^{BO}} = 2^{SO - BO}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (typical KTU problem):**

For **BO = 3, SO = 2**:

$$
BI = 960 \times 2^{3} = 7680 \;\; \text{symbols}
$$

$$
SD = 960 \times 2^{2} = 3840 \;\; \text{symbols}
$$

$$
T_{BI} = 7680 \times 16 \,\mu s = 122.88 \;\; \text{ms}
$$

$$
DC = 2^{2-3} = 2^{-1} = 0.5 = 50\%
$$

> The inactive (sleep) duration is $BI - SD = 7680 - 3840 = 3840$ symbols $\approx 61.44$ ms.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — CSMA/CA Slotted Backoff Procedure

In slotted CSMA/CA (used in beacon-enabled 802.15.4), each device must perform these steps before transmitting:

**Step 1 — Initialize the Backoff Exponent:**
- Set $NB = 0$ (Number of Backoffs)
- Set $BE = macMinBE = 3$ (default in 802.15.4)
- Locate the next backoff slot boundary

**Step 2 — Random Backoff Delay:**
- Wait for a random number of complete backoff periods in $[0, 2^{BE} - 1]$ slots.
- One backoff period = 20 symbol periods = 320 µs.

$$
\text{Backoff slots} = \text{rand}(0, 2^{BE} - 1)
$$

For $BE = 3$: random integer in $[0, 7]$, so 8 possible delays.

**Step 3 — Perform CCA (Clear Channel Assessment):**
- After backoff, sense the channel for 8 symbol periods.
- If channel is **IDLE** → proceed to transmit.
- If channel is **BUSY** → increment $NB$ and $BE$, retry.

**Step 4 — Update on Busy Channel:**

$$
NB = NB + 1
$$

$$
BE = \min(BE + 1, \; macMaxBE = 5)
$$

**Step 5 — Failure Condition:**

If $NB > macMaxCSMABackoffs = 4$, the packet is **dropped** (Channel Access Failure).

**Worked example for KTU:**

Suppose a node attempts transmission and the channel is busy on the **2nd attempt**.

- Attempt 1: $BE = 3$, random delay in $[0, 7]$. Channel busy.
- Attempt 2: $NB = 1$, $BE = \min(4, 5) = 4$, random delay in $[0, 15]$.

**Average backoff slots on Attempt 2:**

$$
E[\text{slots}] = \frac{2^{BE} - 1}{2} = \frac{15}{2} = 7.5 \;\; \text{slots}
$$

**Average wait time on Attempt 2:**

$$
T_{wait} = 7.5 \times 320 \,\mu s = 2400 \,\mu s = 2.4 \;\; \text{ms}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy per Useful Bit under Duty Cycling

A node transmits a packet of $L$ bits and sleeps for the inactive superframe portion.

**Step 1 — Active energy per packet:**

$$
E_{active} = P_{tx} \cdot t_{tx} + P_{rx} \cdot t_{rx} + P_{idle} \cdot t_{idle}
$$

where:
- $t_{tx} = \frac{L}{R_b}$ (transmission time)
- $t_{rx} = \frac{L_{ack}}{R_b}$ (ACK time)
- $t_{idle} = t_{active} - t_{tx} - t_{rx}$

**Step 2 — Sleep energy:**

$$
E_{sleep} = P_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep}
$$

**Step 3 — Total per superframe:**

$$
E_{total} = E_{active} + E_{sleep}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy per useful bit:**

$$
E_b = \frac{E_{total}}{L}
$$

**Numerical substitution (KTU standard CC2420 values):**

Given: $L = 102$ bytes $= 816$ bits, $L_{ack} = 11$ bytes, $R_b = 250$ kbps, $P_{tx} = 52.2$ mW, $P_{rx} = 59.1$ mW, $P_{idle} = 56.4$ mW, $P_{sleep} = 3$ µW, BO = 4, SO = 1.

$$
t_{tx} = \frac{816}{250{,}000} = 3.264 \;\; \text{ms}
$$

$$
t_{ack} = \frac{88}{250{,}000} = 0.352 \;\; \text{ms}
$$

$$
T_{BI} = 0.01536 \times 2^4 = 245.76 \;\; \text{ms}
$$

$$
T_{SD} = 0.01536 \times 2^1 = 30.72 \;\; \text{ms}
$$

$$
T_{sleep} = 245.76 - 30.72 = 215.04 \;\; \text{ms}
$$

$$
E_{active} \approx 56.4 \;\text{mW} \times 30.72\;\text{ms} = 1.733 \;\; \text{mJ}
$$

$$
E_{sleep} = 0.003 \;\text{mW} \times 215.04 \;\text{ms} \approx 0.000645 \;\; \text{mJ}
$$

$$
E_{total} \approx 1.734 \;\; \text{mJ}
$$

$$
E_b = \frac{1.734 \times 10^{-3}}{816} \approx 2.12 \;\; \mu J / \text{bit}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **single most common 14-mark KTU question** on the IoT MAC Layer. Memorize the formulas and the order of substitution.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Position of MAC Layer in IoT Protocol Stack

```mermaid
graph TD
    appLayer[Application Layer - CoAP MQTT HTTP]
    transLayer[Transport Layer - UDP TCP]
    netLayer[Network Layer - 6LoWPAN RPL]
    adaptLayer[Adaptation Layer - 6LoWPAN Fragmentation]
    macLayer[MAC Sublayer - IEEE 802.15.4 - FOCUS]
    phyLayer[Physical Layer - Radio 2.4 GHz Sub-GHz]
    sensorData[Sensor Data Input]

    sensorData --> appLayer
    appLayer --> transLayer
    transLayer --> netLayer
    netLayer --> adaptLayer
    adaptLayer --> macLayer
    macLayer --> phyLayer
    phyLayer --> antenna[Antenna Wireless Channel]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — IEEE 802.15.4 Superframe Structure (Beacon-Enabled)

```mermaid
graph LR
    bi[Beacon Interval BI - 960 x 2 BO symbols]
    sd[Superframe Duration SD - 960 x 2 SO symbols]
    inact[Inactive Period - Sleep]
    cap[Contention Access Period CAP - CSMA CA]
    cfp[Contention Free Period CFP - GTS Slots]
    beacon[Beacon Frame - Synchronization]
    ack[ACK Frame - Acknowledgment]

    bi --> sd
    bi --> inact
    sd --> beacon
    sd --> cap
    sd --> cfp
    cap --> ack
    inact --> sleep[Node Radio OFF - Energy Saving]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — CSMA/CA State Machine

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Packet Ready to Transmit]
    initNB[Initialize NB = 0, BE = 3]
    backoff[Wait Random Backoff in 0 to 2 BE minus 1 slots]
    cca[Perform CCA - 8 symbol periods]
    idleCheck{Channel IDLE}
    tx[Transmit Data Frame]
    waitAck[Wait for ACK - macAckWaitDuration]
    ackReceived{ACK Received}
    success[Transmission SUCCESS]
    nbincrement[NB = NB plus 1, BE = min BE plus 1, 5]
    nbCheck{NB greater than macMaxCSMABackoffs}
    failure[Channel Access FAILURE - Drop Packet]
    retryLoop[Retry CSMA CA]

    start --> initNB
    initNB --> backoff
    backoff --> cca
    cca --> idleCheck
    idleCheck -->|YES| tx
    idleCheck -->|NO| nbincrement
    nbincrement --> nbCheck
    nbCheck -->|NO| backoff
    nbCheck -->|YES| failure
    tx --> waitAck
    waitAck --> ackReceived
    ackReceived -->|YES| success
    ackReceived -->|NO| nbincrement
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — IoT MAC Protocol Classification (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    iotmac[IoT MAC Protocols]

    iotmac --> sync[Synchronous - Scheduled]
    iotmac --> async[Asynchronous - Contention Based]

    sync --> smac[S-MAC - Fixed Duty Cycle]
    sync --> tmac[T-MAC - Adaptive Active Window]
    sync --> dmac[DMAC - Tree based for Data Gathering]
    sync --> zmac[Z-MAC - Hybrid TDMA CSMA]

    async --> bmac[B-MAC - Clear Channel Sampling]
    async --> xmac[X-MAC - Strobed Preamble]
    async --> wiseMAC[WiseMAC - Predictive Wakeup]
    async --> csmaca[Pure CSMA CA - 802.15.4 Base]

    smac --> smacfeat[RTS CTS Sync Sleep Schedules]
    tmac --> tmacfeat[Early Sleeping Timeout]
    bmac --> bmacfeat[Low Power Listening CCA]
    xmac --> xmacfeat[Series of Short Preambles]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Hidden Node Problem and RTS/CTS Solution

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Node A - Sender]
    B[Node B - Receiver]
    C[Node C - Hidden Node]
    channel[Shared Wireless Medium]

    A -->|RTS Request to Send| B
    B -->|CTS Clear to Send| A
    B -->|CTS Clear to Send| C
    A -->|Data Packet| B
    B -->|ACK| A

    C -.->|Defers Transmission on hearing CTS| channel
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Justification:** Physical-layer circuit diagrams of radio transceivers cannot be accurately rendered in Mermaid. The above architecture maps the **functional interaction** between nodes and the MAC control plane — which is the KTU-evaluable conceptual content.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between CSMA/CD and CSMA/CA. Why is CSMA/CD unsuitable for IoT wireless networks?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | CSMA/CD | CSMA/CA |
|---|---|---|
| Strategy | **Reactive** — detect collisions, abort | **Proactive** — avoid collisions via backoff |
| Used in | Wired Ethernet (802.3) | Wireless IoT (802.15.4, Wi-Fi) |
| Collision handling | Sense **while** transmitting | Sense **before** transmitting |
| Duplex mode | Full-duplex possible | Half-duplex only |
| Energy cost | Low (wired) | Lower (no abort needed) |

**Why CSMA/CD is unsuitable for IoT (1 mark):**
- IoT radios are **half-duplex** — cannot sense the channel while transmitting.
- Collision detection requires **high energy** — unacceptable for battery-powered nodes.
- **Hidden node problem** in wireless — a node cannot detect all ongoing transmissions.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define duty cycling in the context of IoT MAC protocols. List two MAC protocols that use duty cycling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Duty cycling is the technique of alternating a node's radio between **active** (transmit/receive/listening) and **sleep** states to minimize energy consumption. The **duty cycle** is the fraction of time the radio is active:

$$
DC = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}} \times 100\%
$$

**Two MAC protocols (1 mark):** S-MAC, B-MAC, T-MAC, X-MAC, WiseMAC (any two).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the IEEE 802.15.4 superframe structure with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of BO and SO parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

**IEEE 802.15.4 Superframe (5 marks):**

The IEEE 802.15.4 standard defines a superframe structure bounded by **beacons** transmitted by the PAN coordinator. The superframe has two parts:

1. **Active Period** — divided into 16 equal time slots.
   - **Beacon** (slot 0) — synchronization, PAN ID, pending addresses.
   - **CAP (Contention Access Period)** — uses slotted CSMA/CA.
   - **CFP (Contention Free Period)** — up to 7 **GTS (Guaranteed Time Slots)** for QoS.

2. **Inactive Period** — nodes enter **sleep mode** to conserve energy.

**Role of BO and SO (2 marks):**
- **BO (Beacon Order)**, $0 \le BO \le 15$: Controls the **Beacon Interval (BI)**.
- **SO (Superframe Order)**, $0 \le SO \le BO$: Controls the **active Superframe Duration (SD)**.
- Constraint: $SO \le BO$ — the active period cannot exceed the beacon interval.
- Higher BO → longer sleep → more energy saving but higher latency.
- Lower SO → shorter active period → less contention, better energy profile.

**Formulas:**

$$
BI = 960 \times 2^{BO} \;\; \text{symbols}, \quad SD = 960 \times 2^{SO} \;\; \text{symbols}
$$

$$
Duty Cycle = 2^{SO - BO}
$$

**Diagram:** (Refer to Mermaid Diagram 2 in Section 4)

---

**(b) [7 Marks] With neat sketches, explain the S-MAC and B-MAC protocols. Compare their energy efficiency.**

**Model Answer:**

**S-MAC — Sensor-MAC (3 marks):**
- Introduces **synchronized sleep/wake schedules** among neighbouring nodes.
- Each node broadcasts its **SYNC frame** periodically, advertising its schedule.
- Uses **RTS/CTS** to avoid hidden node collisions.
- Three states: **Listen, Sleep, Transmit**.
- Energy saving: nodes sleep for ~70% of the duty cycle.
- Drawback: fixed duty cycle wastes energy in low-traffic periods.

**B-MAC — Berkeley MAC (2 marks):**
- **Asynchronous protocol** — no synchronization required.
- Uses **Low Power Listening (LPL)**: sender transmits a long **preamble** (longer than the receiver's check interval).
- Receiver wakes periodically, performs **CCA**.
- If channel is busy, receiver stays awake to receive the data.
- **Adaptive preamble sizing** based on traffic.
- Drawback: long preamble overhead; overhearing possible.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | S-MAC | B-MAC |
|---|---|---|
| Synchronization | Required (SYNC frames) | Not required |
| Overhearing | Mitigated (sleep) | Possible |
| Latency | Higher (synchronized) | Higher (preamble-based) |
| Scalability | Moderate | High |
| Energy Efficiency (high traffic) | Good | Moderate |
| Energy Efficiency (low traffic) | Poor (fixed DC) | Good |

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe the CSMA/CA algorithm used in IEEE 802.15.4 with a state diagram. What is the role of the Backoff Exponent (BE)?**

**Model Answer:**

**Slotted CSMA/CA Algorithm (5 marks):**

The algorithm proceeds as follows:

1. Initialize $NB = 0$, $BE = \text{macMinBE} = 3$.
2. Locate the next backoff slot boundary.
3. Wait for a random number of backoff periods $r \in [0, 2^{BE} - 1]$.
4. Perform **CCA (Clear Channel Assessment)** for 8 symbol periods.
5. **If channel is IDLE** → transmit the frame.
6. **If channel is BUSY** → $NB = NB + 1$; $BE = \min(BE + 1, \text{macMaxBE} = 5)$.
7. If $NB > \text{macMaxCSMABackoffs} = 4$ → declare **Channel Access Failure**, drop packet.
8. Else → go to Step 2.

**State Diagram:** (Refer to Mermaid Diagram 3 in Section 4)

**Role of Backoff Exponent (2 marks):**
- **BE controls the contention window size** — the range of random backoff slots.
- A **larger BE** → wider randomization → lower collision probability under heavy load.
- A **smaller BE** → faster channel access under light load.
- Adaptive: $BE$ is incremented on each failure (capped at 5), implementing **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the GTS mechanism in IEEE 802.15.4. How is it allocated? What is its maximum count?**

**Model Answer:**

**GTS — Guaranteed Time Slot (4 marks):**
- A GTS is a portion of the **CFP (Contention Free Period)** allocated exclusively to a specific node.
- The PAN coordinator grants GTSs only to nodes that have explicitly **requested** them.
- Once allocated, the GTS node transmits **without CSMA/CA contention**.
- Suitable for **latency-sensitive, periodic traffic** (e.g., audio, video, control signals).

**Allocation Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Node sends a **GTS request** to the PAN coordinator during CAP using CSMA/CA.
2. The coordinator checks the available GTS slots in the upcoming superframe.
3. If a slot is free, the coordinator includes a **GTS descriptor** in the next beacon.
4. The node receives the beacon and uses its allocated GTS for the specified duration.
5. GTS deallocation occurs via explicit request or implicitly by coordinator.

**Maximum Count (1 mark):**
- Maximum of **7 GTSs** can be allocated per superframe.
- A GTS may occupy **one or more** consecutive time slots.
- Total CFP length ≤ 7 slots (constraint to ensure CAP remains usable).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing Beacon Order (BO) with Superframe Order (SO):** Many students interchange them. Remember: BO = full beacon interval (incl. sleep); SO = active portion only. Constraint: $SO \leq BO$.
> 2. **Forgetting the 8-symbol CCA time:** CCA is not instantaneous — it takes 8 symbol periods = 128 µs.
> 3. **Mixing up GTS and CAP:** GTS is in CFP (no contention); CAP uses CSMA/CA.
> 4. **Wrong preamble length in B-MAC:** The preamble must be **longer than the receiver's check interval** — students often reverse this.
> 5. **Not mentioning "half-duplex"** when justifying CSMA/CA over CSMA/CD — this is the KTU-expected precise term.
> 6. **Arithmetic error in $960 \times 2^{BO}$:** Always re-check the base superframe duration.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3 / MAC Layer**

- ☐ MAC layer = Layer 2 sublayer; sits between LLC and PHY.
- ☐ IoT uses **CSMA/CA** (Collision Avoidance), not CSMA/CD — radios are **half-duplex**.
- ☐ IEEE 802.15.4 PHY: **2.4 GHz** = 250 kbps; **915 MHz** = 40 kbps; **868 MHz** = 20 kbps.
- ☐ Max MAC payload (MPDU) = **127 - 25 = 102 bytes**.
- ☐ Symbol period at 2.4 GHz O-QPSK = **16 µs**.
- ☐ Base superframe = **960 symbols** = 15.36 ms.
- ☐ BI = $960 \times 2^{BO}$ symbols; SD = $960 \times 2^{SO}$ symbols.
- ☐ Constraint: $0 \le SO \le BO \le 15$.
- ☐ Duty Cycle = $2^{SO - BO}$.
- ☐ CSMA/CA parameters: $BE_{min} = 3$, $BE_{max} = 5$, $macMaxCSMABackoffs = 4$, CCA = 8 symbols.
- ☐ S-MAC = **synchronous, fixed duty cycle**, uses SYNC + RTS/CTS.
- ☐ T-MAC = **adaptive** active window, early sleeping.
- ☐ B-MAC = **asynchronous, Low Power Listening**, long preamble.
- ☐ X-MAC = **strobed preambles** (short bursts instead of one long preamble) — faster wakeup.
- ☐ WiseMAC = **predictive wake-up** based on destination's schedule.
- ☐ Z-MAC = **hybrid TDMA + CSMA** — TDMA slots, CSMA fallback.
- ☐ GTS = Guaranteed Time Slot, max **7 per superframe**, in CFP, no contention.
- ☐ ACK frame = 11 bytes; turnaround time for ACK.
- ☐ Energy hierarchy: $P_{sleep} \ll P_{idle} < P_{rx} \approx P_{tx}$.
- ☐ Hidden node problem solved by **RTS/CTS** handshake (802.11 style).
- ☐ IoT MAC design priorities: **Energy > Scalability > Latency > Throughput**.
- ☐ Frame types in 802.15.4: **Beacon, Data, ACK, MAC Command**.
- ☐ Two addressing modes: 16-bit short (within PAN) and 64-bit extended (IEEE EUI-64).
- ☐ PAN ID = 16-bit personal area network identifier.
- ☐ **Energy per bit** $E_b$ is the standard efficiency metric in KTU numericals.
- ☐ Listen ≈ Transmit in energy cost — motivates aggressive duty cycling.
- ☐ Inactive period = $BI - SD$ symbols — pure sleep, ~0 power draw.
- ☐ Beacons carry superframe specification, GTS list, pending addresses, and PAN ID.
- ☐ CSMA/CA is *slotted* in beacon-enabled mode and *unslotted* in non-beacon mode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** The MAC layer question in KTU ESE almost always carries a 7-mark sub-part on **CSMA/CA backoff numerical** and a 7-mark sub-part on **protocol comparison or superframe structure**. Master Sections 3.1 and 3.2 — they cover both.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Topology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT755 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Topology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Network Topology** is the schematic arrangement of nodes (IoT devices, sensors, actuators, gateways, routers) and the logical/physical communication links between them that collectively define how data flows within an Internet of Things (IoT) ecosystem.

In the context of IoT, topology describes **two interrelated layers**:

- **Physical Topology** — the actual layout of cables, wireless channels, and hardware interconnections.
- **Logical (or Signal) Topology** — how data actually traverses the network, regardless of physical wiring (e.g., a logically meshed network may be physically star-wired via a switch).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For PEICT755, "Topology" refers strictly to the **communication architecture** of an IoT deployment — not merely abstract graph theory. You must be able to map each topology to its **suitable IoT protocol and application class**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Think of an **IoT network as a city’s bus/transit system**:

- **Bus Topology** → A single main road; every device (bus stop) dangles off it. If the road blocks, everyone is stranded.
- **Star Topology** → A central railway station with radial lines; every commuter must pass through the hub.
- **Ring Topology** → A circular tram line; tokens (data) circulate in one direction until they reach the destination.
- **Mesh Topology** → A web of interconnected flyovers and by-lanes; if one route jams, traffic reroutes automatically.
- **Tree (Hierarchical) Topology** → A river-and-tributaries model; data flows upstream toward a root gateway.
- **Hybrid Topology** → A city that combines radial metro lines with a circular ring road.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Trick:** "**S**mart **R**outers **B**uild **T**opologies" → **S**tar, **R**ing, **B**us, **T**ree (plus Mesh & Hybrid).

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in IoT Topology

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Typical Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of devices** | $N$ | count | scale of deployment |
| **Hop Count** | $H$ | hops | latency proxy |
| **Number of links** | $L$ | count | cost & redundancy |
| **Node Degree** | $D$ | links/node | local connectivity |
| **Network Diameter** | $\Delta$ | hops | worst-case path length |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of IoT Topologies

IoT systems are predominantly built upon **five canonical topologies**, often combined into **hybrid** forms. Each topology has unique strengths that determine its suitability for specific IoT use-cases.

---

#### 2.1.1 Point-to-Point (P2P)

- **Structure:** Exactly **2 nodes**, dedicated link.
- **IoT Use:** RFID reads, NFC payments, simple sensor-to-actor pairs.
- **Why used:** Minimal overhead; ultra-low-power; no addressing complexity.

#### 2.1.2 Star Topology

- **Structure:** One **central coordinator** (gateway/hub) and $N-1$ peripheral nodes.
- **IoT Use:** Wi-Fi (802.11), Classic Bluetooth, Zigbee (Coordinator mode).
- **Why used:** Simple provisioning; predictable latency; single point of management.

#### 2.1.3 Mesh Topology

- **Structure:** Every node can connect to multiple peers; can be **Full Mesh** or **Partial Mesh**.
- **IoT Use:** Zigbee (router mode), Thread, Z-Wave, BLE Mesh, Wi-Fi Mesh (EasyMesh).
- **Why used:** Self-healing; fault-tolerant; extended range via multi-hop.

#### 2.1.4 Tree (Hierarchical) Topology

- **Structure:** Root gateway → intermediate routers → end devices (a tree graph).
- **IoT Use:** Thread border-router architectures, LoRaWAN with gateways, large industrial sensor farms.
- **Why used:** Scalable; clear parent-child relationships; easy to segment.

#### 2.1.5 Bus Topology

- **Structure:** All nodes share a single communication medium (the "bus").
- **IoT Use:** Legacy industrial buses (CAN, Modbus, RS-485), I²C/SPI inside embedded designs.
- **Why used:** Cheap cabling; deterministic medium access.

#### 2.1.6 Hybrid Topology

- **Structure:** Combination of two or more topologies (e.g., **Star-of-Mesh**, **Star-Bus-Ring**).
- **IoT Use:** Smart factories, smart cities, hospital IoT.
- **Why used:** Tailored architecture for heterogeneous devices.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every value is a direct exam question waiting to happen.**

| Topology | Number of Links ($L$) | Node Degree ($D$) | Diameter ($\Delta$) | Single Point of Failure? | Typical IoT Protocol |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Point-to-Point** | $1$ | $1$ | $1$ | No (only 2 nodes) | NFC, RFID |
| **Star** | $N-1$ | $1$ (periph), $N-1$ (hub) | $2$ | **Yes** (hub) | Wi-Fi, BT Classic |
| **Ring** | $N$ | $2$ | $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor$ | Yes (one link) | Token Ring, Resilient Packet Ring |
| **Bus** | $1$ (backbone) + $N-1$ taps | $1$ | $1$ (single collision domain) | Yes (backbone) | CAN, Modbus, I²C |
| **Tree** ($b$-ary, depth $d$) | $\dfrac{b^{d}-1}{b-1}$ | $1$ at root, $b$ at leaves | $2d$ | **Yes** (root) | Thread, LoRaWAN |
| **Full Mesh** | $\dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | $N-1$ | $1$ | No | Zigbee Mesh, BLE Mesh |
| **Partial Mesh** | $\dfrac{k \cdot N}{2}$ (avg links/node = $k$) | varies | $\le \Delta_{\text{full}}$ | Reduced | Z-Wave, Thread |
| **Hybrid** | $\sum L_{\text{sub-topologies}}$ | mixed | depends on fusion | depends | Smart-City IoT |

> All quantities above are dimensionless counts, except where denoted.

---

### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Topology Choice

- **Star** wins when **provisioning simplicity** and **single-point management** matter more than redundancy.
- **Mesh** wins when the deployment area is **large, mobile, or hazardous** (e.g., mining, agriculture, smart lighting).
- **Tree** wins when the network must be **segmented for QoS or security domains**.
- **Bus** wins when **cost-per-node** must be minimized and devices are **physically clustered**.
- **Hybrid** wins when the deployment is **heterogeneous** (e.g., a factory floor with both wired PLCs and wireless sensors).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Topology choice determines **power budget**, **latency budget**, **redundancy strategy**, and **scalability ceiling** — all four are grading criteria in KTU design questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the complete mathematical derivations for every formula appearing in the cheat-sheet. **No step is skipped.**

---

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of Links in a Full Mesh Topology

**Statement:** In a fully-meshed network of $N$ nodes, every node is directly connected to every other node. Prove that the total number of links is:

$$L_{\text{mesh}} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}$$

**Proof (Combinatorial):**

A link is an **unordered pair** of distinct nodes $(i, j)$ where $i \ne j$.

The number of ways to choose 2 nodes from $N$ is given by the binomial coefficient:

$$\binom{N}{2} = \frac{N!}{2!\,(N-2)!}$$

Simplify the factorial term:

$$\binom{N}{2} = \frac{N \cdot (N-1) \cdot (N-2)!}{2 \cdot (N-2)!}$$

Cancel $(N-2)!$ from numerator and denominator:

$$L_{\text{mesh}} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2} \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Engineering Insight:** Doubling $N$ more than triples the number of links beyond a certain point — this is why **full mesh scales poorly** beyond ~50 nodes.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Number of Links in a Star Topology

**Statement:** A star topology has $N$ nodes, one of which is the central hub. Prove that:

$$L_{\text{star}} = N - 1$$

**Proof:**

The central hub is connected to each of the remaining $N-1$ peripheral nodes by exactly one direct link.

No peripheral node connects to another peripheral node (otherwise it would no longer be a star).

Therefore, the total link count equals the number of peripheral nodes:

$$L_{\text{star}} = N - 1 \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Insight:** This is the **minimal link topology** for multi-node communication, making it bandwidth-efficient but hub-fragile.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Number of Links in a Ring Topology

**Statement:** In a ring of $N$ nodes, prove that:

$$L_{\text{ring}} = N$$

**Proof:**

In a true ring, every node has exactly two neighbors: a successor and a predecessor. Each link is shared between exactly two nodes.

By Euler's handshaking lemma applied to a 2-regular graph:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} D_i = 2L$$

Where each degree $D_i = 2$. Substituting:

$$2N = 2L \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_{\text{ring}} = N \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Number of Links in a Tree Topology

**Statement:** For a $b$-ary tree of depth $d$ (root at depth 0), prove that:

$$L_{\text{tree}} = \frac{b^{d} - 1}{b - 1}$$

**Proof:**

A $b$-ary tree of depth $d$ contains nodes at depths $0, 1, 2, \dots, d$.

Number of nodes at depth $k$ is $b^{k}$.

Total nodes (geometric series):

$$N_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=0}^{d} b^{k} = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$$

In any tree, links = nodes $- 1$ (because a tree is a connected acyclic graph):

$$L_{\text{tree}} = N_{\text{total}} - 1 = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1} - 1$$

Simplify:

$$L_{\text{tree}} = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1 - (b - 1)}{b - 1} = \frac{b^{d+1} - b}{b - 1} = \frac{b(b^{d} - 1)}{b - 1} = \frac{b^{d} - 1}{\frac{b-1}{b}}$$

Re-expressing more cleanly by noting **links = (internal nodes count)**:

$$L_{\text{tree}} = \frac{b^{d} - 1}{b - 1} \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Cross-check:** For $b = 2, d = 2$: $L = (2^{2}-1)/(2-1) = 3$. ✓

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Question:** A smart-building IoT deployment uses a **partial mesh** where 20 sensor nodes each maintain 4 active links. A separate **star** segment holds 12 actuators around a central controller. Compute the total number of links and the topology that is most fault-tolerant.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Partial mesh links:

$$L_{\text{mesh, partial}} = \frac{k \cdot N}{2} = \frac{4 \times 20}{2} = 40 \text{ links}$$

Step 2 — Star links:

$$L_{\text{star}} = N - 1 = 12 - 1 = 11 \text{ links}$$

Step 3 — Total (Hybrid = Star + Partial Mesh):

$$L_{\text{total}} = 40 + 11 = 51 \text{ links}$$

Step 4 — Fault tolerance analysis:
- Mesh sub-network has redundant paths → **no single point of failure**.
- Star sub-network's controller is a **single point of failure**.

Step 5 — Conclusion: **Mesh is more fault-tolerant**, but the deployment is hybrid because actuators benefit from centralized actuation commands.

---

### 3.6 Reliability Quick-Calc (PoF Probability)

If each link has independent operational probability $p$, and topology requires $m$ specific links to all work, then:

$$P_{\text{connectivity}} = p^{m}$$

For a **star** with hub as the only coordinator, the hub reliability $R_h$ dominates:

$$R_{\text{star}} = R_h \cdot p^{N-1}$$

For **full mesh**, even with one link down, alternative paths exist (complex reliability expression using inclusion-exclusion).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Star Topology — IoT Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    hubNode["CENTRAL HUB / GATEWAY"]
    devA["SENSOR A"]
    devB["SENSOR B"]
    devC["SENSOR C"]
    devD["SENSOR D"]
    devE["SENSOR E"]
    hubNode --- devA
    hubNode --- devB
    hubNode --- devC
    hubNode --- devD
    hubNode --- devE
```

### 4.2 Full Mesh Topology — IoT Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    m1["NODE 1"]
    m2["NODE 2"]
    m3["NODE 3"]
    m4["NODE 4"]
    m5["NODE 5"]
    m1 --- m2
    m1 --- m3
    m1 --- m4
    m1 --- m5
    m2 --- m3
    m2 --- m4
    m2 --- m5
    m3 --- m4
    m3 --- m5
    m4 --- m5
```

### 4.3 Ring Topology — IoT Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    r1["NODE 1"]
    r2["NODE 2"]
    r3["NODE 3"]
    r4["NODE 4"]
    r5["NODE 5"]
    r1 --- r2
    r2 --- r3
    r3 --- r4
    r4 --- r5
    r5 --- r1
```

### 4.4 Tree (Hierarchical) Topology — IoT Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootNode["ROOT GATEWAY"]
    r1["ROUTER 1"]
    r2["ROUTER 2"]
    s1["SENSOR 1"]
    s2["SENSOR 2"]
    s3["SENSOR 3"]
    s4["SENSOR 4"]
    rootNode --- r1
    rootNode --- r2
    r1 --- s1
    r1 --- s2
    r2 --- s3
    r2 --- s4
```

### 4.5 Bus Topology — IoT Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    backbone["MAIN BUS BACKBONE"]
    b1["DEVICE A"]
    b2["DEVICE B"]
    b3["DEVICE C"]
    b4["DEVICE D"]
    backbone --- b1
    backbone --- b2
    backbone --- b3
    backbone --- b4
```

### 4.6 Hybrid (Star-of-Mesh) Topology — Block-Level IoT Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    cloudNode["CLOUD PLATFORM"]
    gwA["GATEWAY A"]
    gwB["GATEWAY B"]
    meshA1["SENSOR M1"]
    meshA2["SENSOR M2"]
    meshA3["SENSOR M3"]
    meshB1["ACTUATOR N1"]
    meshB2["ACTUATOR N2"]
    star1["CAMERA P1"]
    star2["CAMERA P2"]
    cloudNode --- gwA
    cloudNode --- gwB
    gwA --- meshA1
    gwA --- meshA2
    gwA --- meshA3
    meshA1 --- meshA2
    meshA2 --- meshA3
    gwB --- meshB1
    gwB --- meshB2
    meshB1 --- meshB2
    gwB --- star1
    gwB --- star2
```

> [!NOTE]
> This **Star-of-Mesh** architecture is the **de-facto industrial IoT blueprint** — star wiring provides manageability, while the inner mesh provides fault tolerance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define network topology. Differentiate between physical and logical topology with one IoT example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- (1 mark) **Network topology** is the arrangement of nodes and the communication links that interconnect them in an IoT deployment.
- (1 mark) **Physical topology** describes the actual hardware layout (cabling, wireless channels, device placement).
- (1 mark) **Logical topology** describes how data flows. Example: A physically star-wired Ethernet network operating logically as a bus (all share the medium via CSMA/CD). In IoT: A physically star-wired BLE network can logically behave as a mesh (BLE Mesh) via software flooding.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Mention any **three advantages** of mesh topology in IoT deployments.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Self-healing capability:** If one node/link fails, alternate paths are auto-selected.
2. **Extended range via multi-hop:** Devices can relay data, eliminating dead zones in large areas (smart farms, mining).
3. **High fault tolerance and reliability:** No single point of failure; supports redundant paths.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### 📝 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Star**, **Mesh**, and **Tree** topologies. Compare them based on **number of links**, **scalability**, and **fault tolerance**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A smart-home IoT deployment uses a hybrid topology — a star segment with **1 gateway and 8 devices** and a partial mesh segment with **6 sensor nodes, each having 3 active links**. Calculate the **total number of links**, identify the **network diameter** of the mesh, and recommend **one improvement** to increase fault tolerance. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Part Solution:**

- **Star Topology:** Diagram already shown above. Hub at center; devices at spokes. $L = N-1$. Scalability limited by hub capacity; single point of failure. *(2 marks)*
- **Mesh Topology:** Every node connects to multiple peers. $L = N(N-1)/2$ (full). Highly scalable via multi-hop; no single point of failure. *(2 marks)*
- **Tree Topology:** Hierarchical root → routers → end devices. $L = (b^d - 1)/(b-1)$. Segments easily; root remains a single point of failure. *(2 marks)*
- **Comparison Table:** *(1 mark)*

| Criterion | Star | Mesh | Tree |
|---|---|---|---|
| Links | $N-1$ | $N(N-1)/2$ | $(b^d - 1)/(b-1)$ |
| Scalability | Low | Very High | High |
| Fault Tolerance | Low (hub = PoF) | Excellent | Moderate (root = PoF) |

**(b) Part Solution:**

**Step 1 — Star link count:**

$$L_{\text{star}} = 8 - 1 = 7 \text{ links}$$

**Step 2 — Partial mesh link count:**

$$L_{\text{mesh}} = \frac{k \cdot N}{2} = \frac{3 \times 6}{2} = 9 \text{ links}$$

**Step 3 — Total hybrid links:**

$$L_{\text{total}} = 7 + 9 = 16 \text{ links}$$

**Step 4 — Network diameter of partial mesh:**
For 6 nodes with avg 3 links/node, the mesh is dense but not full. Estimated diameter via max-hop BFS = **2 hops**.

**Step 5 — Improvement recommendation:**
Add a **redundant gateway** in the star segment and **enable self-healing routing (RPL/LOADng)** in the mesh segment — eliminates single-point-of-failure risk. *(1 mark)*

---

#### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain **Bus** and **Ring** topologies with diagrams. For each, state **two IoT use-cases** and one limitation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An industrial IoT plant has **25 sensor nodes** distributed in a full-mesh fashion and **15 actuator nodes** in a star around a central controller. Determine: (i) the total number of links, (ii) the topology most prone to a single point of failure, and (iii) the number of links that must fail simultaneously to **completely disconnect** the mesh. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Part Solution:**

- **Bus Topology:** Diagram already shown above. Single backbone; all devices tap into it. Use-cases: CAN-bus in vehicles, Modbus in legacy SCADA. Limitation: Single backbone failure halts the network. *(2 marks)*
- **Ring Topology:** Diagram already shown above. Each device connects to two neighbors; data circulates unidirectionally or bidirectionally. Use-cases: Token-Ring legacy, fiber-ring metro networks, industrial Profibus (logical ring). Limitation: One link break can isolate a segment unless dual-ring. *(2 marks)*
- **Two IoT use-cases each** clearly stated. *(2 marks)*
- **One limitation each** clearly stated. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Part Solution:**

**(i) Total number of links:**

Full mesh (25 nodes):

$$L_{\text{mesh}} = \frac{25 \times 24}{2} = 300 \text{ links}$$

Star (15 nodes around controller):

$$L_{\text{star}} = 15 - 1 = 14 \text{ links}$$

Total:

$$L_{\text{total}} = 300 + 14 = 314 \text{ links}$$

**(ii) Topology prone to single point of failure:**
The **star** (actuator segment) — its central controller's failure severs all 14 actuator links. The mesh has no such single critical node. *(2 marks)*

**(iii) Links to disconnect mesh completely:**
In a full mesh of 25 nodes, each node has degree 24. To isolate a single node, all 24 of its links must fail. By vertex-cut analysis, the **minimum edge cut to disconnect the graph** equals its minimum vertex cut (Menger's theorem). For a complete graph $K_N$:

$$\text{Edge Connectivity} = N - 1 = 24$$

So at least **24 links** must fail simultaneously to disconnect a node from the rest of the mesh. *(2 marks)*

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on Topology questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract 1** when computing links in a star → writing $L = N$ instead of $N - 1$. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing depth vs levels** in tree topology → using $d$ instead of $d-1$ levels of internal links. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Treating logical topology as physical** when the question asks for physical, or vice versa. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 4. **Not labelling diagrams** — KTU examiners award partial marks for diagrams; unlabelled diagrams fetch zero. Always label **Hub, Sensor, Gateway, Actuator**.
> 5. **Skipping the "real-world IoT protocol"** when explaining a topology. KTU 2024 expects **protocol-mapping** (e.g., "Mesh ← Zigbee"). Failing this loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Wrong formula for partial mesh:** Students often apply full mesh formula. Use $L = kN/2$ where $k$ = avg links/node.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ Topology = **arrangement of nodes + links** (physical & logical layers).
- ✅ **6 topologies to master:** Point-to-Point, Star, Ring, Bus, Tree, Mesh (+ Hybrid).
- ✅ Link counts: Star $N-1$, Ring $N$, Bus $1+(N-1)$, Full Mesh $N(N-1)/2$, Tree $(b^d-1)/(b-1)$.
- ✅ **Single Point of Failure:** Star (hub), Tree (root), Ring (one link break), Bus (backbone break). Mesh is the only fully-redundant option.
- ✅ **Diameter:** Star = 2, Full Mesh = 1, Tree = $2d$, Ring = $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor$.
- ✅ **IoT Protocol Mapping:** Wi-Fi/BT Classic → Star; Zigbee/Thread/BLE Mesh → Mesh; LoRaWAN → Tree; CAN/Modbus → Bus.
- ✅ **Hybrid (Star-of-Mesh)** = industry-standard IoT blueprint.
- ✅ **Self-healing, multi-hop, fault tolerance** = Mesh advantages.
- ✅ Always draw a **labelled** diagram — even in 3-mark answers.
- ✅ For partial-mesh numericals, use $L = kN/2$, NOT $N(N-1)/2$.
- ✅ Mesh edge-connectivity of $K_N$ = $N-1$ (Menger's theorem).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NB-IoT - Network Layer - The Next Generation: IP-Based Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT755 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** NB-IoT - Network Layer - The Next Generation: IP-Based Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NB-IoT (Narrowband-Internet of Things)** is a **3GPP Release 13** standardized **Low Power Wide Area Network (LPWAN)** radio technology designed to enable efficient cellular communication for a massive number of low-throughput, delay-tolerant IoT devices. It occupies a **180 kHz** bandwidth resource block within the LTE spectrum and supports three deployment modes: **in-band**, **guard-band**, and **standalone**.

The **Network Layer** in the IoT protocol stack is responsible for **logical addressing, packet routing, fragmentation/reassembly, and end-to-end delivery** of datagrams between heterogeneous devices. For NB-IoT and next-generation IoT systems, this layer is dominated by **IP-based protocols** (primarily **IPv6**) because they provide:

- **Unique addressing** for billions of devices (2¹²⁸ addresses)
- **Open standard interoperability** across manufacturers
- **Seamless integration** with the existing Internet backbone
- **Stateless auto-configuration** via SLAAC

> [!NOTE]
> **Next-Generation IP-Based Protocols** refer to the optimized suite — **IPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL, and CoAP** — engineered specifically for **Low-Power and Lossy Networks (LLNs)** where traditional TCP/IP stacks are computationally and energetically infeasible.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🚚

Imagine the **modern postal system** scaled down to billions of tiny, battery-operated sensor devices:

| Postal System Element | NB-IoT / IP-Based Protocol Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Unique house number (e.g., 4, New Delhi) | **128-bit IPv6 address** (e.g., `2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334`) |
| Postman deciding the best route | **RPL (Routing Protocol for LLNs)** — builds a Destination-Oriented DAG |
| Compressing a parcel to fit in a small box | **6LoWPAN header compression** — shrinks 40-byte IPv6 header to ~3–7 bytes |
| Acknowledgment slip ("Item Delivered") | **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** — uses CON/NON confirmable messages |
| Speed-post vs. registered post | **NB-IoT (LPWAN, low rate)** vs. **LTE-M (higher rate, mobile)** |

A small soil-moisture sensor in a farm, a smart water meter in an apartment, and a tracker on a shipping container — all receive a unique **IPv6 address**, choose optimal paths through **RPL**, compress their traffic using **6LoWPAN**, and exchange lightweight messages using **CoAP**. This is the **next-generation IP-based IoT ecosystem**.

---

## 1.3 Why IP-Based Protocols for NB-IoT?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Driver:** Classical IPv4 (32-bit) cannot address the predicted **75+ billion** IoT devices by 2030. **IPv6 (128-bit)** provides approximately **3.4 × 10³⁸** unique addresses — practically infinite for IoT.

The driving motivations are:
1. **Scalability** — handle massive device densities (up to **52,547 devices per cell** in NB-IoT)
2. **Interoperability** — eliminate proprietary silos (Sigfox, LoRaWAN-only stacks)
3. **End-to-End Security** — leverage IPsec at the network layer
4. **Cloud-Native Integration** — direct communication with AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT
5. **Future-Proofing** — 5G and beyond natively embrace IP-based IoT

---

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Next-Generation IP-Based IoT

```text
Pillar 1: 6LoWPAN  → Adaptation layer (IEEE 802.15.4 ↔ IPv6)
Pillar 2: RPL      → Routing for LLNs (RFC 6550)
Pillar 3: CoAP     → Lightweight RESTful transport (RFC 7252)
```

These three protocols form the **backbone stack** typically running atop NB-IoT's cellular PHY/MAC layer in the next-generation IoT architecture.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The NB-IoT Protocol Stack — Layered View

The NB-IoT protocol stack from bottom to top is structured as follows:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Application Layer:    CoAP / MQTT / LWM2M   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Transport Layer:      UDP (CoAP) / DTLS     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Network Layer:        IPv6 / 6LoWPAN / RPL   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Data-Link Layer:      NB-IoT (LTE-based MAC) │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Physical Layer:       NB-IoT OFDMA / SC-FDMA │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** NB-IoT uses **LTE numerology** at the physical layer (15 kHz subcarrier spacing, QPSK modulation in uplink), but the **upper layers (Network & above) are IP-centric**, making it a true "next-generation IP-based" technology.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of Each Protocol

### 🔹 2.2.1 IPv6 — The Addressing Foundation

- **Address size:** 128 bits (vs. 32 bits in IPv4)
- **Header size:** 40 bytes fixed header
- **Auto-configuration:** Stateless Address Autoconfiguration (SLAAC) via ICMPv6 Router Advertisement
- **Neighbor Discovery Protocol (NDP):** Replaces IPv4 ARP

The IPv6 header format:

```text
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Ver (4) │ Traffic Class (8) │        Flow Label (20)             │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│       Payload Length (16)    │  Next Header (8) │ Hop Limit (8)  │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                  Source Address (128 bits)                       │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│               Destination Address (128 bits)                     │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                  Total: 40 bytes
```

### 🔹 2.2.2 6LoWPAN — The Adaptation Marvel

**6LoWPAN** (RFC 4944, RFC 6282) bridges **IEEE 802.15.4** (max 127-byte frames) and **IPv6** (requires 1280-byte MTU). It performs:

1. **Header compression** — IPHC (IP Header Compression)
2. **Fragmentation & Reassembly** — for packets exceeding 127 bytes
3. **Mesh addressing** — supports multi-hop forwarding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Header Compression Efficiency:** A 40-byte IPv6 + 8-byte UDP header (48 bytes total) can be compressed to as little as **6 bytes** using 6LoWPAN IPHC — a compression ratio of **8:1**.

### 🔹 2.2.3 RPL — Routing for Constrained Devices

**RPL (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks)** is defined in **RFC 6550**. Core concepts:

- **DODAG (Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph):** Logical routing topology rooted at the sink (gateway)
- **Objective Function (OF):** Defines routing metrics (e.g., hop count, ETX, energy)
- **Rank:** Each node's position relative to the root (closer = lower rank)
- **DIO/DAO/DIS messages:** Control plane signaling (DODAG Information Object, Destination Advertisement Object, DODAG Information Solicitation)

### 🔹 2.2.4 CoAP — Constrained Application Protocol

**CoAP (RFC 7252)** is a RESTful transfer protocol designed for **machine-to-machine (M2M)** applications:

- Runs over **UDP** (not TCP) to save overhead
- Uses **GET, POST, PUT, DELETE** methods (HTTP-like)
- Supports **CON (confirmable)**, **NON (non-confirmable)**, **ACK**, **RST** message types
- Built-in **observe** mechanism (pub-sub pattern)
- Typical header: **4 bytes** vs. HTTP's hundreds of bytes

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Protocol | Application Scenario |
|---|---|
| **NB-IoT + IPv6/6LoWPAN** | Smart agriculture soil sensors communicating directly to cloud |
| **RPL** | Multi-hop mesh in industrial monitoring (LLN routing) |
| **CoAP** | RESTful control of smart bulbs, thermostats via `coap://` URIs |
| **Combined stack** | Smart city deployments: parking meters, waste bins, air-quality nodes |

---

## 2.4 ⚡ KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all key formulas, metrics, and boundaries** essential for solving KTU exam problems on NB-IoT and IP-based protocols.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | IPv6 address space | $N_{addr} = 2^{128}$ | ≈ 3.4 × 10³⁸ addresses |
| 2 | IPv4 address space | $N_{addr} = 2^{32}$ | ≈ 4.29 × 10⁹ addresses |
| 3 | IPv6 header size | $H_{IPv6}$ | **40 bytes (fixed)** |
| 4 | UDP header size | $H_{UDP}$ | **8 bytes** |
| 5 | CoAP header size | $H_{CoAP}$ | **4 bytes** (token 0–8 bytes) |
| 6 | IEEE 802.15.4 MTU | $MTU_{802.15.4}$ | **127 bytes** |
| 7 | NB-IoT bandwidth | $BW_{NB-IoT}$ | **180 kHz** (1 LTE PRB) |
| 8 | NB-IoT subcarrier spacing | $\Delta f$ | **15 kHz** |
| 9 | NB-IoT max devices per cell | $D_{max}$ | **52,547** |
| 10 | NB-IoT max coupling loss (MCL) | $MCL_{NB-IoT}$ | **164 dB** |
| 11 | NB-IoT battery life target | $T_{battery}$ | **> 10 years** (5 Wh battery) |
| 12 | NB-IoT peak data rate (DL) | $R_{DL}$ | **~ 26 kbps** |
| 13 | NB-IoT peak data rate (UL) | $R_{UL}$ | **~ 62 kbps** (multi-tone) |
| 14 | 6LoWPAN compression ratio | $CR = \dfrac{H_{original}}{H_{compressed}}$ | Up to **8 : 1** |
| 15 | Max fragments in 6LoWPAN | $F_{max} = 2^{11}$ | **2048 fragments** |
| 16 | Compression efficiency | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{H_{compressed}}{H_{original}}$ | Up to **87.5 %** |
| 17 | CoAP message rate | $T_{CoAP} = \dfrac{1}{R_{UL}}$ | ~ 62 ms per message |
| 18 | RPL rank cost (hop count) | $Rank_{n} = Rank_{parent} + OF(n)$ | Integer ≥ 1 |
| 19 | NB-IoT latency target | $L_{NB-IoT}$ | **< 10 s** (worst case) |
| 20 | IPv6 / 64 prefix subnet size | $S_{/64} = 2^{64}$ | Per link devices |

---

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Smart Metering** (electricity, gas, water): Uses NB-IoT with IPv6/CoAP for periodic readings every 15–60 minutes.
- **Precision Agriculture**: 6LoWPAN mesh sensors report soil pH and moisture via RPL.
- **Asset Tracking**: NB-IoT modules with built-in IPv6 stacks transmit GPS coordinates directly to AWS IoT Core via CoAP.
- **Smart Cities**: Parking sensors use CoAP **Observe** to push real-time availability updates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Mastering these protocols is essential for designing **scalable, secure, and energy-efficient** IoT systems that align with **3GPP**, **IETF (RFC 4944, 6282, 6550, 7252)**, and **ITU-T Y.2060** standards — all directly referenced in the KTU PEICT755 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: IPv6 Header Compression Efficiency

**Problem Statement:** A typical IoT sensor sends a UDP packet over IPv6 over 6LoWPAN. Calculate the compression efficiency when 6LoWPAN IPHC is applied.

### Given:
- IPv6 header size: $H_{IPv6} = 40$ bytes
- UDP header size: $H_{UDP} = 8$ bytes
- Original combined header: $H_{orig} = 48$ bytes
- 6LoWPAN IPHC compressed header (best case, link-local, no fragmentation): $H_{comp} = 6$ bytes

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Define compression ratio**

$$
CR = \frac{H_{orig}}{H_{comp}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$
CR = \frac{48 \text{ bytes}}{6 \text{ bytes}}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate**

$$
CR = 8
$$

So the compression ratio is **8 : 1**.

**Step 4: Define compression efficiency**

$$
\eta = \left( 1 - \frac{H_{comp}}{H_{orig}} \right) \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5: Substitute the values**

$$
\eta = \left( 1 - \frac{6}{48} \right) \times 100\%
$$

**Step 6: Simplify the fraction**

$$
\eta = \left( 1 - 0.125 \right) \times 100\%
$$

**Step 7: Final efficiency**

$$
\eta = 0.875 \times 100\% = 87.5\%
$$

> ✅ **Result:** 6LoWPAN achieves an **87.5 %** header compression efficiency, making IPv6 viable over constrained 127-byte IEEE 802.15.4 frames.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: 6LoWPAN Fragmentation Limit

**Problem Statement:** A sensor generates an IPv6 packet of size 1500 bytes (standard IPv6 MTU). Determine the maximum number of fragments required to transmit via IEEE 802.15.4 with 127-byte frames.

### Given:
- IPv6 packet size: $P_{IPv6} = 1500$ bytes
- IEEE 802.15.4 frame payload: $F_{payload} = 81$ bytes (after MAC header)
- 6LoWPAN fragment header: $H_{frag} = 5$ bytes (1B disp + 4B datagram_tag + offset)

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Available payload per fragment**

$$
P_{fragment} = F_{payload} - H_{frag}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute**

$$
P_{fragment} = 81 - 5 = 76 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3: Number of fragments needed**

$$
N_{frag} = \left\lceil \frac{P_{IPv6}}{P_{fragment}} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 4: Substitute**

$$
N_{frag} = \left\lceil \frac{1500}{76} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 5: Compute**

$$
N_{frag} = \lceil 19.7368 \dots \rceil = 20
$$

**Step 6: Verify against RFC 4944 limit**

$$
N_{frag} \leq 2^{11} = 2048
$$

Since $20 \ll 2048$, the transmission is feasible.

> ✅ **Result:** The 1500-byte IPv6 packet requires **20 fragments** to traverse a 6LoWPAN mesh — well within the RFC-mandated limit of 2048.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: NB-IoT Energy Budget & Battery Life

**Problem Statement:** An NB-IoT water meter transmits one CoAP message of 100 bytes every 6 hours. Estimate the daily energy consumption and battery life given a **3.6 V, 2.4 Ah (8.64 Wh)** battery.

### Given:
- Transmission power: $P_{TX} = 0.5$ W
- Active TX duration per message: $T_{TX} = 2$ s (typical)
- Reception power: $P_{RX} = 0.1$ W
- Active RX duration: $T_{RX} = 1$ s
- Sleep power: $P_{sleep} = 0.00003$ W (30 µW)
- Messages per day: $M = \dfrac{24}{6} = 4$
- Battery capacity: $E_{bat} = 8.64$ Wh

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Energy per TX event**

$$
E_{TX} = P_{TX} \times T_{TX}
$$

$$
E_{TX} = 0.5 \times 2 = 1.0 \text{ Wh per message}
$$

**Step 2: Energy per RX event**

$$
E_{RX} = P_{RX} \times T_{RX}
$$

$$
E_{RX} = 0.1 \times 1 = 0.1 \text{ Wh per message}
$$

**Step 3: Total active energy per day**

$$
E_{active} = M \times (E_{TX} + E_{RX})
$$

$$
E_{active} = 4 \times (1.0 + 0.1)
$$

$$
E_{active} = 4 \times 1.1 = 4.4 \text{ Wh/day}
$$

**Step 4: Sleep energy per day (23 h 54 min)**

$$
T_{sleep} = 24 - M \times (T_{TX} + T_{RX}) = 24 - 4 \times 3 = 12 \text{ hours (worst case)}
$$

$$
E_{sleep} = P_{sleep} \times T_{sleep}
$$

$$
E_{sleep} = 0.00003 \times 12 = 0.00036 \text{ Wh/day}
$$

**Step 5: Total daily energy**

$$
E_{day} = E_{active} + E_{sleep}
$$

$$
E_{day} = 4.4 + 0.00036 \approx 4.40036 \text{ Wh/day}
$$

**Step 6: Battery life in days**

$$
L_{days} = \frac{E_{bat}}{E_{day}}
$$

$$
L_{days} = \frac{8.64}{4.40036} \approx 1.96 \text{ days}
$$

> ⚠️ **Engineering Reality Check:** With continuous 2-second transmissions, battery life is short. NB-IoT's **PSM (Power Saving Mode)** and **eDRX (extended Discontinuous Reception)** reduce duty cycle dramatically, enabling **> 10-year** battery life in practice.

**Step 7: Realistic eDRX-enabled lifetime**

If the radio is in **deep sleep 99.9 %** of the time, $T_{TX}$ effective drops to ~2 ms every 6 hours due to **PSM/eDRX**. New effective active time per day:

$$
T_{active,eff} = 4 \times 0.002 = 0.008 \text{ s/day}
$$

$$
E_{active,eff} = 0.5 \times 0.008 = 0.004 \text{ Wh/day}
$$

$$
L_{days} = \frac{8.64}{0.004} = 2160 \text{ days} \approx 5.92 \text{ years}
$$

> ✅ **Result:** With **PSM/eDRX**, an NB-IoT water meter can achieve **~ 6 years** battery life — approaching the 10-year target under more aggressive duty cycling.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: RPL Rank Calculation in DODAG

**Problem Statement:** A DODAG has a root at rank 0. Node A joins through a parent of rank 256, then Node B joins through Node A. Compute B's rank using the **Minimum Rank with Hysteresis Objective Function (MRHOF)** with default step size **256**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Node A's rank**

$$
Rank_A = Rank_{parent} + Step
$$

$$
Rank_A = 256 + 256 = 512
$$

**Step 2: Node B's rank**

$$
Rank_B = Rank_A + Step
$$

$$
Rank_B = 512 + 256 = 768
$$

**Step 3: Hop count from root**

$$
Hops_B = \frac{Rank_B}{Step} = \frac{768}{256} = 3 \text{ hops}
$$

> ✅ **Result:** Node B sits **3 hops** from the DODAG root with rank **768**.

---

## 3.5 📊 Analytical Comparison Matrix: IP-Based IoT Protocols

| Feature | IPv6 | 6LoWPAN | RPL | CoAP |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Layer** | Network | Adaptation (Network–Link) | Network (Routing) | Application |
| **RFC** | RFC 8200 | RFC 4944 / 6282 | RFC 6550 | RFC 7252 |
| **Header size** | 40 B | 2–7 B (compressed) | Variable | 4 B |
| **Topology** | Any | Star / Mesh | DODAG (Tree/Graph) | Client–Server |
| **Transport** | — | — | ICMPv6 | UDP |
| **Addressing** | 128-bit | Derived from IPv6 | IPv6 + Rank | IPv6 + URI |
| **Security** | IPsec | Link-layer | RPL AUTH | DTLS |
| **Energy profile** | High (raw) | Low | Low | Very Low |
| **Use case** | Backbone | Constrained links | LLN routing | RESTful M2M |
| **Maturity** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
