# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the role of the 6LoWPAN adaptation layer in enabling IPv6 over IEEE 802.15.4 networks. List its two primary functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**6LoWPAN (IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks)** is an adaptation layer defined in **RFC 4944** and **RFC 6282** that enables IPv6 packets to be carried over IEEE 802.15.4 links, which have a maximum frame size of **127 bytes** (with only **81 bytes** of usable payload after MAC overhead) — far smaller than the **1280-byte MTU** required by IPv6.

Its two primary functions are:

1. **Header Compression** — Through the **IPHC (IP Header Compression)** mechanism, 6LoWPAN compresses the 40-byte IPv6 header and 8-byte UDP header down to as little as **2–7 bytes** combined, achieving compression ratios of up to **8:1**. This is critical because the IPv6 addresses and many header fields can be inferred from the link-local context.

2. **Fragmentation and Reassembly** — When an IPv6 packet exceeds the 127-byte IEEE 802.15.4 frame size, 6LoWPAN splits it into smaller link-layer fragments, each identified by a **datagram_tag** and an **offset** field. Up to **2048 fragments** can be sent per datagram, then reassembled at the destination.

*(Marks distribution: 1.5 for explanation, 1 for function 1, 0.5 for function 2.)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is RPL? Briefly explain the concept of a DODAG and the function of the Objective Function (OF).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**RPL (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks)** is an IETF-defined distance-vector routing protocol specified in **RFC 6550**, designed specifically for LLNs (Low-Power and Lossy Networks) used in IoT deployments.

- **DODAG (Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph):** RPL organizes nodes into a logical tree-like graph rooted at a single sink/border router. Each node selects a parent based on routing metrics, and routes are built **upward** (toward the root) for data traffic and **downward** (using source routes or DAO-stored paths) for control/configuration traffic.

- **Objective Function (OF):** The OF defines the **routing metrics and constraints** used by nodes to choose parents and compute their rank. Examples include:
  - **MRHOF (Minimum Rank with Hysteresis Objective Function)** — uses ETX (Expected Transmission Count)
  - **OF0 (Objective Function Zero)** — uses hop count
  The OF allows the network to be optimized for **energy efficiency, latency, or reliability**.

*(Marks distribution: 1 for RPL definition, 1 for DODAG, 1 for Objective Function.)*

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### 🔵 **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### **(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the NB-IoT protocol stack and explain the functions of each layer in detail. **(7 marks)**

#### **(b)** Explain the architecture of 6LoWPAN. How does header compression work? Derive the compression efficiency for a UDP/IPv6 packet. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q-A:**

### **Part (a): NB-IoT Protocol Stack (7 marks)**

The **NB-IoT protocol stack** is derived from LTE but is heavily optimized for IoT, with IP-centric upper layers enabling seamless internet integration.

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Application: CoAP / LWM2M / MQTT-SN  │  ← Device & data management
├────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Transport:   UDP + DTLS              │  ← Lightweight reliability
├────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Network:     IPv6 / 6LoWPAN / RPL    │  ← Addressing & routing
├────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Data Link:   NB-IoT MAC (LTE-based)  │  ← Radio scheduling
├────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Physical:    OFDMA / SC-FDMA (180kHz)│  ← Radio transmission
└────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Layer Functions:**

1. **Physical Layer:** Uses **OFDMA** in downlink and **SC-FDMA** in uplink with **15 kHz** subcarrier spacing. Operates within a **180 kHz** band. Supports QPSK modulation. Enables coverage enhancement via **repetition** (up to 128×) for an MCL of **164 dB**.

2. **Data Link (MAC) Layer:** Based on LTE MAC but simplified. Manages **PRACH (Physical Random Access)** with NPRACH (Narrowband PRACH), HARQ, and **RRC (Radio Resource Control)** connection setup. Supports **eDRX** and **PSM** for power saving.

3. **Network Layer:** Implements **IPv6** for addressing, **6LoWPAN** for header compression/fragmentation, and **RPL** for multi-hop routing in mesh deployments. Uses **ICMPv6** for control messages.

4. **Transport Layer:** Primarily **UDP** (used by CoAP) rather than TCP to minimize overhead. **DTLS** provides mutual authentication and encryption equivalent to TLS for UDP.

5. **Application Layer:** **CoAP** for RESTful interactions, **LWM2M** for device management (firmware updates, sensor reading), **MQTT-SN** for publish-subscribe messaging.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for diagram, 5 for layer-wise explanation.)*

---

### **Part (b): 6LoWPAN Architecture & Header Compression (7 marks)**

#### Architecture:
6LoWPAN consists of three sub-functional blocks:
- **Mesh Addressing** — handles multi-hop delivery
- **Header Compression (IPHC)** — shrinks headers
- **Fragmentation/Reassembly** — splits oversized packets

#### Header Compression Working:
The **IPHC encoding** (RFC 6282) uses 2-byte dispatch fields where each bit toggles between inline and elided (assumed) values:
- **Version (4 bits):** Always 6 (IPv6)
- **Traffic Class & Flow Label:** Often elided
- **Next Header:** Compressed to 2 bits (NHC) for UDP/TCP
- **Hop Limit:** Often elided (set to default 64)
- **Source/Destination Address:** Inline bits indicate how much of the 128-bit address is shared with the link-local prefix (8/16/64 bits)

For **UDP**, the NHC further compresses the 8-byte header to 4 bytes by eliding the checksum (optional) and length (derivable from MAC).

#### Derivation:

**Step 1:** Original header = 40 bytes (IPv6) + 8 bytes (UDP) = 48 bytes.

**Step 2:** Compressed header (IPHC + NHC, link-local, no checksum) = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6 bytes.

**Step 3:** Compression Ratio

$$
CR = \frac{H_{orig}}{H_{comp}} = \frac{48}{6} = 8
$$

**Step 4:** Efficiency

$$
\eta = \left( 1 - \frac{6}{48} \right) \times 100\% = 87.5\%
$$

*(Marks distribution: 2 for architecture, 2 for compression explanation, 3 for derivation.)*

---

### 🟢 **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **(a)** Explain the architecture of RPL with a DODAG diagram. Discuss the four RPL control message types and their roles. **(7 marks)**

#### **(b)** Describe the Constrained Application Protocol (CoAP) with its message format. Compare CoAP with HTTP and explain why CoAP is preferred in NB-IoT scenarios. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q-B:**

### **Part (a): RPL Architecture & Control Messages (7 marks)**

#### RPL Architecture:
RPL organizes the network into a **DODAG (Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph)** rooted at the border router (sink). Each node maintains:
- A set of **parents** (potential next-hops toward root)
- A **rank** (distance from root)
- A **DODAG ID** and **RPL Instance ID**

The **Objective Function (OF)** decides the best parent and rank.

#### DODAG Diagram:
```
          [Root - Rank 0]
           /          \
    [Node A R=256]  [Node B R=256]
      /    \             \
[R=512] [R=512]       [R=512]
```

#### Four RPL Control Messages:

1. **DIO (DODAG Information Object):** Sent **downward** from root to advertise DODAG parameters (RPLInstanceID, DODAGID, Rank, OF). Used to build and maintain the upward routing topology.

2. **DAO (Destination Advertisement Object):** Sent **upward** from child to parent to advertise the node's prefix and reachable destinations. This enables **downward** routing (root → leaves) by storing source routes at intermediate nodes (Storing mode) or in the root (Non-Storing mode).

3. **DIS (DODAG Information Solicitation):** Sent **upward** by a newly joining node to solicit DIO messages from neighbors — equivalent to a "join request."

4. **DAO-ACK:** Sent **downward** by the parent to confirm successful DAO registration.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for architecture, 2 for diagram, 3 for message types.)*

---

### **Part (b): CoAP Protocol & HTTP Comparison (7 marks)**

#### CoAP Message Format:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Ver (2) │ Type (2) │ TKL (4) │   Code (8)   │ Msg ID (16)│   ← 4 bytes
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                  Token (0–8 bytes)                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│              Options (variable, TLV-encoded)             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│               Payload (0+ bytes, marked by 0xFF)         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

- **Ver:** Always 1
- **Type:** CON, NON, ACK, RST (2 bits)
- **TKL:** Token Length (0–8 bytes)
- **Code:** Class (3 bits) + Detail (5 bits), e.g., `2.05` = Content
- **Message ID:** 16-bit for matching ACK/RST

#### CoAP vs. HTTP Comparison:

| Feature | HTTP | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | TCP | UDP |
| Header size | Hundreds of bytes (textual) | 4 bytes (binary) |
| Reliability | Built-in (TCP ACK) | Optional via CON/ACK |
| Methods | GET, POST, PUT, DELETE | Same (semantically) |
| Multicast | Not supported | Supported via NON |
| Observe | Polling only | Built-in push |
| Power use | High | Very Low |
| Suitability for LLNs | Poor | Excellent |

#### Why CoAP is Preferred for NB-IoT:
1. **UDP-based** — no TCP handshake overhead, suitable for bursty, low-rate traffic.
2. **4-byte header** — fits within compressed 6LoWPAN frames.
3. **Confirmable messages** — provides reliability over lossy cellular links without TCP cost.
4. **Observe pattern** — enables server push for sensor readings without polling.
5. **Resource directory & discovery** — natively suited to dynamic IoT ecosystems.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for format, 3 for comparison, 2 for justification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing 6LoWPAN with 6Lo.** 6LoWPAN is **IPv6 over Low-Power WPAN**, NOT a separate network. Always mention the RFC number (**RFC 4944/6282**) for full marks.
> 2. **Writing only "CoAP is HTTP for IoT."** Examiners expect specific details — UDP transport, 4-byte header, CON/NON/ACK types. Vague answers fetch only 50% marks.
> 3. **Skipping the derivation in 6LoWPAN compression questions.** Even if the answer is "87.5 %", students lose 2 marks if the *compression ratio* and *efficiency formula* are not shown.
> 4. **Wrong DODAG direction.** RPL builds **upward** routes via DIO/DAO exchange. Mixing "DIO goes up, DAO goes down" will cost 1.5 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting PSM/eDRX in NB-IoT.** When asked about battery life, simply stating ">10 years" without explaining **Power Saving Mode** and **extended DRX** is incomplete. Examiners look for the energy-saving mechanism.
> 6. **No units in numerical answers.** Always state units (bytes, kbps, dB, Wh, years).

---

## 5.3 ✅ Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 High-Density Summary — Read this 30 minutes before your KTU exam!**

- [x] **NB-IoT** is a 3GPP LPWAN technology using **180 kHz** bandwidth and **15 kHz** subcarrier spacing, supporting up to **52,547 devices/cell** and **164 dB MCL**.
- [x] **Next-Generation IP-Based Stack** = **IPv6 + 6LoWPAN + RPL + CoAP** (over UDP + DTLS).
- [x] **IPv6** has 128-bit addresses and a fixed 40-byte header — addressing scalability for billions of IoT devices.
- [x] **6LoWPAN** (RFC 4944, 6282) provides **header compression** (40B+8B → 2–7B) and **fragmentation** (up to 2048 fragments).
- [x] **6LoWPAN compression efficiency** for IPv6+UDP = **87.5 %** (ratio 8:1).
- [x] **RPL** (RFC 6550) builds a **DODAG** using four control messages: **DIO, DAO, DIS, DAO-ACK**.
- [x] **Objective Functions (OF)** define routing metrics — **OF0** (hop count), **MRHOF** (ETX).
- [x] **CoAP** (RFC 7252) uses **4-byte headers**, runs over **UDP**, supports **CON/NON/ACK/RST** types, and offers **Observe** for server push.
- [x] **NB-IoT battery life target**: **> 10 years**, achieved via **PSM (Power Saving Mode)** and **eDRX (extended Discontinuous Reception)**.
- [x] **NB-IoT peak rates**: **~26 kbps DL, ~62 kbps UL**.
- [x] **DTLS** (Datagram TLS) provides UDP-equivalent security to TLS — essential for CoAP.
- [x] **3GPP Release 13** = NB-IoT standardization; **Release 15+** adds **NB-IoT in 5G** with enhanced features.
- [x] **SCEF (Service Capability Exposure Function)** delivers non-IP data efficiently for NB-IoT in EPC.
- [x] **IEEE 802.15.4** MAC frame = **127 bytes**; 6LoWPAN IPHC fits IPv6 within this constraint.
- [x] **KTU Hot Topics** likely to appear: 6LoWPAN IPHC compression derivation, RPL DODAG + control messages, CoAP vs HTTP comparison, NB-IoT power-saving mechanisms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Raspberry Pi - Raspberry Pi Interfaces (Serial, I2C)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT755 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Raspberry Pi - Raspberry Pi Interfaces (Serial, I2C) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Introduction to Raspberry Pi Hardware Interfaces The **Raspberry Pi** is a single-board computer (SBC) widely used as an IoT edge node. It exposes its peripherals through the **40-pin GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output) header**, which supports multiple serial communication protocols. Two of the most important low-level hardware interfaces used to connect sensors, actuators, and embedded peripherals to the Pi are **Serial (UART)** and **I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** > The 2024 Scheme expects students to identify the correct pins, describe the electrical characteristics (open-drain, push-pull, pull-ups), frame/address formats, and the Linux device files (`/dev/ttyAMA0`, `/dev/i2c-1`) used by the Raspberry Pi OS for these interfaces. ## 1.2 Serial (UART) — Formal Definition **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter)** is an asynchronous, full-duplex, point-to-point serial communication protocol. Two dedicated lines are used: a **Transmit (TX)** line and a **Receive (RX)** line. Because there is **no shared clock line**, both ends must agree beforehand on a fixed **baud rate** (symbols per second). ### Conceptual Analogy — "Walkie-Talkie Without a Clock Tower" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Imagine two people speaking through a walkie-talkie. There is no central clock ticking to mark "now you talk, now I talk". They simply agree *beforehand* on a rule — speak at 9600 syllables per minute, and listen for silence before speaking. UART works exactly this way: both devices are pre-configured to the same **baud rate, parity, and stop bits** before any byte is sent. If even one of these parameters mismatches, the received byte becomes garbled — like hearing words at the wrong speed. ## 1.3 I2C — Formal Definition **I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit)**, developed by Philips Semiconductor (now NXP) in 1982, is a **synchronous, half-duplex, multi-master, multi-slave** serial bus. It uses only **two bidirectional open-drain lines**: - **SDA (Serial Data)** — carries the data - **SCL (Serial Clock)** — carries the clock generated by the master ### Conceptual Analogy — "Classroom Roll Call" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Picture a strict classroom where one teacher (the **master**) controls the clock (SCL). Before any student speaks, the teacher calls a specific student's roll number (the **slave address**). Only that student replies with their answer on the shared talk line (SDA). All other students remain silent (held in a high-impedance state). The teacher then releases the clock line and the next student gets a turn. This is exactly how I2C arbitrates and addresses up to **127 devices** on just two wires. ## 1.4 Physical Pin Mapping on Raspberry Pi GPIO Header | Interface | Pin Function | BCM GPIO Number | Physical Pin # | Direction on Pi | |---|---|---|---|---| | **UART** | TXD (Transmit) | GPIO 14 | Pin 8 | Output | | **UART** | RXD (Receive) | GPIO 15 | Pin 10 | Input | | **I2C** | SDA (Data) | GPIO 2 | Pin 3 | Bidirectional | | **I2C** | SCL (Clock) | GPIO 3 | Pin 5 | Bidirectional | | Power | 3.3 V | — | Pin 1, 17 | — | | Power | 5 V | — | Pin 2, 4 | — | | Ground | GND | — | Pin 6, 9, … | — | > [!WARNING] > **Raspberry Pi GPIO Voltage Level = 3.3 V.** Connecting a 5 V UART or I2C device directly to the Pi pins without a logic-level shifter will permanently damage the Broadcom SoC. This is a very common practical pitfall in lab sessions. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 UART — Theoretical Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Frame Format A UART frame transmits **one character** at a time. The structure, from line-idle to line-idle, is: | Bit Position | Field | Width | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Start Bit** | 1 bit | Always `0` — marks the beginning of a frame | | 2 | **Data Bits** | 5, 6, 7, or **8** bits | The actual payload (LSB first) | | 3 | **Parity Bit** | 0 or 1 bit | Optional error detection (Even/Odd) | | 4 | **Stop Bit(s)** | 1, 1.5, or 2 bits | Always `1` — returns line to idle | ``` Idle ────┐ ┌─D0─┐ ┌D1┐ ┌D2┐ ┌D3┐ ┌D4┐ ┌D5┐ ┌D6─┐ ┌D7─┐ ┌─P─┐ ┌─Stop─┐ 1 | 0 | 0 | … | … | … | … | … | … | 0 | 1 | P | 1 | Idle Start LSB MSB Parity ``` ### 2.1.2 Key UART Parameters - **Baud Rate:** Symbols per second. Standard values: **9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200**. - **Full-Duplex:** TX and RX operate independently and simultaneously. - **Asynchronous:** No clock line; transmitter and receiver must be pre-configured identically. ### 2.1.3 Raspberry Pi UART Implementation The Broadcom BCM2837/BCM2711 SoC contains **two UART peripherals**: a full-featured **PL011** (`/dev/ttyAMA0`) and a mini-UART (`/dev/ttyS0`). On Raspberry Pi 3 and later, `/dev/ttyS0` is mapped to the Bluetooth module, so the **GPIO 14/15 pins are served by `/dev/ttyAMA0`**. > [!IMPORTANT] > To enable the serial console (or hardware UART) on `/dev/ttyAMA0`, you must disable the Linux serial console via `raspi-config` → *Interface Options* → *Serial Port*. KTU lab questions frequently test this configuration step. ### 2.1.4 UART Error Sources (for KTU theory) 1. **Baud-rate mismatch** → frame error, garbled bytes. 2. **Overshoot / undershoot** at high baud (>115200) due to cable capacitance. 3. **Voltage mismatch** (3.3 V vs. 5 V TTL). ## 2.2 I2C — Theoretical Breakdown ### 2.2.1 Electrical Characteristics - **Open-Drain (open-collector) drivers:** Devices can only *pull the line low* (to GND) or *release* it. They **cannot drive the line high**. - **Pull-up resistors:** External resistors (typically **4.7 kΩ** to **10 kΩ**) connect SDA and SCL to **Vcc (3.3 V on Pi)** to pull the released line to logic `1`. - This architecture allows **wired-AND** arbitration and **hot-swapping** of slaves safely. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Open-drain is essential because it prevents "bus contention" when two devices try to drive the line at once — the line stays low (the dominant state) until both release, naturally resolving collisions. This is what enables multi-master I2C. ### 2.2.2 I2C Frame Format A complete I2C transaction consists of: 1. **Start Condition (S):** SDA falls while SCL is HIGH. 2. **Address Byte (7 bits):** Identifies the slave (e.g., 0x68 for DS1307 RTC). 3. **R/W# bit:** `1` = read, `0` = write. 4. **ACK/NACK bit:** Slave pulls SDA LOW to acknowledge. 5. **Data Byte(s):** 8 bits each, each followed by an ACK. 6. **Stop Condition (P):** SDA rises while SCL is HIGH. ``` S Address[6:0] R/W ACK D7...D0 ACK P SDA: ‾\__/¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯\____/¯¯\___/¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯\___/¯¯¯¯¯¯\__/‾‾‾ SCL: __/‾‾\__/‾‾\__/‾‾\__/‾‾\__/‾‾\__/‾‾\__/‾‾\__/‾‾\__/‾‾ Start Repeated Start Stop ``` ### 2.2.3 Addressing - **7-bit addressing** (most common): Up to **2⁷ = 128** addresses; address **0x00** is reserved for General Call, so effectively **127 usable slaves**. - **10-bit addressing**: For larger buses; prefixed with `11110xx`. ### 2.2.4 Raspberry Pi I2C Implementation - The Pi's I2C bus is exposed as `/dev/i2c-1` on most modern Pi models. - Linux kernel modules: `i2c-dev` and `i2c-bcm2835`. - Enable via `raspi-config` → *Interface Options* → *I2C*. - Detect slaves using: `sudo i2cdetect -y 1` ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Value | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | UART bit time | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | seconds | | 2 | UART frame time | $T_{frame} = (1 + N_{data} + N_{parity} + N_{stop}) \times T_{bit}$ | seconds | | 3 | UART throughput | $R = \text{Baud Rate} \times N_{data}$ (no parity, 1 stop) | bits/sec | | 4 | I2C max slaves (7-bit) | $N_{slaves} = 2^{7} - 1 = 127$ | devices | | 5 | I2C pull-up resistance | $R_{pullup(min)} = \dfrac{V_{cc} - V_{OL(max)}}{I_{OL}}$ | ohms | | 6 | I2C pull-up resistance (max, by bus capacitance) | $R_{pullup(max)} = \dfrac{t_{r}}{0.8473 \times C_{bus}}$ | ohms | | 7 | I2C bus capacitance limit | $C_{bus} \le 400 \text{ pF}$ (Standard/Fast mode) | farads | | 8 | Standard-mode I2C speed | 100 kbit/s | — | | 9 | Fast-mode I2C speed | 400 kbit/s | — | | 10 | Fast-mode Plus I2C speed | 1 Mbit/s | — | | 11 | UART line voltage (Pi) | 3.3 V LVTTL | volts | | 12 | I2C SDA/SCL idle level | Vcc (pulled high by R_pullup) | volts | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize these for KTU exams:** > - UART = asynchronous, no clock, point-to-point, full-duplex, TX/RX. > - I2C = synchronous, has clock, multi-drop, half-duplex, open-drain, SDA/SCL, needs pull-ups. ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **UART** is the de-facto interface for GPS modules (NMEA 0183 at 9600 baud), GSM modems (AT commands), Bluetooth HC-05/HC-06 modules, and firmware debug consoles. - **I2C** dominates in low-speed sensor networks: temperature (LM75), RTC (DS3231), EEPROM (AT24C256), accelerometers (MPU6050), and OLED displays (SSD1306) — all coexist on just two wires. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — UART Transmission Time for a Single Frame ### Problem A UART transmits data with the following configuration: **9600 baud, 8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit**. Compute the time required to send the ASCII character `'A'` (0x41). ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** Identify the number of bits in one UART frame. A standard UART frame consists of: - 1 start bit - 8 data bits - 0 parity bit (given: no parity) - 1 stop bit (given) Total bits per frame: $$ N_{frame} = 1 + N_{data} + N_{parity} + N_{stop} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute the known values. $$ \begin{aligned} N_{frame} &= 1 + 8 + 0 + 1 \\ &= 10 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Compute the bit duration (time per bit). $$ \begin{aligned} T_{bit} &= \frac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}} \\ &= \frac{1}{9600} \\ &\approx 104.1667 \;\mu s \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Compute the total frame transmission time. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{frame} &= N_{frame} \times T_{bit} \\ &= 10 \times \frac{1}{9600} \\ &= \frac{10}{9600} \\ &\approx 1.0417 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Effective throughput in bytes/second. Since each frame carries **1 useful data byte**, the number of full frames per second is: $$ \begin{aligned} f_{frames} &= \frac{1}{T_{frame}} = \frac{\text{Baud Rate}}{N_{frame}} \\ &= \frac{9600}{10} \\ &= 960 \text{ frames/second} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, effective throughput: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Throughput} &= 960 \text{ bytes/sec} \\ &= 960 \times 8 = 7680 \text{ bits/sec (useful payload)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6:** Efficiency comparison. The efficiency of the link (useful bits ÷ total transmitted bits) is: $$ \eta_{UART} = \frac{N_{data}}{N_{frame}} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8 = 80\% $$ ### Generalised Formula For any UART configuration, the throughput in **bytes per second** is: $$ R_{bytes} = \frac{\text{Baud Rate}}{1 + N_{data} + N_{parity} + N_{stop}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Many questions give a baud rate + frame format and ask "how long to transmit N bytes?" or "what is the efficiency?". Always compute $N_{frame}$ first, then apply the formula above. --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Minimum I2C Pull-up Resistor Value ### Problem An I2C bus on the Raspberry Pi operates at Vcc = **3.3 V** in Standard mode (100 kHz). The slave's open-drain transistor has $V_{OL(max)} = 0.4 \text{ V}$ and can sink $I_{OL(max)} = 3 \text{ mA}$. Compute the **minimum pull-up resistor value**. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** When the slave pulls SDA LOW, the pull-up resistor must drop the remaining voltage: $$ V_{R} = V_{cc} - V_{OL(max)} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute the values. $$ \begin{aligned} V_{R} &= 3.3 \text{ V} - 0.4 \text{ V} \\ &= 2.9 \text{ V} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Apply Ohm's law to find $R_{pullup(min)}$. $$ \begin{aligned} R_{pullup(min)} &= \frac{V_{R}}{I_{OL(max)}} \\ &= \frac{2.9 \text{ V}}{3 \text{ mA}} \\ &= \frac{2.9}{3 \times 10^{-3}} \\ &\approx 966.67 \;\Omega \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Choose a standard resistor value **above** the minimum. The next standard E12 resistor value is **1 kΩ**, but the commonly used safe choice for the Pi is **4.7 kΩ** or **10 kΩ** — well above $R_{pullup(min)}$, ensuring the current stays below the slave's sink limit. ### Generalised Formula (Standard / Fast Mode) $$ R_{pullup(min)} = \frac{V_{cc} - V_{OL(max)}}{I_{OL(max)}} $$ ### Maximum Pull-up (Rise-Time Constraint) For reliable operation, the RC rise time must be faster than the I2C spec rise time $t_r$ (1000 ns in Standard mode). With maximum bus capacitance $C_{bus} = 400$ pF: $$ \begin{aligned} R_{pullup(max)} &= \frac{t_{r}}{0.8473 \times C_{bus}} \\ &= \frac{1000 \times 10^{-9}}{0.8473 \times 400 \times 10^{-12}} \\ &= \frac{1000 \times 10^{-9}}{338.92 \times 10^{-12}} \\ &\approx 2.95 \text{ k}\Omega \end{aligned} $$ ### Final Range $$ 966 \;\Omega \le R_{pullup} \le 2.95 \text{ k}\Omega $$ For Standard mode, a safe practical value is **2.2 kΩ to 4.7 kΩ**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — UART vs I2C Comparison (High-Yield for KTU) | Parameter | **UART (Serial)** | **I2C** | |---|---|---| | Clock line | None (asynchronous) | SCL (synchronous, master-driven) | | Data lines | TX, RX (separate) | SDA (bidirectional) | | Number of wires (data + clock) | 2 | 2 | | Duplex | Full-duplex | Half-duplex | | Max devices | 2 (point-to-point) | 127 (7-bit addr) or 1024 (10-bit) | | Speed | Up to ~1 Mbaud (Pi PL011) | 100 kHz / 400 kHz / 1 MHz / 3.4 MHz | | Addressing | None (1-to-1 by wire) | 7-bit or 10-bit slave address | | Driver type | Push-pull | Open-drain + pull-up | | Voltage on Pi | **3.3 V** | **3.3 V** | | Linux device file | `/dev/ttyAMA0` | `/dev/i2c-1` | | Typical use | GPS, Bluetooth, console | Sensors, RTC, EEPROM, OLED | | Frame overhead | Start + Stop + optional parity | Start + Address + R/W + ACK + Stop | | Hot-plug tolerance | Poor | Good (wired-AND arbitration) | --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — Reading a Slave Register over I2C ### Problem Write the byte sequence (in hexadecimal) that the master transmits on SDA to **read 1 byte from register `0x07`** of a slave at address `0x68` (e.g., MPU6050 / DS1307). ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** Send a **Start** condition (master initiates). **Step 2:** Transmit the **slave address byte** with R/W# = 0 (write mode) to first select the register. Address byte = `(slave_addr << 1) \vert R/W`: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AddrByte} &= (0x68 \ll 1) \;\vert\; 0 \\ &= 0xD0 \;\vert\; 0 \\ &= \text{0xD0} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Slave returns **ACK** (SDA LOW). **Step 4:** Transmit the **register address** to read from: $$ \text{RegByte} = \text{0x07} $$ **Step 5:** Slave returns **ACK**. **Step 6:** Send a **Repeated Start** to switch direction. **Step 7:** Re-transmit slave address with R/W# = 1 (read mode): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AddrByte} &= (0x68 \ll 1) \;\vert\; 1 \\ &= 0xD0 \;\vert\; 1 \\ &= \text{0xD1} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8:** Slave returns **ACK**, then places the register value on SDA. **Step 9:** Master returns **NACK** (to signal "no more bytes needed"). **Step 10:** Master sends **Stop**. ### Final Byte Sequence on the Wire $$ S \;\; 0xD0 \;\; ACK \;\; 0x07 \;\; ACK \;\; Sr \;\; 0xD1 \;\; ACK \;\; DATA \;\; NACK \;\; P $$ where: - $S$ = Start - $Sr$ = Repeated Start - $ACK/NACK$ = acknowledgement bits from receiver - $P$ = Stop - $0xD0$ = (slave addr 0x68 shifted left + write bit 0) - $0xD1$ = (slave addr 0x68 shifted left + read bit 1) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the left-shift?** Because the standard 7-bit I2C address occupies the **upper 7 bits** of the first transmitted byte; the **LSB is the R/W flag**. Many KTU students forget this shift and answer `0x68` directly — a guaranteed zero. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 UART Point-to-Point Connection (Raspberry Pi ↔ Peripheral) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Raspberry Pi

I2C is preferred for connecting many low-speed peripherals using minimum wiring (e.g., sensors on a board). --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Explain the role of pull-up resistors in an I2C bus. What is the typical range of resistance values used with a Raspberry Pi? **Model Answer:** I2C uses **open-drain drivers**, meaning devices can only pull the SDA/SCL lines **LOW** or **release** them. They cannot drive the line HIGH on their own. Therefore, external **pull-up resistors** (typically **4.7 kΩ to 10 kΩ** for the Raspberry Pi at 3.3 V) connect SDA and SCL to Vcc, ensuring the line returns to a logic HIGH when no device is pulling it low. - **Too small a resistor** → excessive current when line is LOW, exceeding slave sink rating. - **Too large a resistor** → slow rise time, fails I2C timing budget, especially at higher speeds. --- ## PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model Question)]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat timing diagram, explain the **UART frame format** for the transmission of the ASCII character `'K'` (0x4B) using 9600 baud, **8 data bits, even parity, 1 stop bit**. Compute the total frame time and effective throughput. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **I2C protocol** — its bus topology, addressing scheme, start/stop conditions, and the significance of the **ACK/NACK** bit. List two typical I2C devices used with Raspberry Pi. --- ### **Model Answer — Question A (a)** #### Step 1: Frame Structure Given: 8 data bits, even parity, 1 stop bit → total bits per frame: $$ N_{frame} = 1 + 8 + 1 + 1 = 11 \text{ bits} $$ #### Step 2: Bit Duration at 9600 Baud $$ T_{bit} = \frac{1}{9600} \approx 104.17 \;\mu s $$ #### Step 3: Total Frame Time $$ \begin{aligned} T_{frame} &= N_{frame} \times T_{bit} \\ &= 11 \times 104.17 \;\mu s \\ &\approx 1.146 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 4: Throughput $$ \begin{aligned} R_{bytes} &= \frac{9600}{11} \approx 872.7 \text{ bytes/sec} \\ R_{useful} &= 872.7 \times 8 \approx 6982 \text{ bps useful payload} \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 5: ASCII `'K'` = 0x4B = `01001011` (LSB-first on wire → `11010010`) **Even parity** = number of `1`s in data bits (4) is even, so parity bit = **0**. #### Step 6: Wire Sequence (LSB-first transmission) ``` Bit: Start D0 D1 D2 D3 D4 D5 D6 D7 Parity Stop Value: 0 1 1 0 1 0 0 1 0 0 1 ┌──┐ ┌─┐ ┌─┐ ┌─┐ ┌─┐ ┌─┐ ┌───────┐ Idle ──┘ └─┘ └─┘ └─┘ └─┘ └─┘ └───────┘ Idle ``` Where the line transitions only when the bit value differs from the previous one. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to send LSB-first and instead draw the MSB-first sequence, losing 2 marks. UART always transmits **LSB first** by hardware convention. --- ### **Model Answer — Question A (b)** #### I2C Bus Topology - **Two-wire bus**: SDA (data) + SCL (clock). - **Open-drain** with external pull-ups to Vcc. - **Multi-master, multi-slave** — all devices share both lines. #### Addressing Scheme - **7-bit slave address** + 1-bit R/W = first transmitted byte. - Maximum slaves = 2⁷ − 1 = **127** (address 0x00 reserved for General Call). #### Start and Stop Conditions - **Start (S):** SDA falls while SCL is HIGH. - **Stop (P):** SDA rises while SCL is HIGH. - **Repeated Start (Sr):** A new Start issued without a preceding Stop — used to switch direction. #### ACK / NACK - After every byte, the receiver pulls SDA LOW during the 9th clock pulse to **ACK** (acknowledge) — meaning "byte received, ready for next". - If SDA remains HIGH during the 9th clock, it is a **NACK** — meaning "no more data" or "slave not ready". #### Typical I2C Devices for Raspberry Pi 1. **DS3231** — Real-Time Clock (address 0x68). 2. **SSD1306** — 0.96" OLED display (address 0x3C). 3. **MPU6050** — 6-axis IMU (address 0x68). 4. **AT24C256** — I2C EEPROM (address 0x50). --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Question)]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the pinout of the Raspberry Pi **40-pin GPIO header** highlighting the UART (TX, RX) and I2C (SDA, SCL) pins with their BCM numbers and physical pin numbers. Explain how UART is enabled on `/dev/ttyAMA0`. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **UART and I2C** in a tabular format with at least 8 parameters. Justify why I2C is preferred over UART for connecting multiple sensors to a Raspberry Pi. --- ### **Model Answer — Question B (a)** #### Raspberry Pi GPIO Header (Relevant Pins) ``` 3.3V [ 1] [ 2] 5V (I2C) SDA GPIO2 [ 3] [ 4] 5V (I2C) SCL GPIO3 [ 5] [ 6] GND GPIO4 [ 7] [ 8] GPIO14 (UART TXD) GND [ 9] [10] GPIO15 (UART RXD) GPIO17 [11] [12] GPIO18 GPIO27 [13] [14] GND GPIO22 [15] [16] GPIO23 3.3V [17] [18] GPIO24 GPIO10 [19] [20] GND ... (continues to pin 40) ``` #### Enabling UART on `/dev/ttyAMA0` 1. Run `sudo raspi-config`. 2. Select **Interface Options → Serial Port**. 3. Disable *login shell over serial* (this frees `/dev/ttyAMA0` for user data). 4. Enable the *serial port hardware*. 5. Reboot: `sudo reboot`. 6. Verify: `ls -l /dev/ttyAMA0` should appear, and `dmesg | grep tty` shows the PL011 driver bound. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students confuse `/dev/ttyS0` (mini-UART, mapped to Bluetooth on Pi 3/4) with `/dev/ttyAMA0` (PL011, mapped to GPIO pins). For GPIO 14/15 serial, **always use `/dev/ttyAMA0`**. --- ### **Model Answer — Question B (b)** #### Comparison Table | Parameter | UART | I2C | |---|---|---| | Clock | No clock (asynchronous) | SCL clock (synchronous) | | Wires | TX, RX | SDA, SCL | | Duplex | Full-duplex | Half-duplex | | Devices | 2 (point-to-point) | Up to 127 | | Speed | Up to 1 Mbaud | 100 kHz / 400 kHz / 1 MHz | | Addressing | None | 7-bit / 10-bit | | Driver | Push-pull | Open-drain + pull-up | | Hot-plug | Poor | Excellent | | Power | Higher | Lower | | Overhead | Start + Stop bits per byte | Address + ACK per byte | #### Justification: Why I2C for Multiple Sensors? 1. **Scalability** — One pair of wires supports up to 127 devices, vs. UART's 1-to-1 limitation. 2. **Pin savings** — GPIO 2 and GPIO 3 serve the entire bus, freeing other pins for I/O. 3. **Standardised addressing** — Each sensor has a unique I2C address, allowing plug-and-play. 4. **Hot-pluggability** — Open-drain arbitration safely handles connection/disconnection of slaves. 5. **Industry adoption** — Almost every modern sensor (IMU, RTC, OLED, EEPROM) ships with I2C as default. UART, in contrast, would require a separate UART peripheral (or software bit-banging) per sensor — wasteful and impractical for IoT nodes. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Do **not** write "UART has clock" — it does NOT. This single error costs 2 marks. > - Do **not** forget the **pull-up resistors** in I2C diagrams — losing 1.5 marks. > - Do **not** confuse **physical pin numbers with BCM GPIO numbers** — examiners deduct 1 mark. > - Always mention the **3.3 V logic level** of the Pi when discussing voltage compatibility. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **UART = asynchronous, 2 wires (TX/RX), full-duplex, point-to-point, no addressing.** Uses `/dev/ttyAMA0` on Pi. Frame = Start + 8 data + (parity) + Stop. - **I2C = synchronous, 2 wires (SDA/SCL), half-duplex, multi-drop, addressable.** Uses `/dev/i2c-1` on Pi. Frame = Start + Addr+R/W + ACK + Data + ACK + Stop. - **Voltage = 3.3 V** on every Raspberry Pi GPIO pin. Use a **logic level shifter** for 5 V devices. - **Pull-up resistors (4.7 kΩ typical)** are **mandatory** on SDA and SCL for I2C. - The **LSB of the first I2C byte is the R/W bit** — the slave address is shifted left by 1. - UART always transmits **LSB first** by hardware convention. - I2C supports **127 slaves** with 7-bit addressing; **1024** with 10-bit. - **Standard baud rates:** 9600, 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200. - **I2C speeds:** 100 kHz (Standard), 400 kHz (Fast), 1 MHz (Fast-Plus), 3.4 MHz (High-Speed). - Enable interfaces via `raspi-config` → *Interface Options*. - Common pitfall: `/dev/ttyS0` ≠ GPIO 14/15 on Raspberry Pi 3 and later — that is `/dev/ttyAMA0`. - **Bus capacitance limit:** $C_{bus} \le 400$ pF constrains how long and how many devices the I2C bus can have. - For KTU exams, always include a **timing diagram** for UART (Start + 8N1) and an **SDA/SCL waveform** for I2C Start/Stop conditions.

topics.title: Raspberry Pi Interfacing and Programming Examples Using Python (LED, Switch, Sensor, Serial, I2C Devices)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT755 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Raspberry Pi Interfacing and Programming Examples Using Python (LED, Switch, Sensor, Serial, I2C Devices)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Raspberry Pi Interfacing** is the process of connecting external electronic components—such as LEDs, push-button switches, analog/digital sensors, and serial/I2C peripherals—to the **General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)** header of the Raspberry Pi single-board computer, and controlling/monitoring them through **Python scripts** that leverage libraries such as `RPi.GPIO`, `smbus2` (for I2C), and `pyserial` (for UART).

The **GPIO header** is a physical pin array on the Raspberry Pi board (typically a **40-pin** 2.54 mm header) that exposes the BCM2835/BCM2836/BCM2837/BCM2711 SoC pins for direct digital interaction with the outside world. Each pin can be software-configured as either an **Input** (to read a switch or sensor state) or an **Output** (to drive an LED, relay, or actuator).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Raspberry Pi as a Universal Remote Control"**
>
> Think of the Raspberry Pi as a **brain inside a small box**. The GPIO pins are its **hands**—they can either **push** (output HIGH = 3.3 V, output LOW = 0 V) or **feel** (read whether a voltage is present or absent). When you connect an LED to a GPIO pin, it's like plugging a lamp into a smart switch that the Pi can turn on/off thousands of times per second. When you connect a switch, it's like giving the Pi a **finger** to press and ask "Did someone just press me?" A temperature sensor is like a **thermometer** that whispers numbers to the Pi over a special two-wire "telephone line" (I2C). In short: **GPIO pins = Pi's way of talking to the physical world**.

### 1.3 The GPIO Pin Numbering Schemes (CRITICAL)

Raspberry Pi supports **two numbering schemes**, and confusing them is the #1 beginner mistake:

| Scheme | What it Numbers | Example | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BOARD** | Physical pin position on the 40-pin header | Pin 11, Pin 16 | Physical wiring diagrams |
| **BCM** | Broadcom SoC channel number | GPIO17, GPIO27 | Most Python libraries |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always declare the mode (`GPIO.BOARD` or `GPIO.BCM`) **at the very start** of every Python program, or the script will throw a runtime warning. KTU examiners often test whether students know both schemes.

### 1.4 Standard Operating Parameters

The Raspberry Pi GPIO pins operate at **3.3 V DC logic** (NOT 5 V tolerant on most pins). Critical safety and operational limits:

- **Logic HIGH Voltage:** **3.3 V**
- **Logic LOW Voltage:** **0 V (GND)**
- **Maximum source/sink current per pin:** **16 mA** (recommended **≤ 8 mA**)
- **Total current across all GPIOs:** **≤ 50 mA**
- **Default internal pull-up/pull-down resistance:** **~50 kΩ** (configurable via software)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Supplying **5 V** to a GPIO input pin will permanently damage the Broadcom SoC. Always use a **level shifter** when interfacing with 5 V logic devices (e.g., Arduino Uno).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Interfacing Model — Three Logical Steps

Every Raspberry Pi interfacing task, regardless of the peripheral, follows this universal flow:

1. **Setup Phase:** Import the library → set pin numbering mode → configure pin direction (IN/OUT) → enable pull-up/pull-down if needed.
2. **Action/Read Phase:** If OUTPUT, write `GPIO.HIGH` (1) or `GPIO.LOW` (0). If INPUT, read the pin state.
3. **Cleanup Phase:** Call `GPIO.cleanup()` to reset all pins to a safe default state (prevents "pin busy" errors on re-run).

### 2.2 LED Interfacing — Operational Logic

An LED is a **polarity-sensitive** diode that emits light when forward-biased. To safely drive an LED from a GPIO pin, we **MUST** add a **current-limiting resistor** in series.

**Why is the resistor needed?**
- An LED has a near-constant **forward voltage drop (V_f)** once conducting (~2 V for red, ~3.2 V for white/blue).
- Without a resistor, the GPIO pin tries to source as much current as it can → LED burns out or Pi pin is damaged.

### 2.3 Switch / Push-Button Interfacing — Debouncing Theory

A mechanical switch does **not** transition cleanly between OPEN and CLOSED. Its contacts physically **bounce** for ~5–50 ms, producing multiple spurious transitions. Software must "debounce" this signal.

**Two debounce strategies:**
- **Software debounce:** Wait for the state to remain stable for a fixed time (e.g., 200 ms) before accepting it.
- **Hardware debounce:** RC low-pass filter (e.g., 10 kΩ + 100 nF → τ = 1 ms).

**Pull-up vs Pull-down resistors:**
- A switch connected between **GPIO and GND** needs a **pull-up resistor** (default state = HIGH, pressed = LOW → "active low").
- A switch connected between **GPIO and 3.3 V** needs a **pull-down resistor** (default state = LOW, pressed = HIGH → "active high").

### 2.4 Sensor Interfacing

Sensors fall into two categories:
- **Digital sensors** (DHT11, DS18B20): Output a digital bitstream or use protocols like 1-Wire. Connect directly to a GPIO.
- **Analog sensors** (LM35, TMP36): Output a variable voltage. The Pi lacks a built-in ADC, so we use external chips like **MCP3008** via the **SPI bus**, or build a **voltage divider** to scale the output into the 0–3.3 V range.

### 2.5 Serial (UART) Communication

The Pi exposes **UART0** on GPIO 14 (TXD) and GPIO 15 (RXD). UART is **asynchronous, full-duplex**, point-to-point.

**Key parameters (must match on both sides):**
- **Baud rate** (bits per second): 9600, 115200, etc.
- **Data bits:** typically 8
- **Parity:** None, Even, Odd
- **Stop bits:** 1 or 2
- **Flow control:** None, RTS/CTS, XON/XOFF

### 2.6 I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit) Communication

I2C is a **synchronous, half-duplex, multi-master, multi-slave** serial bus using just **two wires**:
- **SDA (Serial Data):** GPIO 2 (BCM)
- **SCL (Serial Clock):** GPIO 3 (BCM)

**Addressing:** Every I2C slave has a **7-bit address** (0x00–0x7F), so up to **127 devices** can share the bus. Two key addresses are reserved: **0x00** (general call) and **0x7F** (sometimes used by Raspberry Pi's own HDMI driver — avoid).

**Pull-up resistors on SDA and SCL:** Mandatory (typically **4.7 kΩ** to 3.3 V) because I2C devices use **open-drain** outputs.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Constant | Description | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_{LED} = \dfrac{V_{supply} - V_f}{I_f}$ | Current-limiting resistor for an LED | **220 Ω – 1 kΩ** |
| 2 | $V_f$ | LED forward voltage drop | **1.8 V – 3.3 V** |
| 3 | $I_{GPIO,max}$ | Max safe current per GPIO pin | **16 mA** (8 mA recommended) |
| 4 | $V_{logic,HIGH}$ | Raspberry Pi logic high voltage | **3.3 V** |
| 5 | $V_{logic,LOW}$ | Raspberry Pi logic low voltage | **0 V** |
| 6 | $R_{pullup,I2C}$ | I2C bus pull-up resistor | **4.7 kΩ** (standard) |
| 7 | $R_{pullup,switch}$ | Switch pull-up resistor (external) | **10 kΩ** |
| 8 | $t_{bounce}$ | Mechanical switch bounce time | **5 ms – 50 ms** |
| 9 | $t_{debounce}$ | Software debounce delay | **200 ms – 300 ms** |
| 10 | $N_{devices,I2C}$ | Max I2C devices on bus | **127** (7-bit addr) |
| 11 | $f_{I2C}$ | I2C clock frequency | **100 kHz** (standard), **400 kHz** (fast) |
| 12 | $f_{baud}$ | UART baud rate (must match both ends) | **9600**, **115200** |
| 13 | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{f_{baud}}$ | Time per UART bit | **104.17 µs** (at 9600) |
| 14 | $V_{ADC} = \dfrac{ADC_{code}}{2^n - 1} \times V_{ref}$ | ADC digital-to-voltage conversion | depends on n (e.g., 10-bit) |
| 15 | $N_{pins}$ | Raspberry Pi GPIO header pin count | **40 pins** (26 on older) |
| 16 | $V_{IH,min}$ | Minimum voltage recognized as HIGH | **2.0 V** |
| 17 | $V_{IL,max}$ | Maximum voltage recognized as LOW | **0.8 V** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** The LED current-limiting resistor formula is the **single most-asked formula** in KTU 2024 ESE papers for IoT modules. I2C pull-up values (4.7 kΩ) and UART baud rate matching are also high-frequency questions.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LED interfacing** → Traffic light controllers, status indicators in IoT dashboards, smart lighting.
- **Switch interfacing** → Industrial control panels, elevator buttons, home automation keypads.
- **Sensor interfacing** → Weather stations, agricultural monitoring, wearable health devices.
- **UART** → Debug console, GPS modules (NEO-6M), GSM modems (SIM800L), Bluetooth HC-05.
- **I2C** → LCD displays (PCF8574, HD44780 backpacks), RTC chips (DS1307, DS3231), accelerometers (MPU6050), atmospheric sensors (BMP280), ToF distance sensors (VL53L0X).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: LED Current-Limiting Resistor

**Given:**
- Raspberry Pi GPIO HIGH voltage: $V_{supply} = 3.3\ \text{V}$
- Red LED forward voltage: $V_f = 2.0\ \text{V}$
- Desired LED current: $I_f = 10\ \text{mA}$ (well within the 16 mA GPIO limit)

**Find:** The required series resistance $R_{LED}$.

**Step 1:** Apply **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the loop:
$$V_{supply} - V_{R_{LED}} - V_f = 0$$

**Step 2:** Express the voltage drop across the resistor using **Ohm's Law**:
$$V_{R_{LED}} = I_f \times R_{LED}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the KVL equation:
$$3.3 - (I_f \times R_{LED}) - 2.0 = 0$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $R_{LED}$:
$$I_f \times R_{LED} = 3.3 - 2.0 = 1.3\ \text{V}$$

$$R_{LED} = \dfrac{1.3\ \text{V}}{0.010\ \text{A}} = 130\ \Omega$$

**Step 5:** Round up to the **nearest standard E12 resistor value** for safety:
$$R_{LED} = 220\ \Omega \quad (\text{most common practical choice})$$

**Verification:** $I_f = \dfrac{3.3 - 2.0}{220} = 5.9\ \text{mA} \le 16\ \text{mA}$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalized formula derivation:** Starting from $V_{supply} - I_f R_{LED} - V_f = 0$, we algebraically isolate $R_{LED}$ to obtain the master equation:
$$R_{LED} = \dfrac{V_{supply} - V_f}{I_f}$$
This is the form KTU examiners expect in the answer sheet.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: ADC Reading to Physical Temperature (LM35 via MCP3008)

**Scenario:** An LM35 temperature sensor outputs **10 mV per °C** and is connected to an MCP3008 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3\ \text{V}$. The ADC returns a digital code of **312**.

**Step 1:** Write the ADC resolution equation. For an n-bit ADC:
$$V_{ADC} = \dfrac{ADC_{code}}{2^n - 1} \times V_{ref}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute known values ($n = 10$, so $2^{10} - 1 = 1023$):
$$V_{ADC} = \dfrac{312}{1023} \times 3.3\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the fraction:
$$\dfrac{312}{1023} = 0.30499...$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $V_{ref}$:
$$V_{ADC} = 0.30499 \times 3.3 = 1.0065\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5:** Convert voltage to temperature using LM35 sensitivity (10 mV/°C):
$$T = \dfrac{V_{ADC}}{10\ \text{mV/°C}} = \dfrac{1.0065\ \text{V}}{0.010\ \text{V/°C}} = 100.65\ \text{°C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic recap:** The chain is **Sensor voltage → ADC (analog-to-digital) → Digital code → Python math → Physical quantity**. This is the canonical IoT pipeline, and KTU 2024 questions often test all four stages.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: I2C Pull-up Resistor Sizing

**Why is a pull-up needed?**
I2C devices have **open-drain outputs** — they can only pull the line LOW or leave it floating. They cannot drive HIGH. Hence, an **external resistor** pulls the line HIGH when no device is active.

**Standard value selection logic:**

The pull-up resistor $R_p$ must satisfy two constraints:

1. **Sink current limit (max value):** The device must be able to pull the line LOW against the resistor.
$$R_p \le \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{OL,max}}{I_{OL,min}}$$

For a typical I2C device: $V_{OL,max} = 0.4\ \text{V}$, $I_{OL,min} = 3\ \text{mA}$, $V_{DD} = 3.3\ \text{V}$:
$$R_p \le \dfrac{3.3 - 0.4}{0.003} = \dfrac{2.9}{0.003} = 966.67\ \Omega$$

2. **Rise time limit (min value):** The line must rise fast enough to meet the bus timing.
$$R_p \ge \dfrac{t_r}{0.8473 \times C_{bus}}$$

For a typical $t_r = 1000\ \text{ns}$ (Standard mode 100 kHz) and $C_{bus} = 100\ \text{pF}$:
$$R_p \ge \dfrac{1000 \times 10^{-9}}{0.8473 \times 100 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 11.8\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Conclusion:** $966.67\ \Omega \le R_p \le 11.8\ \text{k}\Omega$ → **$R_p = 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega$** is the standard, safe choice.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Interfacing Patterns

| Peripheral | Protocol | Pi Pins (BCM) | Python Library | Key Function | Common Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LED** | GPIO Output | Any (e.g., GPIO 17) | `RPi.GPIO` | `GPIO.output(pin, HIGH)` | Forgetting current-limiting resistor |
| **Push Button** | GPIO Input + Pull-up | Any (e.g., GPIO 27) | `RPi.GPIO` | `GPIO.input(pin)` | Not debouncing → multiple counts |
| **DHT11** | 1-Wire (custom) | Any (e.g., GPIO 4) | `Adafruit_DHT` | `read_retry(sensor, pin)` | Needs 10 kΩ pull-up, 2-sec delay between reads |
| **MCP3008 ADC** | SPI | CE0=GPIO8, MOSI=GPIO10, MISO=GPIO9, SCLK=GPIO11 | `spidev` | `spi.xfer2([byte1,byte2])` | Wrong SPI mode (MCP3008 needs mode 0) |
| **HC-05 Bluetooth** | UART | TXD=GPIO14, RXD=GPIO15 | `pyserial` | `ser.read()`, `ser.write()` | Baud rate mismatch (default 9600) |
| **BMP280 Sensor** | I2C | SDA=GPIO2, SCL=GPIO3 | `smbus2` / `board` | `bus.read_i2c_block_data()` | Forgetting 4.7 kΩ pull-ups on SDA/SCL |
| **16x2 LCD (I2C)** | I2C | SDA=GPIO2, SCL=GPIO3 | `RPLCD` | `lcd.write_string()` | Address conflict (PCF8574 = 0x27 or 0x3F) |
| **DS18B20** | 1-Wire | Any (e.g., GPIO 4) | `w1-gpio` kernel module | Reading `/sys/bus/w1/.../w1_slave` | Only 1 device per pin (without parasite power tricks) |

---

### 3.5 Complete Python Programs (Model Examples for KTU Lab/Exam)

> [!TIP]
> **Program 1 — LED Blinking (The "Hello World" of IoT)**

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

LED_PIN = 17            # BCM pin number

GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)            # Use BCM numbering
GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)     # Configure as output

try:
    for i in range(10):           # Blink 10 times
        GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH)   # LED ON
        time.sleep(0.5)                    # Wait 500 ms
        GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW)    # LED OFF
        time.sleep(0.5)
finally:
    GPIO.cleanup()                # Reset pins to safe state
```

> [!TIP]
> **Program 2 — Push-Button Counter with Debouncing**

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

BTN_PIN  = 27
LED_PIN  = 17
DEBOUNCE = 0.2          # 200 ms debounce window

GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setup(BTN_PIN, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP)  # Internal pull-up
GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)

count = 0
last_state = GPIO.HIGH

try:
    while True:
        reading = GPIO.input(BTN_PIN)
        if reading == GPIO.LOW and last_state == GPIO.HIGH:
            count += 1
            print(f"Button pressed {count} times")
            GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH)
            time.sleep(DEBOUNCE)                  # Wait out the bounce
        else:
            GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW)
        last_state = reading
        time.sleep(0.01)                          # 10 ms polling
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    GPIO.cleanup()
```

> [!TIP]
> **Program 3 — DHT11 Temperature & Humidity Reading**

```python
import Adafruit_DHT
import time

DHT_SENSOR = Adafruit_DHT.DHT11
DHT_PIN    = 4

while True:
    humidity, temperature = Adafruit_DHT.read_retry(DHT_SENSOR, DHT_PIN)
    if humidity is not None and temperature is not None:
        print(f"Temp = {temperature:0.1f}°C  Humidity = {humidity:0.1f}%")
    else:
        print("Sensor read failure. Check wiring.")
    time.sleep(2)              # DHT11 needs >= 1 sec between reads
```

> [!TIP]
> **Program 4 — I2C Read from BMP280 (Pressure/Temperature)**

```python
import smbus2
import time

bus = smbus2.SMBus(1)                  # I2C bus 1 (modern Pi)
BMP280_ADDR = 0x76                      # Default SDO=GND address

# Calibration read (simplified; full code has 26 calib registers)
bus.write_byte_data(BMP280_ADDR, 0xF4, 0x57)   # Normal mode, oversampling x4
time.sleep(0.1)

data = bus.read_i2c_block_data(BMP280_ADDR, 0xF7, 8)   # Read 8 bytes
raw_temp = (data[3] << 12) | (data[4] << 4) | (data[5] >> 4)
print(f"Raw temperature ADC code: {raw_temp}")
```

> [!TIP]
> **Program 5 — Serial (UART) Communication with HC-05 Bluetooth**

```python
import serial
import time

ser = serial.Serial(
    port    = '/dev/ttyAMA0',     # Pi's UART0
    baudrate= 9600,
    parity  = serial.PARITY_NONE,
    stopbits= serial.STOPBITS_ONE,
    bytesize= serial.EIGHTBITS,
    timeout = 1
)

ser.write(b"AT\r\n")              # Send AT command
time.sleep(0.5)
response = ser.readline()
print(f"Bluetooth response: {response}")
ser.close()
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Raspberry Pi GPIO Header — Pin Map (Top 20 Pins)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph GPIO["RASPBERRY PI 40-PIN GPIO HEADER"]
        direction TB
        A["PIN 1  -- 3.3V POWER"]
        B["PIN 2  -- 5V POWER"]
        C["PIN 3  -- GPIO2 SDA I2C"]
        D["PIN 4  -- 5V POWER"]
        E["PIN 5  -- GPIO3 SCL I2C"]
        F["PIN 6  -- GROUND"]
        G["PIN 7  -- GPIO4 1-WIRE"]
        H["PIN 8  -- GPIO14 TXD UART"]
        I["PIN 9  -- GROUND"]
        J["PIN 10 -- GPIO15 RXD UART"]
        K["PIN 11 -- GPIO17 GENERAL"]
        L["PIN 12 -- GPIO18 PWM"]
        M["PIN 13 -- GPIO27 GENERAL"]
        N["PIN 14 -- GROUND"]
        O["PIN 15 -- GPIO22 GENERAL"]
        P["PIN 16 -- GPIO23 GENERAL"]
        Q["PIN 17 -- 3.3V POWER"]
        R["PIN 18 -- GPIO24 GENERAL"]
        S["PIN 19 -- GPIO10 SPI MOSI"]
        T["PIN 20 -- GROUND"]
    end
```

### 4.2 LED Interfacing — Circuit Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    P["RASPBERRY PI GPIO17 OUTPUT 3.3V"] -->|Current flows out| R["CURRENT LIMITING RESISTOR 220 OHM"]
    R -->|Voltage drop 1.3V| L["LED ANODE POSITIVE"]
    L --> K["LED CATHODE NEGATIVE"]
    K --> G["GROUND PIN PIN 6"]
    
    style P fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style R fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style L fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style K fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style G fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

### 4.3 Switch (Active-LOW) Interfacing with Internal Pull-up

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CircuitBlock["PUSH BUTTON INTERFACE WITH PULLUP"]
        V["3.3V"] -.->|Internal 50k pullup enabled in code| P1["GPIO27 PIN 13"]
        P1 --> SW["PUSH BUTTON NORMALLY OPEN"]
        SW --> GND["GROUND PIN 14"]
        
        P1 -.->|Reads HIGH when open| RD["RPi.GPIO INPUT READS 1"]
        SW -.->|Press closes circuit| RP["RPi.GPIO INPUT READS 0"]
    end
    
    style V fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style SW fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style GND fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#424242
```

### 4.4 I2C Bus Communication — Master/Slave Block Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    M["RASPBERRY PI I2C MASTER BCM2837"] -->|SDA GPIO2| SDA["SDA LINE WITH 4.7K PULLUP TO 3.3V"]
    M -->|SCL GPIO3| SCL["SCL LINE WITH 4.7K PULLUP TO 3.3V"]
    
    SDA --> D1["SLAVE 1 BMP280 SENSOR ADDR 0x76"]
    SDA --> D2["SLAVE 2 LCD PCF8574 ADDR 0x27"]
    SDA --> D3["SLAVE 3 MPU6050 IMU ADDR 0x68"]
    
    SCL --> D1
    SCL --> D2
    SCL --> D3
    
    style M fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1

    style SCL fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style D1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style D2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style D3 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
```

### 4.5 UART Serial Communication — Pi ↔ External Device

```mermaid

    W1 --> RXD["EXTERNAL DEVICE RXD"]

    W2 --> GPIO15["RASPBERRY PI GPIO15 RXD receives"]

    style PI fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c

```

### 4.6 Complete IoT Interfacing — Functional Block Architecture

